An Introduction to Biopharmaceutical Facility Design & Layout Integrated Project Services (IPS) — A Berkshire Hathaway Company Source: IPS Insights | Published: June 23, 2016 URL: https://www.ipsdb.com/happenings/insights/an-introduction-to-biopharmaceutical-facility-design-and-layout Compiled for GMP RAG Reference Use Overview Expansions and renovations to existing biological facilities, and construction of new facilities, provide a unique opportunity to rethink basic design strategies and use new technologies to build a better facility that will improve compliance. The implementation of single-use systems (SUS) is enabling an evolutionary step in the nature of bioprocessing, allowing for a smaller plant footprint and elimination of several common operations. This article explores modern facility design principles that make use of the most flexible new technologies and provide a platform to rein in costs, reduce schedules, and deliver products compliantly and within business risk tolerance. 1. Where to Start: The Core Layout Problem The key to layout of a biopharmaceutical facility is solving the mixed-integer programming problem of multiple goals, constraints, and adjacencies — and all for the lowest cost (typically, the lowest square footage). The goal is to evaluate the process requirements and risks involved with the product and process and build a facility that will facilitate the operation. The driving force behind any facility is the product it will produce. The facility must be built to promote: Ease of production by the operators Workflow path optimization Safety enablement Minimization of bioburden Compliance enforcement The production process includes all aspects of the operation: personnel flow, product flow, raw material flow, waste/trash/spill removal, cleaning, and equipment flow. 1.1 Product-Driven Design Considerations Although a narrow range of equipment types will be used to produce the product, implications differ by product class: Simple protein / mAb: Minimize bioburden by minimizing handling and transfer Viral inoculated / plasma derived: Segregation of viral and non-viral processes and viral clearance Cytotoxic / toxins / potent compounds: Containment and isolation of toxic process segments to protect operators Liposomes: Use of and containment of solvents into an OSHA Class I, Division 2 environment 1.2 Occupational Exposure Bands (OEBs) When dealing with conjugated mAbs, occupational exposure limits (potency) is an important factor. ISPE provides general guidelines on handling potent compounds by occupational exposure bands (OEBs), ranging from Band A (harmless) to Band F (immediate, life threatening). Handling of these materials varies from normal GMP gowning at Band A, through dust masks and respirators, to full isolation/remote manipulation at Band F. Each process design requires appropriate level(s) of containment, typically including a room as secondary containment. 2. Open vs. Closed Processing Before implementing SUS, it is important to completely understand what steps will utilize a 'closed' process versus an 'open' process. While the bioreactor itself might use an SUS and be considered a closed process, all other processing steps must also be assessed, including connections, cleaning, and transport. In any process design exercise, it is useful to create a flow chart of the process and identify which steps have open processing (where product or components are exposed to the room or human handling). These can include: Media preparation (must always have its own contained space)

cleaning, and transport. In any process design exercise, it is useful to create a flow chart of the process and identify which steps have open processing (where product or components are exposed to the room or human handling). These can include: Media preparation (must always have its own contained space) Buffer prep, dispensing, make-up, disposal, and cleaning Cleaning vessels, skids, parts, mixers, and handling equipment Waste handling, including discarded SUS bags Bulk blending, mixing, and filling For each step, the decision must be made: can we contain the operation (make it 'closed') or must we dedicate a room/suite area to that operation? Once designated 'open,' dedicated floor space is required and a cleaning routine must remove cross-contamination potential. If 'closed' using SUS, we can reduce the number and size of these spaces and allow a higher turnover of use without cleaning. 3. Risk Assessment Framework Risk assessment focuses on the potential for product cross-contamination, product loss, and financial loss. The assessment typically breaks down into the following classes: 3.1 Single-Product Facility Risk profile is low. Main concerns are worker safety, bioburden, and post viral exposure/viral clearance. The design can consolidate any number of steps into a single room/suite as practical. 3.2 Multiple-Product Facility Concerns increase with potential cross-contamination via microbial or viral means and by inadvertent mistakes. Multiple products at lower risk during R&D and clinical Phase I. For Phase II through commercial manufacturing, individual products must have a closed or secure manufacturing sequence and path. Careful campaigning and sanitization/decontamination can help reduce risks. 3.3 Contract Manufacturing Organization (CMO) Multiple clients, products, and processes must coexist in the same facility. Most clients want to know what products have been manufactured previously. This almost always drives CMOs to a compartmentalized suite approach for Phase II through commercial. R&D, preclinical, and Phase I programs can often run in a large generic service area such as a 'ballroom' with appropriate controls. 3.4 Batch Value Risk As titers and batch sizes increase, and yearly productions are consolidated into fewer batches, the financial implications of a batch failure from bioburden or cross-contamination become extreme. As financial exposure increases, so too does the need to increase isolation levels, reduce batch size, increase production time, and protect the process steps. This will also increase facility size. 4. Regulatory Compliance Requirements A series of regulatory laws must be adhered to in order to design, build, and construct any biopharm facility. Below are key regulations that constrain facility design: 4.1 Basic GMP Requirements 21 CFR 211:42-58 — Facilities and controls 21 CFR 600.3(t) — Biological product definitions 21 CFR 601.22 — Biologics manufacturing requirements 21 CFR 600.12e — Records requirements 4.2 Contract Manufacturing 21 CFR 210, 211, 600–680, 820 4.3 International Standards EMA Annex II — Biological medicinal products 40 CFR Part 261 & 264 — Waste handling and flow 21 CFR Part 600.11, Subchapter F — Safety in processing 4.4 Biosafety and Containment NIH/CDC guidelines for containment — BSL 1 through BSL 4 OSHA, NIOSH, NFPA — Safety and containment standards National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) — Plant cell cultures

Part 261 & 264 — Waste handling and flow 21 CFR Part 600.11, Subchapter F — Safety in processing 4.4 Biosafety and Containment NIH/CDC guidelines for containment — BSL 1 through BSL 4 OSHA, NIOSH, NFPA — Safety and containment standards National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) — Plant cell cultures Federal, state, and local building codes: NEC, ADA, NFPA 5. Key Design Takeaways for GMP Layout The following summarizes the practical principles for biopharmaceutical facility layout: Start with the product: Every design decision must start from what the product requires, not from the building Map all flows first: Personnel flow, product flow, material flow, waste flow, and equipment flow must all be mapped before any room assignment Classify open vs. closed: Every unit operation must be categorized; closed operations reduce room requirements dramatically Risk drives segregation level: Single-product, multi-product, and CMO scenarios all require different degrees of physical separation Regulatory compliance is the floor, not the ceiling: Meet all applicable regulations but design above minimum requirements for flexibility Source: IPS — Integrated Project Services https://www.ipsdb.com/happenings/insights/an-introduction-to-biopharmaceutical-facility-design-and-layout

GMP Gowning Protocols: Designing Transition Zones GMP vs. Non-GMP Zones: Managing Personnel Flow Author: Trevor Henderson | Lab Design News Published: March 16, 2026 URL: https://www.labdesignnews.com/content/gmp-vs-non-gmp-zones-managing-personnel-flow Compiled for GMP RAG Reference Use Introduction: You Can't Wear Jeans in the Pilot Plant Designing the transition zones for GMP compliance requires a massive behavioral and architectural shift. While an R&D scientist might stroll into the laboratory wearing street clothes and a casually draped lab coat, producing clinical-grade therapeutics demands absolute, unrelenting sterility. When an organization upgrades its infrastructure to support a GMP pilot plant or full-scale manufacturing, the most challenging aspect is often managing the human element. In these highly regulated environments, the facility's architecture must proactively dictate human behavior. Facility planners achieve this by engineering rigorous transition zones — specialized airlocks that act as the physical gateways between the standard commercial building and the pristine manufacturing core, ensuring that street-level contaminants never breach the critical sterile boundary. Key Takeaways Personnel Flow: Engineering strict, unidirectional pathways that prevent cross-contamination by dictating exactly how and where humans move through the facility Gowning Room Design: Utilizing physical barriers such as the step-over bench (SOB) to mentally and physically separate the 'dirty' and 'clean' zones of an airlock Cleanroom Classification: Stepping down airborne particulate counts through cascaded airlocks to achieve necessary ISO or Grade ratings for aseptic processing Pressure Cascades: Designing HVAC systems to maintain precise positive pressure gradients that constantly push contaminants away from the critical manufacturing core 1. How Personnel Flow Dictates GMP Facility Design In any cleanroom environment, the human body is the single largest source of contamination. Research confirms that a fully gowned, motionless person generates approximately 100,000 particles per minute, escalating to 1,000,000 or more particles per minute when walking. To protect the product, architects must design the facility to act as a physical filter for the people working inside it. Unlike standard laboratories with open corridors and two-way swinging doors, GMP facility design relies on strict unidirectional personnel flow. This means creating a deliberate, one-way path: Employees enter the clean core through a dedicated ingress gowning suite Personnel perform their shifts in the classified manufacturing area Personnel exit through a completely separate de-gowning suite This unidirectional layout prevents cross-contamination. It ensures that personnel exiting the suite — who may carry trace amounts of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) or biological vectors — never cross paths with clean personnel entering in fresh sterile garments. 2. The Structural Anatomy of Gowning Room Design A gowning room is not merely a locker room; it is a highly engineered architectural airlock. Effective gowning room design breaks the transition down into distinct, sequential zones. 2.1 Sequential Gowning Zones Pre-gowning area: Personnel remove street coats, jewelry, and personal items, swapping outdoor footwear for dedicated captive plant shoes Core transition — Step-over bench (SOB): The SOB creates a hard, undeniable boundary between the unclassified 'dirty' side and the classified 'clean' side. Personnel sit on the bench, don sterile booties over their plant shoes, and swing their legs over to the clean side without their feet ever touching the dirty

transition — Step-over bench (SOB): The SOB creates a hard, undeniable boundary between the unclassified 'dirty' side and the classified 'clean' side. Personnel sit on the bench, don sterile booties over their plant shoes, and swing their legs over to the clean side without their feet ever touching the dirty floor again Interlocked door zone: Building access control mechanically prevents the cleanroom door from opening if the corridor door is open — guaranteeing the airlock is never 'open to the street' 2.2 Surface and Material Requirements The flooring in transition zones must be monolithic and highly durable to withstand heavy foot traffic and aggressive cleaning agents. Required features include: Welded sheet vinyl or poured epoxy floors Integral cove bases where the floor curves seamlessly up the wall Elimination of 90-degree corners where dirt and bacteria can accumulate 3. Managing Cleanroom Classification Through Architectural Airlocks Every step a technician takes deeper into a GMP facility should correspond with a stricter cleanroom classification. Moving from an uncontrolled office corridor to a classified manufacturing core cannot happen through a single door. Airlocks serve as pneumatic transition chambers, allowing the HVAC system to step down particulate counts gradually. 3.1 Pressure Cascade Design The most critical, cleanest room is supplied with the highest volume of HEPA-filtered air, creating a positive pressure 'bubble.' When an interlocked gowning room door opens, this positive pressure forces clean air outward into the airlock, actively sweeping away contaminants — ensuring airflow always moves from the cleanest zone outward toward dirtier zones. 3.2 Typical Classification Cascade The cascaded airlock approach more typically transitions personnel as follows: Uncontrolled corridor → ISO 8 (Grade D) anteroom via airlock ISO 8 anteroom → ISO 7 (Grade C) processing room via gowning airlock Grade A (ISO 5) protection applied locally at the critical product exposure point (e.g., LAF hood or isolator) within the ISO 7 background 4. Comparing Environments: R&D Lab vs. GMP Manufacturing 5. Expert FAQ: GMP Gowning Protocols Q: Can a personnel gowning room also serve as a material airlock? A: No. Regulatory agencies strongly discourage mixing personnel flow with material flow. Humans shed particulates, while pallets, carts, and raw materials shed cardboard dust and industrial debris. GMP facilities must design separate Material Airlocks (MALs) and Personnel Airlocks (PALs) to prevent cross-contamination. Q: How do you handle emergency exits in a unidirectional GMP facility? A: Life safety always supersedes GMP compliance. Emergency exit doors are equipped with crash bars and local break-glass alarms that override the cleanroom interlocks. This allows for immediate evacuation during a fire or chemical spill, even though opening the door temporarily compromises the cleanroom's pressure cascade and classification. Q: What flooring is best for a gowning transition zone? A: The flooring must be monolithic and highly durable to withstand heavy foot traffic and aggressive cleaning agents. Welded sheet vinyl or poured epoxy floors with integral cove bases are mandatory to eliminate 90-degree corners where dirt and bacteria can accumulate. Q: How does GMP transition zone design connect to the broader pilot plant architecture? A: Gowning suites and airlocks are the human-facing

traffic and aggressive cleaning agents. Welded sheet vinyl or poured epoxy floors with integral cove bases are mandatory to eliminate 90-degree corners where dirt and bacteria can accumulate. Q: How does GMP transition zone design connect to the broader pilot plant architecture? A: Gowning suites and airlocks are the human-facing layer of a much larger compliance architecture. These zones integrate with the structural, utility, and regulatory demands of the scale-up environment as a whole, including HVAC design, pressure mapping, and contamination control strategy (CCS). 6. Page 2 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Preface Since 2013, the biomanufacturing industry has seen many changes in terms of technology, modalities, and a greater emphasis on patient safety. These changes have been reflected in many new approaches to risk assessment, process closure, contamination control, and process automation. The industry shift, while subtle in terms of new breakthrough technology in equipment, control, and digitalization, has significantly impacted how regulatory guidance is being defined and implemented. We are seeing a paradigm shift in how facilities are designed, how processes are closed to mitigate risk, and how the once held product-process-facility relationship is being revisited in order to reduce cost of goods while improving operational effectiveness. This third edition of the ISPE Baseline ® Guide: Biomanufacturing Manufacturing Facilities establishes a well-defined roadmap on how the process and facility attributes can be aligned in a manner that reduces risk to the patient while advancing the body of knowledge around product protection. This Guide provides insight from a team of global subject matter experts and what they see as key attributes of process and facility design that raise the bar across all aspects of drug substance manufacturing of biological therapeutics.

The authors of this Guide strongly recommend the utilization and implementation of closed systems for both stainless steel and single-use equipment as an effective method to mitigate the risks associated with the production of biopharmaceuticals. 1.2 Purpose and Scope This Guide is focused on aligning the existing, recognized body of knowledge that drives facility design and operation with advances in technology, regulatory guidance, and QRM practices. The objective is to provide a roadmap of sound practices and robust implementation tools. The tools and practices detailed in this Guide apply to biopharmaceutical drug substance manufacturing, for both clinical and commercial scale, including large molecule (biologics) Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) used in various biopharmaceutical products. Application of this Guide should help to ensure a compliant approach aligned with current GMP guidance defined under 21 CFR Part 211 Current Good Manufacturing Practice for Finished Pharmaceuticals [1], EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 2 [2], PIC/S Guide to Good Manufacturing Practice for Medicinal Products Annexes [3], as well as other globally recognized design and operational guidance. The globally recognized concepts around product protection, driven by aseptic and low bioburden practices and closed system designs, provide the foundation for a risk-based approach that examines the relationship between product, process, and facility. This Guide is intended to provide individuals and teams with the tools to make informed decisions about layouts, area classifications, segregation strategies, project delivery approaches, and operational approaches, all aimed at creating compliant, cost-effective biomanufacturing facility assets. The types of drug substance considered in the development of this Guide include: • Large molecule protein therapeutics • Synthetic proteins • Monoclonal antibodies • Enzymes • Plasmids For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. the ISPE Baseline® Guide: Volume 4 – Water and Steam Systems (Third Edition) [12] • Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (ATMPs) – see the ISPE Guide: Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products – Autologous Cell Therapy [13] • Pharma 4.01 1.3 Key Concepts and Terms Several key concepts and terms are defined in this section of the Guide. When these terms are used in the context of this Guide, these definitions should be used. Definitions for other terms used in this Guide are provided in the Glossary (see Appendix 4). This Guide discusses the concepts of closed processing and the potential impact of closing a biopharmaceutical drug substance manufacturing process on the facility design. Critical Process Parameters (CPPs) and Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs) should be understood before defining process closure and other control strategies. Critical process conditions should be defined, and the risk of not closing the process should be understood because of its impact on the facility design. 1.3.1 Key Concepts Process Closure The third edition of the Guide recognizes that processes are either open or they are closed. They are either exposed to the surrounding environment or they are not. This Guide also recognizes that after removal of contaminants, the ingress of contaminants into the system must be prevented prior to and during product contact for the system to be considered closed. Process closure is achieved through a cleaning, sanitization, and/or sterilization process that is appropriate or consistent with the process requirements, whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden. These systems need to remain closed during production within the system. As a result of the process closure, the environment does not represent a critical aspect of the closed process. A closed system must be validated to show that sufficient layers of protection have been implemented to mitigate the risk of contamination from the environment. Transfers into or from these systems must also be validated as closed. 1 The ISPE Baseline® Guide for Pharma 4.0 is a planned future ISPE publication. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Page 10 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The following methods can be used to remove latent bioburden or contaminants: • Steam in Place (SIP) • Chemical sanitization • Non-caustic Clean in Place (CIP) • Filtration The following methods can be used to prevent the ingress of contamination after removal of latent bioburden or contaminants: • Aseptic transfers into a closed system • Maintaining positive pressure (with sterile-filtered air/gas) on the system Refer to Chapter 5 for additional details regarding process closure. Process ZoneThe third edition of this Guide introduces the term process zone to refer to the processing environment that can influence or affect the process and product. What is included in the process zone is dependent upon whether the process is open or closed. For a closed process, the process zone is the process equipment itself (e.g., process boundary), and the surrounding

to refer to the processing environment that can influence or affect the process and product. What is included in the process zone is dependent upon whether the process is open or closed. For a closed process, the process zone is the process equipment itself (e.g., process boundary), and the surrounding environment has no impact on the product or the process. For an open process, the process zone includes the process equipment itself and the surrounding environment (e.g., clean room, Biological Safety Cabinet (BSC), Laminar Air Flow (LAF) hoods, etc.) that may have a direct impact on the product or the process. For additional details regarding the process zone, refer to Chapter 6. 1.3.2 Key Terms Closed ProcessA scheme in which all manufacturing systems used in a process are closed. See Closed System.Note: A closed process may be sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden, and must be validated.Closed SystemThe term closed system is defined in three different global regulations, as shown below . While the wording varies slightly, there is a consistent recognition across all these regulations that in a closed system, the product (in any form, such as final product, drug substance, active drug substance, etc.) is not exposed to the surrounding or immediate environment. “A system in which the product is not exposed to the surrounding environment. For example, this can be achieved by the use of bulk product holders (such as tanks or bags) that are connected to each other by pipes or tubes as a system, and where used for sterile products, the full system is sterilised after the connections are made. Examples of these can be (but are not limited to) large scale reusable systems, such as those seen in active substance manufacturing, or disposable bag and manifold systems, such as those seen in the manufacture of biological products. Closed systems are not opened until the conclusion of an operation. The use of the term ’closed systems’ in this Annex does not refer to systems such as RABS or isolator systems.” (EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7]) For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 11 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities “Where a drug substance or product is not exposed to the immediate room environment during manufacture.” (EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 2 [2]) “Where an active substance or product is not exposed to the immediate room environment during manufacture.” (PIC/S Annex 2A [3]) Additionally, there is an industry effort underway to create a harmonized definition for a closed system and to provide further clarity around closed systems. The proposed definition for a closed system is as follows: A system that isolates the process zone from its surrounding environment and prevents ingress of environmental contaminants during product contact. The process zone is limited by the equipment. System closure requires two steps: 1. Removal of latent contaminants within the process zone to a level commensurate with the

as follows: A system that isolates the process zone from its surrounding environment and prevents ingress of environmental contaminants during product contact. The process zone is limited by the equipment. System closure requires two steps: 1. Removal of latent contaminants within the process zone to a level commensurate with the process requirements, whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden, prior to process contact 2. Prevention of ingress of environmental contaminants during process contact Note: A closed system is not necessarily a sterile system. It is closed to the appropriate condition whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden. Contamination Control Strategy (CCS) “A planned set of controls for microorganisms, endotoxin/pyrogen and particles, derived from current product and process understanding that assures process performance and product quality. The controls can include parameters and attributes related to active substance, excipient and drug product materials and components, facility and equipment operating conditions, in-process controls, finished product specifications, and the associated methods and frequency of monitoring and control.” (EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7]) Open Process (System) A process that is exposed to the environment and therefore requires environmental conditions to mitigate the risk of contamination from the environment. In the case of an open process, the environment represents a critical aspect and can have a direct impact on product quality and patient safety . Open sterile and aseptic operations must be performed in an environment where the probability of contamination is acceptably low (i.e., an environment meeting the bioburden requirements for a Grade A space). Open bioburden-controlled processing may be performed in an ISO Grade 8/EU Grade C or EU Grade D environment as appropriate for the unit operation. Process ZoneThe environment that includes all elements (e.g., raw materials, chemicals, products, contaminants, personnel, waste) that may contact the process or product in an open or closed system. In a closed system, the process zone is limited to the equipment/component that isolates the process zone from the surrounding environment. In an open system, the process zone includes the surrounding environment. Quality Risk Management (QRM) “A systematic process for the assessment, control, communication and review of risks to the quality of the drug (medicinal) product across the product lifecycle.” (ICH Q9 [6]) Surrounding Environment (Manufacturing Environment) The space around the equipment/component housing a process. It includes the conditions, equipment, personnel, materials, and waste occupying that space. In open processing, the surrounding environment constitutes part of the process zone. In closed processing, the surrounding environment is outside the process zone. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Guide Chapters Chapter 2: Regulatory Chapter 2 provides an overview of the regulatory requirements for facility design and summarizes the sources that influence both regulators and industry best practices. Chapter 3: Risk ManagementChapter 3 provides an overview of the importance of robust QRM. The chapter also identifies several risk management tools and where they may be applied to a biopharmaceutical facility lifecycle.Chapter 4: Contamination Control StrategyWith the increased focus on CCS resulting from the publication of the revised EudraLex V olume 4 Annex 1 in 2022 [7], an entire chapter in this Guide has been dedicated to the importance of contamination control for biopharmaceutical facilities and ensuring facility design considers this crucial topic. Chapter 5: Process ClosureProcess closure is a key element in biopharmaceutical facility design. A bioprocess that is closed is isolated from the environment and therefore can impact area classifications, layouts, sustainability , and how a facility is operated/ maintained. This chapter builds on the concepts developed in the previous edition; however, it simplifies the original concepts and recognizes that processes are either open or closed. The chapter defines what an open process is and what a closed process is. Chapter 6: OperationsThe operations chapter enhances the concepts from Chapters 4 and 5 and provides examples of the impact of CCS and open or closed processes on operations. The chapter describes advances in the operational strategy of biopharmaceutical facilities and supports the relationship between the product attributes, process operations, and facility attributes that are essential in producing a compliant, robust facility design. Contemporary facilities operate under diverse requirements; often, they are repurposed assets that are multi-product in their definition, requiring short product campaigns and efficient, thorough changeover between products. Higher titers and products focused on smaller markets allow for reduced scale and increased use of SUT . Chapter 7: GMP Layout Approaches Chapter 7 provides guidelines and discussions for the design of integrated GMP facilities. This information is in the context of regulatory, process, production, and technological factors that significantly influence the layout and flow of a production facility. The chapter builds on the concepts developed in Chapters 4, 5 and 6 to present how the state of process closure (e.g., open or closed) can have a substantial impact on facility size as well as GMP layouts and approaches. Chapter 8: Architectural DesignChapter 8 presents an expanded architectural approach to biopharmaceutical design with a focus on site master planning, building layout, modular options, and why it is important to define the end user requirements early in the design phases. The chapter highlights the importance of a fully integrated facility where the core process areas and the support areas form a comprehensive solution that work together to provide a safe and reliable facility . ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 13 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Chapter 8 also presents design issues and best practices for establishing a well-defined project scope and the extensive planning activities required to define performance requirements early in the design process, where the impact on cost can be evaluated and alternatives proposed. The importance of a well-defined and structured approach to sequential architectural activities and deliverables is highlighted, demonstrating how such a structured approach can help to speed up the early design process and reduce the need for rework in later design phases. Chapter 9: Mechanical and Electrical SystemsAlthough serving process equipment and operations with utilities is a common requirement and not specific to this facility type, Chapter 9 provides an overview with a focus on the unique balancing of risks required for biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities. The chapter discusses how mechanical systems are focused on environmental control to manage the conflicting risks of general product contamination, cross-contamination, biosafety, and biosecurity. The impact of process closure (i.e., open versus closed) has a direct impact on the design of the mechanical systems and the level of energy required to support a given manufacturing process or product. The chapter also discusses how electrical systems can further support the mitigation of risk through support of mechanical systems in emergency situations, and how proper electrical means and methods can help support cleaning and containment. Chapter 10: SustainabilityChapter 10 aims to present a framework of sustainability beyond just reduction of carbon, energy conservation, or reduction of natural gas consumption. It strives to get the readers to think of sustainability as it pertains to the entire product lifecycle. From the beginning of initial research to decisions made during small scale development, for raw materials used during production, and for the packaging and distribution of drug products, sustainability goes beyond just the biopharmaceutical manufacturing facility. This chapter provides background and links to some of the latest trends in this area and how they affect biopharmaceutical thinking. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 15 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 2 Regulatory 2.1 Introduction Regulatory agencies around the globe share the same goal of ensuring through oversight, that safe, effective, and high-quality medicines and therapies are delivered to patients. It is in support of this central and critical mission that regulations and guidelines for manufacturing biopharmaceutical products are continually evolving to align with the latest manufacturing technologies, advances in therapeutic medicines, and robust risk management strategies.

oversight, that safe, effective, and high-quality medicines and therapies are delivered to patients. It is in support of this central and critical mission that regulations and guidelines for manufacturing biopharmaceutical products are continually evolving to align with the latest manufacturing technologies, advances in therapeutic medicines, and robust risk management strategies. This chapter is intended to highlight the main concepts behind the current regulatory framework and to provide references to key regulatory and guidance documents related to biopharmaceutical manufacturing. Regulatory agencies recognize that industry guidance can only be so prescriptive in defining expectations and requirements. For this reason, recent guidance documents put an increased focus on the principles of risk management and the adoption of Agile technologies. Formal risk assessments with associated risk mitigation practices are no longer considered optional, but rather, a mandatory basis for GMP compliance. A CCS has become an essential lifecycle document that is initiated during the design phase to ensure the appropriate design of the facility, equipment, systems, and associated processes to mitigate and control the risks of contamination and cross-contamination. The risk assessments and CCS help define what is appropriate and required to meet regulatory expectations, including but not limited to, open/closed processes, concurrent and consolidated processes, environmental requirements (outdoors/uncontrolled, Controlled Not Classified (CNC), classified), finishes, flows, single or multi-product processing, changeovers, cleaning, gowning, monitoring, etc. This chapter provides a summary of what is required for regulatory (GMP and biosafety) compliance and then refers to subsequent chapters of this Guide for the details of the respective topic. 2.2 Regulatory Agencies, Directives, and Guidance Worldwide regulations and guidance documents are continually evolving, therefore the original source websites (provided in Appendix 4) should be referenced for the most current revision and information as well as potential draft revisions. An important part of maintaining cGMP2 compliance is staying “current”. Regulations are updated or revised periodically, with updates often not occurring for more than ten years. Therefore, to remain current, companies should review recently published and relevant guidance documents, even if not specific to the product category or facility type in question. As an example, EudraLex V olume 4 Annex 1 [7] was released in August 2022. The CPPs that will impact the CQAs of the product must be fully understood and defined during process development, design of experiments, and process design. The CPPs and CQAs of both current and projected future products should be considered in the facility and process design. Variability in CPPs can impact CQAs and should be monitored and controlled to ensure that a process is robust and validated to produce the desired product quality. CPPs are the set of manufacturing and processing variables (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow , agitation, pH, etc.) that when controlled, yield a consistent and reproducible product with the defined CQAs. In addition, the control of CPPs is included through design aspects of equipment, systems, and often times the facility itself (Critical Design Elements (CDEs)). As an example, if a process step is open and the product is exposed to the room environment, some room environment parameters (temperature, Relative Humidity (RH), total and viable particulates, etc.) can be For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright © 2024 International Society for Pharmaceutical Engineering. All rights reserved. Page 18 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities CPPs (e.g., a potential source of contamination), which can affect product CQAs. If a process step is closed and the product is not exposed to the room environment or impacted by temperature and/or humidity , the room environment parameters can potentially become non-critical process (or operating) parameters rather than CPPs. This is a key concept and the basis for the product protection approach in facility design and should be defined in the risk assessments and CCS. The areas where the facility needs to directly provide protection to the product, as determined through the risk assessment, should be defined. Open process steps that cause room environment parameters to be CPPs should be identified in a User Requirement Specification (URS), on Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs), or other appropriate design documents. In

the facility needs to directly provide protection to the product, as determined through the risk assessment, should be defined. Open process steps that cause room environment parameters to be CPPs should be identified in a User Requirement Specification (URS), on Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs), or other appropriate design documents. In addition to product quality and patient safety, open process steps also need to be evaluated for operator exposure/safety and applicable codes (explosive or flammable materials) and addressed by Quality by Design (QbD). The facility design aspects for when the environment is or is not a CPP are defined in subsequent chapters of this Guide. 2.4 Risk Management and Risk Mitigation (Chapter 3) The principles and application of risk management and risk-based approaches are not new topics for biopharmaceutical manufacturers to ensure product quality and GMP compliance. The FDA published Pharmaceutical CGMPs for the 21st Century – A Risk-Based Approach [36] in 2004, followed by ICH Q9 Quality Risk Management [6] (originally published in 2005) and the overall risk management application as part of a robust Pharmaceutical Quality System (ICH Q10, 2008 [37]). Recently revised guidance documents reiterate the importance of these principles and set the expectation that a risk management program must include formal risk assessments for GMP compliance. Although EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7] and Part IV [34] are not necessarily direct compliance requirements for biopharmaceutical manufacturing, many of the principles from these guidelines may still be used. As an example, Annex 1 states: “The intent of the Annex is to provide guidance for the manufacture of sterile products. However, some of the principles and guidance, such as contamination control strategy, design of premises, cleanroom classification, qualification, validation, monitoring and personnel gowning, may be used to support the manufacture of other products that are not intended to be sterile such as certain liquids, creams, ointments and low bioburden biological intermediates, but where the control and reduction of microbial, particulate and endotoxin/pyrogen contamination is considered important. Where a manufacturer elects to apply guidance herein to non-sterile products, the manufacturer should clearly document which principles have been applied and acknowledge that compliance with those principles should be demonstrated.” Annex 1 [7] includes some specific guidance including, but not limited to, the following: • “An effective risk management system is integrated into all areas of the product life cycle with the aim to minimize microbial contamination and to ensure the quality of sterile products manufactured.” • “The manufacturer has sufficient knowledge and expertise in relation to the products manufactured and the equipment, engineering and manufacturing methods employed that have an impact on product quality. “ • “Risk management is applied in the development and maintenance of the CCS, to identify , assess, reduce/ eliminate (where applicable) and control contamination risks. Risk management should be documented and should include the rationale for decisions taken in relation to risk reduction and acceptance of residual risk.” • “Senior management should effectively oversee the state of control throughout the facility and product lifecycle. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 19 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities EudraLex Volume 4 Part IV on GMP for ATMPs [34] dedicates a whole section on risk-based approach. Some of the key points include the following: •“The risk-based approach permits the manufacturer to design the organisational, technical and structuralmeasures that are put in place to comply with GMP -and thus to ensure quality- according to the specificrisks of the product and the manufacturing process. While the risk-based approach brings flexibility , it also implies that the manufacturer is responsible to put in place the control/mitigation measures that arenecessary to address the specific risks of the product and of the manufacturing process.” •“When identifying the control/mitigation measures that are most appropriate in each case, the ATMP manufacturer should consider all the potential risks related to the product or the manufacturing processon the basis of all information available, including an assessment of the potential implications for thequality, safety and efficacy profile of the product, as well as other related risks to human health or to theenvironment. When new information emerges which may affect the risks, an assessment should be madewhether the control strategy (i.e. the totality of the control and mitigation measures applied) continues to beadequate.” •“The evaluation of the risks and the effectiveness of the control/mitigation measures should be based oncurrent scientific knowledge and the accumulated experience. Ultimately , this evaluation is linked to the protection of patients.” •“The level of effort and documentation should be commensurate with the level of risk. It is neither alwaysappropriate nor always necessary to use a formal risk management process (using recognized tools and/ orinternal procedures e.g., standard operating procedures). The use of informal risk management processes(using empirical tools and/or internal procedures) can also be considered acceptable.” From a regulatory and GMP compliance perspective, risk management practices need to assess and mitigate the risk of contamination and cross-contamination during the design of the processes, equipment, systems, and the facility itself. Page 20 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 2.5 Contamination Control (Chapter 4) Another important aspect for overall facility GMP compliance is the development, implementation, and maintenance of a CCS. EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7] provides the following guidance: •“A Contamination Control Strategy (CCS) should be implemented across the facility in order to define allcritical control points and assess the effectiveness of all the controls (design, procedural, technical andorganisational) and monitoring measures employed to manage risks to medicinal product quality and safety . The combined strategy of the CCS should establish robust assurance of contamination prevention. TheCCS should be actively reviewed and, where appropriate, updated and should drive continual improvementof the manufacturing and control methods. Its effectiveness should form part of the periodic managementreview. Where existing control systems are in place and are appropriately managed, these may not requirereplacement but should be referenced in the CCS and the associated interactions between systems shouldbe understood.” •“Contamination control and steps taken to minimize the risk of contamination from microbial, endotoxin/pyrogen and particle sources includes a series of interrelated events and measures. These are typicallyassessed, controlled and monitored individually but their collective effectiveness should be consideredtogether.” The development of the CCS requires detailed product and process knowledge. Based on the overall importance, Chapter 4 of this Guide is dedicated to CCS. In addition, the ISPE Baseline ® Guide: Volume 7 – Risk-Based Manufacture of Pharmaceutical Products (Second Edition) [38] provides valuable guidance on the control of cross- contamination. 2.6 Biosafety Biological Safety Levels (BSLs) are a series of protections established for activities within biological laboratories. BSLs are individual safeguards designed to protect the operating space, personnel, as well as the surrounding environment and community. For an evaluation of the risk of product contamination and cross-contamination, refer to Chapter 7. These levels, which are ranked from one to four, are selected based on the agents or organisms that are being researched or worked on in any given operating space setting. The primary risks that determine levels of containment are infectivity, severity of disease, transmissibility, and the nature of the work being conducted. For example, a basic laboratory setting specializing in the research of nonlethal agents that pose a minimal potential threat to laboratory workers and the environment are generally considered BSL-1, the lowest biosafety lab level. A specialized research operating space that deals with potentially deadly infectious agents, like Ebola, would be designated as BSL-4, the highest and most stringent level. Biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities traditionally employ various biological safety levels including BSL-1, BSL-2, BSL-3 Laboratory scale, as well as

considered BSL-1, the lowest biosafety lab level. A specialized research operating space that deals with potentially deadly infectious agents, like Ebola, would be designated as BSL-4, the highest and most stringent level. Biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities traditionally employ various biological safety levels including BSL-1, BSL-2, BSL-3 Laboratory scale, as well as Large Scale (LS) manufacturing. In the US, the CDC and NIH are the primary agencies that set BSL levels. These levels are set as a way of exhibiting specific controls for the containment of microbes and biological agents. This Guide uses the term BSL but recognizes that the terminology outside the US may be different; however, the principles are similar.For instance, in Europe, biological agents are classified as Groups 1 through 4 (with 1 being lowest risk and 4 being highest risk). Group 1 biological agents require a containment level 1 space, and Group 4 agents require a containment level 4 space. These levels are determined by the following: •Risks related to containment •Severity of infection •Transmissibility •Nature of the work conducted •Origin of the microbe •Agent in question •Route of exposure Biosafety levels are important because they dictate the type of work practices that are allowed to take place in a laboratory or manufacturing setting. They also heavily influence the overall design of the facility as well as the type of specialized safety equipment used within it. The following is a brief explanation of each biosafety level based on CDC/NIH BMBL guidelines [35] in the US, what they mean, and how they differ in safety measures and best practices. For additional details, consult the local laws and regulations in the location where the laboratory or manufacturing facility is located. Biocontainment rules are more prescriptive than GMPs and include a matrix of requirements. This is required because the end result is similar to GMPs but it is harder to justify an alternative solution. 2.6.1 BSL-1 As the lowest level of the four, biosafety level 1 applies to operating space settings in which personnel work with low-risk microbes that pose little to

of requirements. This is required because the end result is similar to GMPs but it is harder to justify an alternative solution. 2.6.1 BSL-1 As the lowest level of the four, biosafety level 1 applies to operating space settings in which personnel work with low-risk microbes that pose little to no threat of infection in healthy adults. An example of a microbe that is typically worked with at BSL-1 is a nonpathogenic strain of E. coli. The BSL-1 operating space setting typically consists of research taking place on benches without the use of special contaminant equipment. A BSL-1 laboratory, which is not required to be isolated from surrounding facilities, houses activities that require only standard microbial practices, such as: •Mechanical pipetting only (no mouth pipetting allowed) For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Page 22 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities • Safe sharps handling • Avoidance of splashes or aerosols • Daily decontamination of all work surfaces when work is complete • Hand washing • Prohibition of food, drink, and smoking materials in laboratory setting • Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), such as eye protection, gloves, and a laboratory coat or gown • Biohazard signs BSL-1 laboratories also require immediate decontamination after spills. Infection materials are decontaminated prior to disposal, generally through the use of an autoclave. 2.6.2 BSL-2 Biosafety level 2 covers laboratories with work involving agents associated with human diseases (i.e., pathogenic or infectious organisms) that pose a moderate health hazard. Examples of agents typically worked with at BSL-2 laboratories include equine encephalitis viruses and HIV, as well as Staphylococcus aureus (staph infections). BSL-2 laboratories maintain the same standard microbial practices as BSL-1 laboratories, but also include enhanced measures due to the potential risk of these microbes. Personnel working in BSL-2 laboratories are expected to take even greater care to prevent injuries such as cuts and other breaches of the skin, as well as ingestion and mucous membrane exposures. In addition to BSL-1 expectations, the following practices are required in a BSL-2 laboratory setting: • Appropriate PPE must be worn, including lab coats and gloves. Eye protection and face shields can also be worn, as needed. • All procedures that can cause infection from aerosols or splashes are performed within a BSC. • Autoclaving or alternate decontamination is required for proper disposal. • The operating space has self-closing, lockable doors. • A sink and eyewash station should be readily available. • Biohazard warning signs should be prominent. Access to a BSL-2 laboratory is far more restrictive than a BSL-1 laboratory . Outside personnel, or those with an increased risk of contamination, are often restricted from entering when work is being conducted. 2.6.3 BSL-3 Building upon the two prior biosafety levels, a BSL-3 operating space typically includes work on microbes that are either indigenous or exotic and can cause serious or potentially lethal disease through inhalation. Examples of microbes worked with at BSL-3 operating space include yellow fever, West Nile virus, and the bacteria that causes tuberculosis. These microbes are so serious that the work is often strictly controlled and registered with the appropriate government agencies. Operating space personnel are also under medical surveillance and may receive immunizations for the microbes they work with. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 23 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Common requirements in a BSL-3 operating space include: •Standard PPE (required) and respirators (might be required) •Solid-front wraparound gowns, scrub suits, or coveralls (often required) •All work with microbes performed within an appropriate BSC •Hands-free sink and eyewash that are accessible near the exit •Sustained directional airflow to draw air into the operating space from clean areas towards potentially contaminated areas (exhaust air cannot be recirculated) •A self-closing set of locking doors with access away from general building corridors •Airlocks for gowning, degowning and showers, decontamination of gowning •Operates under lower pressure than the surrounding (negative to the perimeter of the room) •Gastight ventilation system •High Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA)-filtered exhaust air •Decontamination system for drain lines •Room decontamination (Vaporized Hydrogen Peroxide (VHP)) Access to a BSL-3 operating space is restricted and controlled at all times. 2.6.4 BSL-4 BSL-4 laboratories are rare with less than 60 installed worldwide as of 2021 [45]. As the highest level of biological safety, a BSL-4 laboratory consists of work with highly dangerous and exotic microbes. Infections caused by these types of microbes are frequently fatal without available treatments or vaccines. Two examples of such microbes are the Ebola and Marburg viruses. In addition to BSL-3 considerations, BSL-4 laboratories have the following containment requirements: •Personnel are required to change clothing before entering, shower upon exiting •Decontamination of all materials before exiting •Personnel must wear appropriate PPE from prior BSL levels, as well as a full body, air-supplied, positive pressure suit •Class III BSC •Airlocks for gowning, degowning, and showers; decontamination of gowning •Operates under lower pressure than the surroundings (negative to the perimeter of the room) •Gas tight ventilation system •HEPA-filtered exhaust air For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. The operating space also features a dedicated supply and exhaust air, as well as vacuum lines and decontamination systems. 2.6.5 Large Scale (LS) Biosafety L evels The principles of biological safety levels or containment levels for large scale manufacturing facilities are based on the same principles as laboratory levels with the additional concerns related to the use of larger equipment and larger volumes of chemicals and raw materials. As a result of these larger volumes, additional risk assessments beyond what is traditionally performed for laboratories are required and these risk assessments focus on the hazard identification, hazard risk evaluation, risk control, and engineering controls associated with the larger equipment and increased volumes. An entire appendix in the CDC/NIH BMBL 6th edition [35] has been dedicated to large scale biosafety (Appendix M) and the NIH Guidelines for Research Involving Recombinant or Synthetic Nucleic Acid Molecules [39] includes an additional appendix focusing on large scale biosafety. Appendix K is where large scale (LS), greater than 10 liters per container is defined. Understanding the differences in biosafety levels and their corresponding safety requirements is imperative for anyone working with microbes in a laboratory or manufacturing setting. Biological safety requirements may differ slightly based on geographic location, so it is critically important to understand and follow the local laws and regulations where the facility is located. 2.7 Commissioning, Qualification, and Validation The Commissioning and Qualification (C&Q) of biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities, utilities, equipment, and systems is similar to other pharmaceutical facilities and can follow the principles and methodologies as detailed in the ISPE Baseline ® Guide: Volume 5 – Commissioning and Qualification (Second Edition) [46]. This C&Q Baseline Guide details a compliant, integrated commissioning and qualification process that is based on a science and risk-based approach. The methodology described in the C&Q Baseline Guide aligns with the QRM and risk-based approach principles detailed in this Guide and that are required in the manufacturing of biopharmaceutical products. In addition to the ISPE Baseline ® Guide: Volume 5 – Commissioning and Qualification (Second Edition) [46], ISPE has several other Guidance Documents that provide guidance specific to qualification and validation requirements directly applicable to biopharmaceutical manufacturing. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 25 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 3 Risk Management 3.1 Introduction QRM is an integral element of a quality management system. It provides a method to systematically identify, evaluate, control, and monitor risks to process and product quality. Risk management can be applied proactively and retrospectively. It also facilitates continual improvement throughout the product lifecycle as described in ICH Q9 [6]. Risk management enables optimal solutions to common problems by clarifying design intent, identifying hidden hazards, and preventing over-design which can result in unnecessary costs or complexity. QRM is a dynamic process that is managed throughout the lifecycle of a product, system, or facility. There are several regulatory requirements and guidance for industry applicable to biologics design, manufacturing, and control, as detailed in Chapter 2. EudraLex Volume 4 GMP Chapter 1 [34] describes the fundamental principles for the application of risk management as follows: •“The evaluation of the risk to quality is based on scientific knowledge, experience with the process andultimately links to the protection of the patient •The level of effort, formality and documentation of the quality risk management process is commensuratewith the level of risk.” The application of these principles at the appropriate phases of facility requirements definition, facility design, process development, and manufacturing enables companies to identify mistakes early, when the cost to address them is lower. QRM should be applied to enhance the understanding of products, processes, systems, and facilities, as well as the relationships between them. The output of QRM is used to improve facility design and inform qualification and control strategies. 3.2 Benefits of Risk Management for Facility Design, Build, and Qualification The primary benefit of QRM in facility design is to ensure that facility infrastructure and equipment are well-designed and suitable for manufacturing. This is accomplished by identifying potential risks associated with the facility and its interface with the equipment and processes within it via connecting facility critical aspects to CQAs and CPPs. QRM helps increase knowledge about the connection between the facility and the process to ensure product quality as well as personnel and patient safety. Better assurance of product quality is achieved through the proactive identification and avoidance or minimization of quality risks, as well as the identification of sources of variability in the product and manufacturing process that may be targeted for continual improvement. In addition, the decision-making process can be enhanced, as QRM provides a lens through which scientific data and information can be viewed to better weigh options and understand potential outcomes of a given decision. QRM can also “beneficially affect the extent and level of direct regulatory oversight” by increasing regulator’s trust in a company’s self-awareness and continuous

enhanced, as QRM provides a lens through which scientific data and information can be viewed to better weigh options and understand potential outcomes of a given decision. QRM can also “beneficially affect the extent and level of direct regulatory oversight” by increasing regulator’s trust in a company’s self-awareness and continuous improvement culture through transparency of QRM efforts. [6] Additional benefits of QRM are outlined in ISO 31000 [53] and include but are not limited to: •Improving controls, performance, and reliability •Reducing deviations and rework •Reducing the potential impact of human error •Effectively allocating and using resources, whether related to personnel or capital •Enhancing health and safety performance, as well as environmental protection For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Page 26 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Increasing the likelihood of achieving objectives •Improving operational effectiveness and efficiency •Encouraging proactive management •Improving the identification of opportunities and threats •Improving governance •Improving stakeholder confidence and trust •Establishing a reliable basis for decision-making and planning •Improving loss prevention and incident management •Improving organizational learning and resilience 3.3 Risk Management Principles and Practices 3.3.1 Risk Management Master Plan QRM activities may be planned or unplanned, depending on the application circumstances. For the construction of a new facility, planned improvements to an existing facility, or other circumstances in which the application of QRM can be anticipated, the establishment of a Risk Management Master Plan may be helpful. The plan will be of most value when it includes the following: •Risk assessments to be completed, including: -Timing for each risk assessment, as linked to the overall project plan -Risk question for each risk assessment (refer to Section 3.3.2.3 for additional detail) -Resources required (e.g., participants/subject matter experts) for each risk assessment -Risk tool(s) to be used for each risk assessment •Mechanism for documenting the output of each risk assessment (e.g., risk assessment report) •Mechanism for tracking the progress of each risk assessment and resultant risk control/mitigation activities (refer to Section 3.3.5 for additional detail) •Requirements for aligning the timing of risk control/mitigation implementation with project stage gates to ensure risk has been reduced and deemed acceptable before progressing the project •Timing for periodic risk review and triggers for event-driven risk review (refer to Section 3.3.6 for additional detail) Generally, risk assessments should be performed as early as possible in the strategic planning phase of the project and updated throughout the project (e.g., preliminary design, detailed design) as additional data and information is available. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 27 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities • Contamination control strategy • Cross-contamination control strategy • Process control strategy • Process closure analysis • Operational risks to quality or performance • Reliability and redundancy • Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) review for hazards • Sampling strategy • Health, Safety, and Environment (HSE) – slips, falls, chemical hazards, potent compounds, BSL hazards, etc. Table 3.1 in Section 3.4 provides suggested tools to be used for the various types of risk assessments. 3.3.2 QRM Initiation The first step in the QRM lifecycle is the initiation of the process. ICH Q9 [6] describes activities that might be performed during this initiation step, including but not limited to: • Identification of resources (stakeholders and participants) • Defining the scope of the risk assessment • Defining the risk question or problem statement • Selecting a risk tool or method (refer to Section 3.4 for additional information) • Gathering applicable data and information that will serve as inputs into the risk management effort Where a Risk Management Master Plan has been established, initiation of QRM occurs as directed by the plan. Otherwise, the organization should establish clear criteria for when QRM is required. It is important to note that QRM activities are most valuable when done as early as possible, before significant resources have been expended to implement designs with inherent risks. The insight offered through QRM should inform design activities in a proactive way, rather than through the identification of flaws later in the process where mitigation or correction may require significant resources to correct. 3.3.2.1 Identifying Resources QRM is best performed using a cross-functional team of experts to provide data and knowledge to support the identification and evaluation of risk. Such individuals, collectively referred to as Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) should have direct experience or expertise in the subject central to the risk assessment. It should be noted that SMEs are separate from stakeholders, i.e., those with a vested interest in the outcome of the risk assessment and the ultimate recipients of risk communication. A company may identify many stakeholders for a given QRM exercise, typically framed as functional group affiliation or leadership positions within the company . These individuals may not possess the same knowledge or have access to the same data required of SMEs to participate in the process, and therefore should be considered separate from the team who will conduct the risk assessment and subsequent risk control actions. Page 28 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Architects •Facilities/maintenance experts •Engineers •Automation experts •Process scientists •Qualification/validation experts •Manufacturing operators •Microbiologists •Quality/compliance experts Many companies benefit greatly from the use of qualified QRM facilitators as part of their QRM teams. Facilitators are neutral arbitrators with in-depth knowledge of QRM principles and practices, including QRM tools (refer to Section 3.4). The facilitator leads the QRM team through a QRM tool or risk-based approach, ensuring the principles of QRM are maintained, the tool is used properly, subjectivity or bias (refer to Section 3.3.3) is identified and minimized, and there is an appropriate balance of participation from all identified SMEs. The enhanced QRM training received by QRM facilitators ensures the highest quality and most value-added outputs are achieved through each risk assessment. Finally, QRM activities benefit from the identification of a risk assessment owner. This individual is ultimately responsible for managing a specific risk assessment and related QRM activities. Typically, risk assessment owners are those with primary authority over the subject or area being evaluated through the risk assessment (e.g., system owners). They are responsible for identifying, assessing, and managing the risk, as well as ensuring that the risk is being effectively controlled and monitored. Risk assessment owners work with others in the organization to develop and implement risk management strategies and are responsible for communicating information about the risks to stakeholders. 3.3.2.2 Defining the Scope of the Risk Assessment In order to ensure QRM activities add value and can be completed in a timely manner, the scope of the risk assessment needs to be clearly defined. Elements that will be addressed (in scope) should be described, as well as those that will not be addressed (out of scope). Any assumptions that are necessary to preserve the scope of the risk assessment should also be listed. For example, risk assessments performed in support of facility design should include product CQAs and process CPPs as inputs. In these cases, the accuracy of CQA and CPP identification can be assumed, as these can be addressed throughout separate risk assessments. Similarly , in some cases, harm to the patient may not be explicitly addressed but can be inferred based on impact to CQAs or CPPs, rendering patient harm out of scope. 3.3.2.3 Defining the Risk Question The risk question is what the risk assessment seeks to answer. It encompasses the problem statement or objective in a question format. Risk questions vary from short and simple, such as “what are the risks associated with this facility?” to complex, such as “what are the risks associated with disrupting ongoing GMP activities in Building 1 during concurrent construction in Building 2?” Risk questions should clearly identify the scope and nature of the concerns. Risk questions should also be neutral to the outcome, to preserve objectivity

ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 29 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 3.3.2.4 Gathering Applicable Data and Information Prior to beginning a risk assessment, SMEs should gather data and information on potential hazards, the likelihood (and where applicable, detectability) of those hazards occurring, and the impact they would have if they did occur . Additionally, data on the controls in place to control and/or mitigate risks should be collated. This data can be in the form of: •Applicable documents, such as PFDs, P&IDs, facility maps, utility drawings, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), etc. •Historical data and records for the current topic or for similar products, processes, systems, facilities, utilities, etc. •Applicable regulations, standards, guidelines, or industry best practices •External regulatory and industry literature, articles, and technical documents that discuss the potential topic being assessed •Information from internal or external audits or inspections, where applicable •Facility/process walk-throughs; examining samples of materials or products, video demonstrations, etc. •Results from experiments or engineering runs •Qualification and validation data and records These data, as well as scientific knowledge and expertise of SMEs participating in the risk assessment, will serve as inputs into the QRM process and support the identification, analysis, and evaluation of risk. 3.3.3 Risk Assessment After initiation, a risk assessment is performed. This phase of the QRM lifecycle seeks to determine which risks associated with the product, process, or system under review are unacceptable. The determination is made in three general steps. First, hazards are identified as applicable to the problem statement/risk question ( hazard identification ). Each hazard is then analyzed to determine its relative criticality ( risk analysis), in most cases based on the likelihood of occurrence and severity of the associated harm. Finally , the identified hazards and analyzed risks are compared with pre-defined criteria to determine their acceptability or need for reduction ( risk evaluation). The risk assessment phase of the QRM lifecycle typically draws most heavily upon the use of risk management tools, discussed in Section 3.4, which allows for a methodical, structured way to identify and analyze risks. It is important to note that subjectivity can have a significant adverse impact on the outcome of a risk assessment. Over-reliance on participant experience or disregarding legitimate data sources, as well as using heuristics (rules of thumb) in lieu of critical analysis, can introduce bias into the process, leading to inaccurate or unreliable results. For example, an individual may personally favor a particular technology or method, and this could influence their assessment of the associated risks.

legitimate data sources, as well as using heuristics (rules of thumb) in lieu of critical analysis, can introduce bias into the process, leading to inaccurate or unreliable results. For example, an individual may personally favor a particular technology or method, and this could influence their assessment of the associated risks. Additionally, subjectivity can lead to disagreements and inconsistencies between SMEs, making it difficult to reach a consensus on the level of risk associated with a particular hazard. To minimize the impact of subjectivity in a risk assessment, it is important to use a systematic and objective approach, such as utilizing established methodologies and guidelines, using a cross-functional team, and establishing guidelines regarding the weight of scientific evidence needed for a risk assessment. Page 30 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 3.3.4 Risk Control The risk control phase in the QRM lifecycle uses the output of the risk assessment to focus on reducing risks to an acceptable level. This phase is perhaps the most important of the QRM lifecycle, as it is the point in the process in which control strategies are identified, implemented, and continually improved. Risk control is comprised of two activities: risk reduction (a concerted reduction in risk through the application of risk mitigation techniques) and risk acceptance (a confirmation that the reduced and/or remaining risk is adequately controlled and therefore acceptable, including determination that the risk mitigation actions did not adversely affect the overall risk profile through the introduction of new risks or an increase in risk levels). The process of risk assessment and risk control is repeated as necessary until all risks are identified, analyzed, and accepted. 3.3.5 Risk Review Once risk control is complete and the results have been documented, the risk review phase begins. The objective of risk review is to apply lifecycle management principles to the risk assessment and risk control outputs. Risk review ensures that prior activities and associated deliverables remain accurate, relevant, and complete in consideration of changing conditions. The knowledge gained over the product lifecycle, such as that revealed through changes to the facility, equipment, process, or product or through unplanned event management (i.e., deviations and investigations), has the potential to impact decisions made in the risk assessment and risk acceptance phases of the lifecycle. Risk review, therefore, entails a periodic or event-driven review of these changes to determine whether the original risk assessment should be updated and whether the acceptability of the risk may be affected as a result. 3.3.6 Risk Communication Throughout the QRM lifecycle, risks and related information are shared with interested parties through a process called risk communication. Risk communication aims to ensure all applicable stakeholders are aware of risk information and knowledge and can, therefore, use such knowledge for risk-based decision-making. Such communication most commonly occurs at the output stage of the QRM lifecycle, leveraging the documentation

shared with interested parties through a process called risk communication. Risk communication aims to ensure all applicable stakeholders are aware of risk information and knowledge and can, therefore, use such knowledge for risk-based decision-making. Such communication most commonly occurs at the output stage of the QRM lifecycle, leveraging the documentation associated with the risk assessment and control activities as the primary mechanism to communicate. However, risk communication can, and should, occur at other stages of the QRM lifecycle, based on the nature and criticality of the identified risks. 3.3.7 Inappropriate Use of QRM Risk assessments are not appropriate under the following circumstances and, therefore, should not be used for these purposes: •To justify or support practices that are inadequate or non-compliant with GMP requirements, regulatory expectations, and regulatory commitments •As a substitute for science or data •When issues are clearly obvious and must be fixed •To justify a predetermined conclusion •To rationalize workarounds For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 31 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 3.4 Risk Management Tools and Techniques While there are several thousand risk management tools used for a variety of contexts, most companies engaged in biopharmaceutical manufacturing will benefit from learning and using a curated toolkit consisting of 5 to 10 core tools spanning different levels of complexity and formality. Such tools may include but are not limited to: •Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PrHA or PHA) •Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) •Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) •Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)/Failure Modes, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) •Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) •Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA) •What-if analysis •System Risk Assessment (SRA; refer to the ISPE Baseline® Guide: Volume 5 – Commissioning and Qualification (Second Edition) [46] for addition detail) An appropriate QRM tool should be selected prior to starting a risk assessment, taking into consideration the risk question, scope, and data available to complete the assessment.
