Page 32 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities High-level descriptions including why and when to use these tools are detailed in the following sections. 3.4.1 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PrHA/PHA) A PrHA (also known as a PHA) is a preliminary evaluation of hazards often undertaken early in the life of a process. The purpose of a PrHA is to evaluate any potential hazards at a high level only. Hazards are ranked based on frequency and consequence. Once the hazards or hazard scenarios are identified, improvements, safeguards, and preventive measures are formulated. An example PrHA is presented in Table 3.2. Table 3.2: Example PrHA Hazard Mitigation Action Pressure out of specification Use pressure regulator and monitor Microbial contamination of fermentation vesselImplement use of presterilized single-use systems for fermentation, confirm sterility via review of certification upon receipt, perform visual inspection for damage of outer bag prior to use Loss of power Connect system to Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS) A PrHA is used to analyze the product, process, equipment, environment, facility design, and system interfaces. It can also be useful when evaluating the major hazards for a general product type, product class, or a specific product. A PrHA is typically executed in the early design phases of the project when little design detail or operating procedures are developed. It can be beneficial when analyzing larger facilities when circumstances prevent other techniques from being used. This method has been used successfully for high-level chemical and biological hazards as well as high-level HSE considerations and initial or preliminary reliability and redundancy analyses. 3.4.2 Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) A HAZOP is a structured, systematic examination of a facility, process, or operation to identify credible deviations from the design and/or operational intent. The purpose of a formal HAZOP is to stimulate the imagination of participants to identify potential hazards, risks, and operability issues. Structure and direction are given to the review process by applying standardized guide words to the review of each specified node (see Table 3.3). The HAZOP calls for team members to display intuition and good judgement, and for the meetings to be held in a climate of positive thinking and open, honest discussion. The HAZOP’s use of parameters and guide words can spark thought and discussion in the session, helping with inexperienced teams. Deviations, or potential problems, to design specifications and design intent are identified by combining

for the meetings to be held in a climate of positive thinking and open, honest discussion. The HAZOP’s use of parameters and guide words can spark thought and discussion in the session, helping with inexperienced teams. Deviations, or potential problems, to design specifications and design intent are identified by combining relevant process parameters and their effect using guide words. For example, for the parameter “flow”, guide words such as “high” or “reverse” can be used to identify the deviations “excessive flow” and “backflow”. A key feature of HAZOPs is using nodes to subdivide the process into manageable pieces. Nodes are typically developed from architectural zones, P&IDs, or PFDs where the design intent is defined. New nodes should be used where the design intent changes. For example, a boiler could be defined as a node since it changes the state of the fluid, and a new node could be used for the distribution system with a defined pressure and flow . Page 34 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 3.4.3 Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HA CCP) HACCP is a systematic, science-based, preventive analysis used to identify, assess, and prevent contamination and other hazards in product production. It includes steps designed to prevent problems before they occur and to correct deviations as soon as they are detected. The main purpose is to ensure product quality, reliability, and safety. It is typically used in food, cosmetic, biopharmaceutical, and medical device applications. HACCP is based on the following seven principles: 1.Hazard analysis 2.Critical control point identification 3.Establishment of critical limits 4.Monitoring procedures 5.Corrective actions 6.Record keeping 7.Verification procedures HACCP can be used to identify and prevent biological, chemical, and physical agents that are likely to cause hazards if not controlled. It can be executed for a new facility and process design to determine if the facility is appropriate for the desired process. HACCP can be useful in determining the appropriate environment to house a biopharmaceutical process, by assessing potential ingress of environmental contaminants during the total process lifecycle. The outputs of an HACCP are a Risk Mitigation Action Plan that establishes corrective actions to be taken if a critical limit is not met and a Monitoring Plan for critical control points. Corrective actions are intended to ensure that no product injurious to health or otherwise adulterated as a result of the deviation enters commerce, while monitoring of critical control points is intended to ensure the process or facility remains in control over time. HACCP has been used successfully for the evaluation of risks pertaining to the performance or quality of a product, and for developing robust sampling and monitoring programs. This methodology has been used successfully for forensic evaluation of contaminations and similar deviation events. 3.4.4 Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA) / Failure Modes, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) An FME(C)A is a semi-quantitative, methodical analysis of potential failure where the analyst asks, “What happens if…?” to each component or process step independently of one another. It is a bottom-up process of analysis where the result is evaluated for impact, causes are investigated, and corrective actions are recommended. FME(C)As can be performed on equipment, systems, processes, use applications, or other designs (as with medical device design). The remainder of this section will focus on the use of the FME(C)A method for assessing equipment failure. An equipment focused FME(C)A starts with listing all equipment (e.g., valves, pumps, compressors, vessels, lines, etc.) and may take into account sequences in the operation of a system. It then involves identifying the potential failure modes, causes, consequences, existing preventive controls and detection methods, probability, severity, and risk classification. Facilities The purpose of an FME(C)A is to improve the performance of a process by identifying the potential effects of failure and generating recommendations for responses, safeguards, redundancy , and spare parts. The effects are determined by the operation’s response to equipment failure. An FME(C)A helps to determine whether there are adequate controls in place, what could go wrong for every component in the system, and whether the components are appropriately sized for the system. An FME(C)A can be used to prioritize risks and monitor the effectiveness of the risk control and mitigation activities. The outputs of an equipment focused FME(C)A is a Risk Priority Number (RPN) which prioritizes the relative risk of all identified failures, a systematic reference list of equipment, and the corresponding failure modes and effects, worst case estimate of consequences, and recommendations to mitigate risk. The level of detail investigated in an FME(C)A can assist in determining critical components and parameters. This tool has been used successfully in detailed P&ID reviews, highly consequential events due to inadequate implementation of safety and Quality Control (QC) measures, and component criticality analysis. It can be revisited and updated as additional data and information becomes available. 3.4.5 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) An FTA uses a top-down method of analysis that looks at all potential root causes and scenarios leading to the result of failure. It is based on logic and usually requires published or historical data. It starts with a graphical diagram to stimulate discussion of all sequences of events that could result in an incident (e.g., contamination of a bioreactor). The FTA approach evaluates all potential sources of hazard and investigates each scenario one at a time in further detail. This methodology can also be used as a preventive strategy before incidents take place, to develop monitoring programs and safeguards. 3.4.6 Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA) The LOPA method aims to identify the multiple layers of control necessary to adequately control a specific hazard, such as contamination. A LOPA is commonly used to evaluate containment strategies in microbial/particulate contamination control, cross-contamination control (i.e., product-to-product contamination), and hazardous material containment control. LOPA is derived from normal accident theory, which holds that accidents occur (and risks realized) when an event occurs that allows vulnerabilities to manifest simultaneously or in sequence, enabling a hazard to bypass all control layers and result in harm. Normal accident theory is often visualized using the Swiss cheese model, shown in Figure 3.2 and discussed further in Chapter 4, which depicts the multiple layers of control as cheese slices and the vulnerabilities within each control layer as holes in the cheese. Any gaps in the control strategy, or risks/vulnerabilities associated with the controls themselves, are documented and analyzed. Additional or modified design or engineering controls, as well as procedural or personnel controls, are determined to remediate gaps or mitigate the risk. Conducting a LOPA early in the design process (i.e., ~30% design) can help optimize the design to ensure containment of hazards and completeness of risk control. Revisiting and updating the LOP A through later stages of design and associated quality system alignment ensure that risks or vulnerabilities associated with the identified controls are likewise minimized. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 41 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 4 Contamination Control Strategy 4.1 Introduction Ensuring the safety of a biological drug product requires comprehensive contamination control across the entire manufacturing process. Contamination control is an essential program, not only as a sound business practice, but it also aligns with the requirements in EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7] (refer to Chapter 2 for more information on Annex 1). This chapter provides guidance on how to develop a CCS for biopharmaceutical drug substance manufacturing facilities. Contamination control requires a holistic approach, starting with an understanding of the types of

with the requirements in EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7] (refer to Chapter 2 for more information on Annex 1). This chapter provides guidance on how to develop a CCS for biopharmaceutical drug substance manufacturing facilities. Contamination control requires a holistic approach, starting with an understanding of the types of contamination and the sources by which contaminants may be introduced to the product. The product should be provided with layers of protection against these sources of contaminants. Risk assessments should be performed to ensure sufficient layers of protection for the product. These risk assessments require periodic review and verification. In case of discrepancy between expected and actual contamination control, changes to the layers of protection should be considered and verified with a revised risk assessment. The strategy that is developed for the layers of protection are the risk mitigation measures that should be captured in the CCS. A CCS should be developed for a facility to ensure sufficient protection is provided for all products manufactured in that facility. The CCS should be a living document. Contamination control risk assessments drive revisions to a CCS, for which changes should be managed through the quality change management process. 4.2 Contamination 4.2.1 Types of Contamination Contamination is the introduction of an undesired level of impurities, whether chemical, microbiological, or foreign matter (e.g., particles), into or onto raw materials, intermediates, or API. The first step in contamination control requires an understanding of the different types of contamination. Contamination can be introduced to the product in various forms: •Biological •Particulate (inert) •Chemical Biological contamination encompasses living biological organisms such as microbes (i.e., bioburden), submicroscopic infectious agents such as viruses, pathogenic agents such as endotoxins/pyrogens and prions, as well as other cellular debris such as proteolytic enzymes that break down proteins and fragments of genetic material (i.e., RNA, DNA). Bioprocessing is generally performed in an aqueous media that can introduce viruses and support the growth of bacteria. Because viruses and other adventitious agents can propagate in the host cells used to manufacture the product, bioprocesses are prone to contamination by the propagation of these agents. Bioburden contamination such as by bacteria, yeast, or molds can disrupt the physiochemical properties of a bioprocess solution, yielding chemical contaminants and conditions that are potentially harmful to the process, product, and ultimately, the patient. Inert particulate contamination includes, for example, packaging materials, gasket fragments, broken seals, dust, hair , skin, paint chips, and metal shavings. Particulates can serve as vehicles for chemical and biological contaminants, presenting a risk to biopharmaceutical processes and products. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 42 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Chemical contaminants include compounds that could react with the manufacturing process, impact sampling, inhibit or inactivate the API, or otherwise provide a risk to the patient (e.g., carcinogenic, toxic, undesirable side effects). Examples of chemical contaminants are leachables and extractables from

Page 42 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Chemical contaminants include compounds that could react with the manufacturing process, impact sampling, inhibit or inactivate the API, or otherwise provide a risk to the patient (e.g., carcinogenic, toxic, undesirable side effects). Examples of chemical contaminants are leachables and extractables from process equipment surfaces and single-use components. For further information on bioprocessing, see the ISPE Guide: Biopharmaceutical Process Development and Manufacturing [55]. 4.2.2 Sources of Contamination Contamination can enter a bioprocess through various pathways. To mitigate the risk of contamination, it is important to assess the various sources of contamination for product processes. Figure 4.1 depicts the different sources of contamination. It shows the potential sources and ingress pathways of contaminants entering Process 1, which is enclosed by a primary barrier depicted as the dark blue box. The primary barrier is the layer of protection, closest to the product, that is controlled and surrounding the process. The primary barrier can be equipment, such as a vessel or a single-use bag, or an isolator used when performing open processing. The primary barrier can be provided with a utility jacket for process control purposes (e.g., cooling, heating), as depicted in the figure as the yellow box connected with the process barrier . The primary barrier can also be connected with utilities shared by the different process barriers, such as CIP systems or waste collection systems. The secondary barrier is the second layer of protection that is controlled and surrounding the process. In this example, the secondary barrier is the environment surrounding the primary barrier (e.g., cleanroom, biosafety cabinet). The environment can be entered by personnel and is controlled by the HV AC system. For example, the process zone for a closed bioreactor is limited to the equipment boundary as shown in Figure 4.2. The boundaries of the process zone are indicated by the orange border. Page 46 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 4.3 Contamination Control Strategy Contamination control serves to protect the product from harmful contaminants, preventing the introduction of an undesired level of impurities with the potential to adversely impact product quality. Per Annex 1 [7], a CCS is required for sterile drug product manufacturing facilities and is recommended for biopharmaceutical drug substance manufacturing facilities. Mitigation of the potential harm to the product from contamination can be achieved by creating layers of protection in and around the process. In addition to protecting the product, layers of protection may also be required to protect the operators from a hazardous process. A risk assessment should be used to assess the need for different layers of protection required to protect the product. Since contamination risk is product specific, a risk assessment should be performed for each product manufactured in the biopharmaceutical facility. The rest of this section further discusses the development of a CCS and protective layers. 4.3.1 Elements of Contamination Control Contamination control requires a holistic approach to the design and operation of the biopharmaceutical manufacturing facility as depicted in Figure 4.4. Contamination control starts with foundational elements of scientific knowledge of the process and operations, quality culture that fosters personnel awareness of contamination control, and QRM. These elements are critical for ensuring that the CCS is built on a solid foundation that the organization can support and to which it will adhere. Figure 4.4: Foundational Elements of Contamination Control Strategy Courtesy

the process and operations, quality culture that fosters personnel awareness of contamination control, and QRM. These elements are critical for ensuring that the CCS is built on a solid foundation that the organization can support and to which it will adhere. Figure 4.4: Foundational Elements of Contamination Control Strategy Courtesy of Sanofi, adapted with permission. A product contamination event can have a substantive impact on patients, either directly or indirectly via a product shortage. For events with a high probability of impacting a product CQA, a common approach is to develop multiple layers of protection, as described in Chapter 3. For a bioprocess, these layers can be organized as process design, materials, equipment, and environment, forming the pillars with which the CCS is built. Each of these layers of protection depends on personnel and operational procedures which are further discussed in Section 4.3.2. Once established, the CCS is maintained by validation of the controls, continuous monitoring of the controls, and a governance framework that defines oversight and escalation during contamination events. Continual adjustments and improvements are required to adjust the CCS based on further developed understanding and knowledge of contamination risks or contamination events. This continual improvement is driven and implemented by utilizing programs such as Corrective Actions and Preventive CAPA, investigations, as well as a formal change control process. Further guidance on developing a comprehensive CCS can be found in the PDA Technical Report 90 [56]. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 47 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 4.3.2 Mitigation of Risk Potential Pr oduct and Process Contamination The objective of contamination control is to protect the product from contamination through appropriate protective layers in the process zone. Realizing that each protective layer has weaknesses, it is common practice to have multiple layers of protection to protect the product from each of the contamination risks. Figure 4.5 illustrates how the product can be protected from an initiating event or contaminant by providing protective layers. The intent of the layers is to reduce the risk of contamination to acceptable levels. The risk assessment should account for risk factors such as severity, likelihood of occurrence, and detectability. The implementation of these layers of protection against contamination should be documented according to the company’s QRM system (see ICH Q9 [6]), demonstrated as robust in process validation, and monitored and evaluated for performance through, e.g., in-process testing, monitoring data, requalification [47, 56]. Figure 4.5: Layers of Protection 4.3.2.1 Process At the core of the contamination control risk assessment is the product, which is generated by the process. The process design is generally the innermost control layer. Risks to the product that are not controlled by the process need to be mitigated by other layers. The most effective layers of protection of the product from harm caused by contaminants are the process conditions. When process conditions are designed with harsh

design is generally the innermost control layer. Risks to the product that are not controlled by the process need to be mitigated by other layers. The most effective layers of protection of the product from harm caused by contaminants are the process conditions. When process conditions are designed with harsh biological environments, such as bactericidal or bacteriostatic conditions, the product is protected against biological contaminations. These process conditions can be accomplished by low or high pH, process conditions with low water activity levels (e.g., solvents mixtures), high or low temperatures, or UV light for fluids with high UV absorbency . To maintain these process conditions, operational procedures are required, e.g., process duration limitations, solution hold times, and solution storage conditions. Process steps may be required to provide an additional layer of protection against particulate and chemical contaminations. This can be achieved by removal of contaminants via filtration, diafiltration, chromatography, precipitation, or washing. Operational procedures are required to effectively perform these process steps and achieve the desired outcome. Designing functionally closed processes can also provide the required protection against particulate contaminants. Figure 4.6 shows an example of the various ways that the process can provide a protective layer to the product. In this example, process conditions alone do not provide protection against non-viable and chemical contamination, requiring process steps such as filtration to protect the product. If the process is bacteriostatic, the process conditions inherently provide a protective layer against biological contaminant (V iable 1). If the process is not bacteriostatic, For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. Page 48 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities operational procedures (e.g., limited process time) and process steps (e.g., filtration) may be required to protect against biological contaminants (Viable 2) and control the bioburden to acceptable levels. Since many biological processes are aqueous and do not provide sufficient protection against viable contaminants, it is critical to control bioburden by using materials containing low levels of bioburden, limiting processing time, as well as providing process steps to protect the product. It is not an acceptable practice to allow biological contaminants to proliferate in the process (Viable 3) and rely on subsequent process steps, such as filtration, to control the bioburden. Figure 4.6: Protection by Process Conditions and Process Steps 4.3.2.2 Equipment Equipment can provide a physical barrier between the product and the environment. In closed systems, equipment can be the primary protection for the product against contamination. Where possible, the equipment should be designed to be closed in order to limit the process zone to within the equipment and to prevent ingress of contaminants from the environment with a high degree of assurance. Closed systems are process systems that can be opened, but are functionally closed through cleaning, sanitization, and/or sterilization processes after which the system remains closed during production within the system. The system is isolated from the environment prior to introduction of

from the environment with a high degree of assurance. Closed systems are process systems that can be opened, but are functionally closed through cleaning, sanitization, and/or sterilization processes after which the system remains closed during production within the system. The system is isolated from the environment prior to introduction of the product and during process contact. Open interventions during material additions or sampling need to be eliminated from the process. Closed systems can be multi-use systems, single-use systems, or a hybrid of both. Functionally closing a system is considered to be an effective approach for mitigating the risk of crossover contamination. Closing a system also effectively mitigates the risk of contamination by personnel and all other sources of contamination present in the environment. Contamination of a closed system from the environment would require a breach of that system, typically as a result of multiple failures of mechanical components (e.g., valve and/or filter failures) representing the layers of protection for that system. Crossover contamination between two adjacent closed systems would require a breach of integrity of both systems (e.g., where one system spills or releases a contaminant and the second system’s integrity is breached concurrently allowing ingress of the contaminant). When all equipment used to manufacture a product is closed, the surrounding environment becomes less critical. In these cases, the environment is a no impact system. The cleanroom requirements should be based on the outcome of a business risk assessment and could be categorized as unclassified. The execution of a formal preliminary closure analysis risk assessment is recommended during the conceptual design phase of a project to determine the appropriate environmental conditions in which to locate a bioprocess. Examples are provided by PDA [57] and BioPhorum [58]. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For multi-use systems, CIP is typically used for removal of potential physical, chemical, and biological contaminants to a level appropriate for the low bioburden operation. For multi-use systems, this includes removal and/or inactivation of latent contaminants adhering to equipment surfaces (e.g., residues from previous uses) to prevent carryover, per cleaning validation standards). Operational procedures are required for multi-use equipment to maintain the functionality of the protective equipment layer. These procedures cover aspects such as setup, process hold times, dirty and post-CIP/sanitization clean hold times, and post-SIP sterile or sanitary hold times. Carryover contamination in multi-use systems typically results from inadequate system preparation, including cleaning and sanitization operations between manufacturing batches. The residues from a previous product campaign, batch, or process step – which are carried into a subsequent product, batch, or step – need to be removed to acceptable levels. The equipment should be designed and constructed to support CIP or cleaning while the piping system should be devoid of dead legs. Validation of the cleaning and sanitization program should adequately mitigate the risk of carryover. An effective cleaning and sanitization program should include robust monitoring, ensuring that systems are being maintained in a validated state and that operations are performed according to the established cleaning plan validated within the program. Extractables and leachables of single-use systems should be assessed in terms of the risk to the product. Single-use systems also require operational procedures to provide effective protection against contaminants, such as making aseptic connections (e.g., sterile connectors, tubing welds) when/where required. For additional design and operation guidance of single-use systems, refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Single-Use Technology [59]. 4.3.2.3 Materials Materials consist of raw materials, critical utilities, and consumables that are used to manufacture the product. Materials can introduce contaminants in the product. A comprehensive materials strategy can provide the necessary layers of protection for the product. QC programs should include the specification, preparation, storage, and testing of materials. Materials need to meet the required low contaminant criteria prior to use. Pre-use conditioning may be required to reduce contaminant levels to acceptable levels before introduction to the process. The details of managing the fit-for-purpose design, qualification, and continuous verification of materials differ by source. Vendor quality programs should be in place to ensure that materials meet the specifications upon delivery [60]. These programs may include supplier qualification, audits, annual evaluation, and process validation, depending on the impact

process. The details of managing the fit-for-purpose design, qualification, and continuous verification of materials differ by source. Vendor quality programs should be in place to ensure that materials meet the specifications upon delivery [60]. These programs may include supplier qualification, audits, annual evaluation, and process validation, depending on the impact and risk of contaminants in the material. Utility systems should be designed to provide materials free of contamination and be monitored continuously or prior to use. Refer to the ISPE Baseline ® Guide: Volume 4 – Water and Steam Systems (Third Edition) [12] and the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Process Gases (Second Edition) [61]. The risk of contamination from raw materials can be monitored and mitigated using established QC strategies. Ingredient specifications, including bioburden, impurities, properties, and recommended storage conditions, should be verified and controlled. Dry materials such as powders, salts, and filters should be stored in environments with controlled humidity and temperature. Solutes used in media and buffer formulation are usually not sterile; storage of these ingredients may require: •Sterilization •Storage under bacteriostatic conditions and protection from particulate contaminants •Usage within specified hold periods (with limited shelf life and before microbial contaminants proliferate) Media and buffer preparation is a common example of a briefly exposed process. In this process, the solute is added to the preparation vessel via an open process and quickly dissolved. Subsequently, the process is rendered closed by filtration or by transferring the solution to a closed system where the solution undergoes suitable heat treatment. Media are typically at higher risk than buffer solutions because of their growth-promoting properties. The composition of media may affect their susceptibility to contamination; chemically defined media may carry lower risk compared to undefined media. The use of serum or other animal derived ingredients in media can affect the susceptibility to contamination. Figure 4.8 shows an example where materials provide a layer of protection for the product. In this example, the media components are added to the process. QC of the material (media components) provides a layer of protection. Additional protection is provided by the equipment used to prepare the media and the process steps (filtration before introducing the media to the product). Filtration itself also relies on several layers of protection, such as the filter cartridge (material), the housing or enclosure of the filter (equipment), and the filtration step and conditions (process step). potential sources of contamination. QC programs for these consumables also provide a layer of protection for the product. This includes sourcing and vendor monitoring (including sterilization, shipping, storage, and handling conditions) integrity testing for critical components, and introduction into the process zone. 4.3.2.4 Environment Performing aseptic and even low bioburden operations in an open system can represent a high risk. Accordingly, these operations should be performed in closed systems where feasible. Closed processing represents the option with the lowest risk in terms of mitigating the risk of contamination from the environment for both aseptic and low bioburden operations. For operations that cannot be practically closed, the process zone expands to include the cleanroom. These operations should be processed in a monitored classified environment that aligns with the bioburden and particulate requirements of the process unit operations, whether aseptic or low bioburden. Control of the environment provides another layer of protection for the process when there are open operations. When the environments housing these open processes are shared, contamination from the environment and crossover contamination from neighboring systems can occur. The layer of protection provided by the environment consists of facility design and operational procedures to maintain the environmental conditions. Facility design requirements to control the environment include room finishes, utilities (e.g., waste collection systems), HVAC systems, and layout considerations (e.g., air locks and GMP flow of personnel, materials, product, equipment, and waste). The facility design should provide the appropriate classification for the operations performed in each space. Room finishes should support the required cleanroom conditions and cleaning/sanitization requirements. Periodic room cleaning and sanitization is required to meet the overall environmental conditions required to manufacture a safe product. Personnel and material flows should be evaluated to mitigate the risks of cross-contamination and exogenous contamination. For example, a facility designed for unidirectional flow of process, materials, and personnel may be required for open process-centric operations, whereas bi-directional flow is acceptable for closed processes. This difference arises because in a closed process, the environment does not impact the product and therefore flows do not impact contamination control. See Chapter 7 for a more detailed discussion of facility layouts and Chapter 8 for architectural design. HVAC systems should be designed and operated to maintain the viable and total particle count to levels commensurate with the process requirements and to control temperature and humidity . In a facility based on closed processes, controlling environmental conditions may not be required for contamination control. Conversely, in a facility based on open processing, control of these conditions is critical and provides an additional protective layer to the product. In addition, the HVAC design should ensure that mixing of air between spaces is controlled, if necessary. Differential pressures and unidirectional airflows are a method for limiting the ingress of contaminants into a space. In certain scenarios involving biosafety concerns, potent compounds, or particle generation by powder handling, additional air locking methods are used to create bubbles, sinks, or cascades. These measures provide product protection and containment, limiting uncontrolled migration of compounds or particles in and out of a manufacturing area. For additional guidance, refer to Chapter 9 on mechanical systems. Personnel also play a critical role in maintaining the environmental protective layer, as personnel are continuously interacting with the equipment and process. Protecting an open system from potential contamination by operators requires attention to personal hygiene and effective gowning practices. Enhanced gowning practices may help reduce bioburden levels where a high level of environmental control is required due to the risk to the product (e.g., open processing). Enabling personnel to be an effective element in maintaining the environmental protective layer requires documented procedures, training, gowning/task qualification, and routine monitoring. Open processes are most vulnerable to contamination from their environments and may require many layers of protection. An example of this is open processing of cell cultures in a biosafety cabinet, as shown in Figure 4.9. Cell culture process conditions are typically aqueous and provide limited, if any, protection for the product. Contaminants in the raw materials may require protective layers provided by the materials used, equipment, and process steps (Initiating Event 1). Open processing in the BSC requires protective layers from the environment, equipment, and process steps (Initiating Event 2). For example, processes involving the handling of viral particles may require biosafety protection depending on the level of hazard. Potent compounds may require containment to protect manufacturing personnel. The use of solvents may require safety features for the equipment and facility to protect against deflagration or ignition. The containment requirements and safety features to protect manufacturing personnel and the facility from these hazards can impact the CCS and should undergo a risk assessment. For example, isolators are typically operated under positive pressure to the environment to protect the product. However, in the case of processing potent compounds, the isolator may need to be maintained at a lower pressure than the surrounding environment (negative pressure) to protect the operators. This increases the risk of background environmental air leaking into the isolator, potentially impacting the product. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 53 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 5 Process Closure 5.1 Introduction The biopharmaceutical industry employs a wide range of processes and addresses various modalities, as detailed in Chapter 1. As stated in Chapter 4, contamination control and the development of a CCS are required in the manufacture of active biopharmaceutical drug substances. Bioburden control constitutes the most important contamination control category. Non-viable particulates are likely inert to most bioprocesses and can typically be removed through standard bioprocess purification operations. However , the presence of bioburden in an aqueous bioprocess can result in the proliferation of an undesired culture, resulting in the generation of undesired metabolites and toxins if not properly controlled. These agents have the potential to impact product quality, and their removal cannot be guaranteed. This underscores why certain bioprocessing operations, such as cell culture operations, require aseptic conditions. In contrast, operations such as chromatography, where many nutrients required for bioburden proliferation have been removed, require controlled low bioburden operations. Additionally, there are operations performed under controlled conditions that are naturally bacteriostatic, where the risk of bioburden proliferation is effectively mitigated. The final state of a closed system must therefore consider the process conditions and the process requirements. It is important to emphasize that closed is not synonymous with sterile or aseptic except in those conditions where aseptic processing is required. To properly address process closure, a consensus first needs to be established on the definition and properties of a closed equipment, systems, or processes. As explained in the 2nd edition of this Guide, a closed system is one in which the process environment is isolated from the surrounding manufacturing space. The term environment can be ambiguous, as it can be used to describe the manufacturing suite but can also be used to define the space within a bioreactor. For this reason, this edition introduces the term process zone as shown in Figure 5.1. Figure 5.1: Closed System Process Zone – Bioreactor Example The process zone is the space that is in direct contact with the process, product, or product intermediate. As will be further described in Chapter 6, the guiding principle for process closure is the elimination of latent contaminants within the product-contact boundary to a level commensurate with the process requirements, whether those are sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden. This removal of latent contaminants occurs prior to product or process contact. For single-use systems, this objective is typically achieved by fabricating and assembling the system within a cleanroom environment. For single-use systems used in sterile or aseptic operations, the assembled system is subsequently sterilized (e.g., gamma irradiation

low bioburden. This removal of latent contaminants occurs prior to product or process contact. For single-use systems, this objective is typically achieved by fabricating and assembling the system within a cleanroom environment. For single-use systems used in sterile or aseptic operations, the assembled system is subsequently sterilized (e.g., gamma irradiation or autoclaving). In contrast, for multi-use systems, system closure is typically For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright © 2024 International Society for Pharmaceutical Engineering. All rights reserved. Page 54 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities achieved through CIP. When multi-use systems are used in sterile or aseptic operations, the cleaned system is then typically sterilized by SIP operations. Whether it is a single-use or multi-use system, and whether the process is required to be sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden, it is necessary to prevent ingress of contaminants from the environment surrounding the product-contact boundary prior to and during process contact. This includes the transfer of materials into and out of the closed system. It is important to note that non-aseptic transfers into any closed system (even those involving low bioburden processes) could potentially result in a breach of the closed system. Therefore, it is essential that all connections between closed systems remain closed. In essence, the establishment of a closed system lifecycle roadmap includes the following key aspects: 1.Defining the specific conditions required by the process, whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden 2.Defining the limits and boundaries of the process zone (product-contact surface) 3.Removing latent contaminants from the process to a level commensurate with the required process conditions 4.Maintaining the system/process as closed for the required duration by preventing the ingress of contaminants from the surrounding environment 5.Enabling the transfer of materials into and out of the closed system in a closed or aseptic fashion This chapter focuses on addressing the foundational activities of process closure. The requirements for contamination control have been addressed in Chapter 4 and operations will be addressed in Chapter 6. The evaluation and benefits associated with the implementation of closed processes and the evaluation of process closure are identified in various chapters of this Guide: •Regulatory (Chapter 2) – Regulations and guidance documents are provided to ensure product safety, effectiveness, and high-quality medicines and therapies for patients. Closure of the process contributes toenhanced control and reproducibility in the manufacturing of active biopharmaceutical ingredients and final drugproducts. •Risk Management (Chapter 3) – The principles of QRM are applicable across all facets of the facility, process design, and operation. Risk analysis of closed process systems is integral to the final design, qualification, andperformance of these systems. •Contamination Control Strategy (Chapter 4) – Where open or closed process systems are considered, the CCS is pivotal in the proper evaluation of each process step and sequence of process steps. Risk assessmentand analysis are employed to assess the source of contamination, its potential impact, and the likelihood of itsoccurrence, leading to the development of effective measures to mitigate

closed process systems are considered, the CCS is pivotal in the proper evaluation of each process step and sequence of process steps. Risk assessmentand analysis are employed to assess the source of contamination, its potential impact, and the likelihood of itsoccurrence, leading to the development of effective measures to mitigate the risk to product quality . •Operations (Chapter 6) – The operations chapter provides examples and detailed insights into both open and closed processing steps within various biopharmaceutical process modalities. •GMP Layouts (Chapter 7) – The GMP layout is directly impacted by the decision between open versus closed manufacturing schemes. The needs of the process are considered in a holistic overview of the process frombeginning to end, encompassing the product lifecycle in addition to multi-product segregation needs. •Mechanical (Chapter 9) – Product-contact utilities (clean air, Water for Injection (WFI), pure steam, CIP) and their control within specified limits provide for their introduction into closed process systems. Environmental control(HVAC) of classified cleanrooms is critical for open process systems. The quality of these environments shouldbe defined through risk assessment. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 55 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 5.2 Process Zone The point at which a process is protected from environmental contaminants has a significant impact on the design and operation of a biopharmaceutical manufacturing facility. Implementing closed processes effectively decouples the process from the manufacturing environment. As such, the use of multiple, large, expensive-to-design, expensive-to-build, and expensive-to-operate cleanrooms is not required to house these closed processes. ICH Q7 [5], originally published in 2000 and subsequently incorporated into the EudraLex and US FDA guidance documents, reinforces the concept that closed processes do not require cleanroom environments. Specifically , it states: “4.12 Where the equipment itself (e.g., closed or contained systems) provides adequate protection of the material, such equipment can be located outdoors.” The preference for closed systems is further reinforced: “5.15 Closed or contained equipment should be used whenever appropriate. Where open equipment is used, or equipment is opened, appropriate precautions should be taken to minimize the risk of contamination.” Closing a system or process by default limits the process zone. A closed system is one where the product-contact surfaces are limited by the process zone boundary. In the example of the bioreactor depicted in Figure 5.1, the process zone is limited by the walls of the bioreactor vessel and the walls of the piping system, components, and feeder vessels tethered (connected) to the bioreactor. Any breach of the integrity of these walls constitutes a breach of the closed system. In alignment with ICH Q7 item 4.12 [5], the closed stainless steel bioreactor could be successfully located outdoors without potential compromise to the quality of the cell culture. Additionally, the closed bioreactor provides a much higher level of assurance that the cell culture will not be compromised by environmental contaminants when compared to what any

item 4.12 [5], the closed stainless steel bioreactor could be successfully located outdoors without potential compromise to the quality of the cell culture. Additionally, the closed bioreactor provides a much higher level of assurance that the cell culture will not be compromised by environmental contaminants when compared to what any cleanroom could provide for an open system. Open systems used for large scale sterile or aseptic operations are not a viable choice in contemporary manufacturing schemes. As shown in Figure 5.2, a process zone that is limited by the closed system boundary is inherently easier to control than a manufacturing environment housing open operations. Open aseptic operations require a Grade A environment. Maintaining a Grade A cleanroom housing a 2K or 20K bioreactor is simply not feasible. A risk assessment would demonstrate that housing a closed aseptic process in a Grade C or Grade B cleanroom would not mitigate the likelihood or severity of contamination of the closed system. It would, however, result in lower detectability of a breach of integrity of the closed system, resulting in higher risk (higher RPN = Severity × Likelihood × Detection) associated with the overall process. The reason for this is that Grade C and B cleanrooms, while maintaining lower levels of contaminants, are not sterile or contaminant-free within their environments. Page 56 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Low bioburden operations also benefit from the use of closed systems. This benefit comes from limiting the process zone that needs to be controlled during operation and mitigating the risk of contaminants entering from the manufacturing environment during product contact. The manufacturing environment includes various elements such as conditions, neighboring operations, equipment, personnel, materials, and waste occupying the same space, all of which can potentially introduce contaminants into the process stream. In cases where a process is not closed, all these elements within the manufacturing environment constitute part of the process zone. Therefore, isolating the process zone from the manufacturing environment is an important factor in contamination control. For a process to be closed, all system process zones need to be connected (assembled or integrated) through a series of closed unit operations. In an ideal scenario, the entire process would be a fully integrated set of closed unit operations. A typical chromatography operation is used to illustrate the measures required to close a process step, with other examples provided in Chapter 6. A chromatography system comprises numerous components. The operation is characterized as low bioburden because it is not feasible (as of the time of Guide publication) to sterilize most chromatography media. However , like all bioprocesses, the need to control contamination, including bioburden, is a requirement. Even though the chromatography process is low in bioburden, achieving process closure is preferred. A typical chromatography system includes components such

not feasible (as of the time of Guide publication) to sterilize most chromatography media. However , like all bioprocesses, the need to control contamination, including bioburden, is a requirement. Even though the chromatography process is low in bioburden, achieving process closure is preferred. A typical chromatography system includes components such as a chromatography column, buffer delivery skid, eluate (product) collection system, waste collection system, bubble trap and filter, product feed, potentially a gas feed, and up to 6 (or more) buffer feed vessels. Any one of these components can serve as a potential source of contamination from the environment, if not properly assembled, prepared, conditioned, or connected. Non-bacteriostatic buffers that will be held for extended periods are at risk for bioburden proliferation if not prepared and stored appropriately . Sterile filtration to remove latent bioburden and storage in a presterilized vessel are typically required. While the column containing the chromatography media cannot be sterilized, it may require frequent sanitization to mitigate the risk of bioburden proliferation over the duration of the chromatography operation. As per the definition of process closure, the chromatography column must be cleaned and sanitized (to remove latent contaminants) to achieve an appropriate low bioburden state before the introduction of the product. The same principles apply to the other components (piping, pumps, filters, bubble trap, etc.) comprising the chromatography system. To properly close the system and maintain system closure, the chromatography skid and buffer supply manifolds within the closed system should be rendered closed after assembly of the fully integrated system is completed. If this is not possible, connection of any tethers to previously closed systems should be performed using aseptic connectors to mitigate the risk of introducing contaminants from the manufacturing suite environment where the system is housed. This concept applies equally to single-use systems as well as multi-use systems. When preparing closed systems that are designed for reuse, it is essential to consider not only the contaminants present in the manufacturing environment but also the potential for carryover contamination as well as contaminants that may be introduced during post-use CIP operations. Other considerations for closed systems include sampling and connections to waste streams. It is important to design sampling systems that do not pose a risk of contaminating the closed system. Additionally, implementing appropriate barriers from a tethered waste stream is necessary to protect the integrity of the closed system. These considerations are further discussed in Chapters 4 and 6. The determination of the process zone and the requirements for the manufacturing environment are assessed on a case-by-case basis, supported by the process closure risk assessment. The approach to achieving a closed process depends on the method of closure and the specific characteristics of the process itself. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 57 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 5.3 Impact of the Process Platf orm on Process Closure Bioprocessing can be performed using batch, fed-batch, semi-continuous, or continuous platforms. The selection of the platform significantly impacts the activities related to process closure. Batch processes require the discrete preparation of multiple systems, each dedicated to producing a distinct batch of the product. This approach requires significant intervention in closing multiple process systems over several batches. The objective is to isolate the process zone for a relatively short duration, typically less than 16 days for upstream operations and less than 24 hours for downstream operations. This enables the manufacture of the product while mitigating the risk of contamination from the manufacturing suite environment. In contrast, continuous processing relies more extensively on closed systems to maintain its integrity while isolating the process zone from the manufacturing environment for much longer durations. Timelines for continuous production can extend beyond 120 days of operation, demanding a very high level of control to maintain the integrity of the closed system. This platform has the advantage of requiring fewer cycles of preparation and setup per kilogram of product manufactured. The remainder of this section covers closed systems, process closure, and open systems in more detail. 5.3.1 Closed Systems A closed system is one that isolates the process zone from its manufacturing environment and prevents ingress of environmental contaminants during product contact. Any additions to or draws from closed systems must be performed in a manner that maintains a closure, ensuring that the product zone is not exposed to the manufacturing environment. When a system is closed (i.e., isolated from the manufacturing environment), further mitigating the risk of contamination to the product or process by housing the operation in a classified cleanroom becomes unnecessary . In a closed system, the process zone is limited by the equipment housing the process. The presence of a contaminant from the manufacturing environment within a closed system indicates a breach of the closed system and therefore constitutes a system failure. 5.3.2 Closing the Process The previous edition of this Guide made a distinction between closed systems and functionally closed systems. It is essential to understand that functional closure is not a state of the process, but rather the method by which process closure is achieved. There are no degrees or levels of closure; a system is either identified as closed or open. Most closed systems begin as open systems that are rendered closed prior to initiation of the bioprocess or introduction of the bioprocess ingredients (e.g., raw materials, gases, liquids, drug substances, intermediates, etc.) into the process zone within the closed system. Process closure is achieved by: 1.Removing latent contaminants within the process zone to a level commensurate with the process requirements, whether it is sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden, prior to process contact. 2.Preventing the ingress of environmental contaminants during process contact (maintaining

into the process zone within the closed system. Process closure is achieved by: 1.Removing latent contaminants within the process zone to a level commensurate with the process requirements, whether it is sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden, prior to process contact. 2.Preventing the ingress of environmental contaminants during process contact (maintaining process closure). Note: A closed system does not necessarily mean that it is sterile; it is closed to the appropriate condition, whether it is sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Page 58 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The removal of latent contaminants can be achieved by various closure operations. The choice of the appropriate methodology for process closure should be based on the equipment, platform, operation, and the required state of the system (sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden). This choice should be based on the contamination risk assessment. Examples of methods to achieve closure include: •Sterile Filtration – Bioprocessing solutions are seldom prepared aseptically. Raw materials, solvents (WFI), pH adjusting solutions, as well as the vessel used to prepare the solutions, typically contain varying levels ofenvironmental contaminants. The preparation of these solutions is typically performed in protected but opencontainers. Sterile filtration of these solutions into a clean and sterile container is an effective way of closing thesystem. •Thermal Sanitization or Sterilization – As mentioned earlier, bioprocessing solutions are rarely prepared aseptically. As an alternative to sterile filtration, these solutions can also be thermally sterilized by autoclaving(for single-use and multi-use systems) or SIP (for multi-use systems). Autoclaving or SIP can also be used tofunctionally close equipment intended for the closed storage of solutions prepared for closed operations. Theinitial phase of SIP is also effective for flushing product-contact surfaces prior to thermal sanitization. •Radiation – As an alternative to SIP, single-use systems, especially those in sterile or aseptic operations, aregamma-irradiated prior to use. Single-use systems are typically prepared in classified cleanrooms in an effort tominimize the level of latent particulates on the product-contact surfaces. •Chemical Sanitization – As an alternative to SIP or gamma irradiation, bioprocess systems can be closed by chemical sanitization. Examples include VHP, chlorine dioxide, and spray/wash with ozonated water, asappropriate to achieve the desire state (e.g., sterile, or low bioburden). •CIP – Not all closed systems can or need to be operated in a sterile or aseptic manner. As presented in Section5.2, chromatography operations can successfully run in a low bioburden state. CIP can be performed withsanitizing agents, but numerous studies (e.g., ASME BPE [62], BioPhorum [63]) and GMP operations haveshown that low bioburden states can be achieved by CIP without caustic (i.e., without sanitizing agents). Water isan excellent solvent for most of the contaminants that could potentially affect an aqueous bioprocess. Bioburdenreduction of 3 to 6+ log can be achieved in ASME BPE conforming multi-use bioprocess systems. As previously stated, process closure does not only involve the removal of

without caustic (i.e., without sanitizing agents). Water isan excellent solvent for most of the contaminants that could potentially affect an aqueous bioprocess. Bioburdenreduction of 3 to 6+ log can be achieved in ASME BPE conforming multi-use bioprocess systems. As previously stated, process closure does not only involve the removal of latent contaminants. For a system to be closed, the system must also prevent the ingress of contaminants after the removal of latent contaminants. The integrity of the closed system needs to be maintained for the full duration of the bioprocess, and for a process to be considered closed, the integrity of all integrated closed systems needs to be maintained. 5.3.3 Maintenance of a Closed Sys tem Once the latent contaminants of an assembled system are removed, ingress of contaminants from the manufacturing suite environment must be prevented. This is achieved by a combination of measures, examples of which are listed here: •Aseptic attachment of a system to a previously closed system – the attachment should provide that both systems being connected are closed prior to the tether to maintain closure. Multiple technologies can be used to maintainthe integrity of the closed system during a tether to that system: -Aseptic connectors allow for the aseptic connection of a transfer line between closed systems in an unclassified environment. Several aseptic connection technologies are commercially available. -Sterilizable connectors allow for non-aseptic connection of closed systems but are thermally or chemically sanitized, as appropriate, prior to exposing the product-contact surfaces and process zones of each closedsystem to each other. Although commercially available connectors of this type are available, many of theseconnectors are custom fabricated for the application. For example, steam docks are often found on multi-usestainless steel bioreactors. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 59 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Closed sampling systems are used to provide assurance of integrity of the sample taken from the closed system as well as to ensure the process zone within the closed system is not compromised by the sampling process. Closed sampling systems are available as single-use or multi-use options. Multi-use systems undergo SIP between sampling operations. Both single-use and multi-use sampling systems are presterilized prior to attachment to the closed bioprocessing system either by gamma irradiation or autoclaving. •Sterile vent filters and maintenance of positive pressure – temperature fluctuations and other processes and conditions in the manufacturing suite can result in a closed system exhausting/venting gas (e.g., as thetemperature in the suite decreases) and pulling a vacuum (e.g., as the temperature in the suite increases).Protecting the process zone with a sterilizing grade vent filter ensures that this gas exchange process does notcompromise the process zone. Following SIP, the cooldown of a stainless steel multi-use bioreactor results ina very significant vacuum (0.4 Vessel Volumes per Minute (VVM) or higher is possible depending on the rate ofcooling) in the process zone. It is critical

vent filter ensures that this gas exchange process does notcompromise the process zone. Following SIP, the cooldown of a stainless steel multi-use bioreactor results ina very significant vacuum (0.4 Vessel Volumes per Minute (VVM) or higher is possible depending on the rate ofcooling) in the process zone. It is critical that the bioreactor always remains under positive pressure by supplyingclean compressed air via a sterilizing grade filter into the process zone to prevent the uncontrolled draw ofcontaminated environmental air into the process zone, including the vessel and all tethered components andpiping systems. •Mix-proof piping systems – the integrity of closed systems is required to mitigate the risk of ingress of contaminants from the manufacturing suite environment as well as to protect the closed process zonefrom neighboring operations. This consideration is particularly important in multi-use systems, where thebioprocessing system is tethered to shared support systems. The typical multi-use stainless steel bioreactorillustrates this criticality: -In typical large scale bioprocessing facilities, many multi-use stainless steel process systems share a common CIP support system. A production bioreactor might be housing an active aseptic cell culture whilea centrifuge, previously used in a different batch or campaign downstream, is being cleaned and sanitizedby CIP. To ensure success, it is critical that the CIP operation does not pose any risk of compromisingthe closed bioreactor system. This is an important design consideration given the CIP solutions containharmful chemicals as well as soils from the previous batch or campaign, and the CIP process operatesunder high velocities and pressures. It is clear that appropriate design elements are required to ensure theCIP operation never penetrates into the cell culture. The ASME BPE [62] provides design elements andstrategies to mitigate the risk of compromising the bioreactor’s closed process zone. Examples include: >Implementation of block and bleed isolation valve systems to mitigate the risk of high pressures affecting the closed bioreactor process zone boundary >Installation of transfer panels that provide air breaks between the CIP and bioreactor systems >Use of mix-proof valves -Other means of mitigating the risk of compromising the integrity of a closed system include: >Incorporation of bypass systems, such as implementing bypass ports on a chromatography system to isolate the chromatography pump and piping system from the buffer feed ports and chromatographycolumn >Implementation of bypass ports on a Tangential Flow Filtration (TFF) system to isolate the TFF pump and piping system from the buffer feed ports and TFF filter holder These examples are further described in Section 5.4. EudraLex Annex 1 [7] mentions the possibility of closed systems being located outdoors (item 4.12), it is important to note that this statement does not include closed isolators. Annex 1 states: “Closed system – A system in which the product is not exposed to the surrounding environment. For example, this can be achieved by the use of bulk product holders (such as tanks or bags) that are connected to each other by pipes or tubes as a system, and where used for sterile products, the full system is sterilised after the connections are made. Examples of these can be (but are limited to) large scale reusable systems, such as those seen in active substance manufacturing, or disposable bag and manifold systems, such as those seen in the manufacture of biological products. Closed systems are not opened until the conclusion of an operation. The use of the term ‘closed system’ in this Annex does not refer to systems such as RABS or isolator systems.” As explained in Chapter 2, regulatory agencies acknowledge that closed isolators provide an effective means of isolating open operations from the manufacturing suite environment. Annex 1 [7] supports the implementation of closed isolators in appropriately controlled environments for open aseptic operations, which is consistent with the strategy presented in this Guide. A risk assessment should be performed to determine the necessary controls and conditions for ensuring the integrity of closed isolators during critical operations in the manufacture of active biopharmaceutical ingredients. 5.4 Equipment and Components Used in Closed Systems The procedures used to close a system should be commensurate with the risk and requirements of the closed process. For example, processes that are performed under bactericidal conditions, or even bacteriostatic conditions, may only require equipment cleaning to reduce the latent non-viable contaminant load on product-contact surfaces to an appropriate level (e.g., 3 log reduction = 99.9%) prior to product contact. This assessment should consider all potential contaminants (e.g., latent contaminants that are considered potent or toxic may require higher clearance levels although this is rare). All equipment used in closed sterile (e.g., hold vessels or bags used for long-term storage of a non-bacteriostatic solution) or closed aseptic operations (e.g., single-use or multi-use bioreactors for cell culture operations) need to be sterilized (6+ log reduction = 99.9999% reduction in viable contaminants) prior to product contact. A risk assessment should be performed to determine the appropriate functional closure strategy. Non-bacteriostatic solutions, such as the product solutions processed in early purification operations, are typically nutrient-rich and at greater risk of bioburden proliferation if not adequately controlled or stored for long durations under growth-promoting conditions (e.g., pH 7, temperatures 20–40°C, etc.). Low bioburden operations such as downstream chromatography or TFF operations are often limited by the inability (or impracticality) of sterilizing the media in the chromatography column or TFF filter elements. However , these operations can still achieve and maintain low bioburden closed processing through other appropriate controls without the risk of ingress of environmental contaminants. This allows such operations to take place in unclassified environments. In addition, functional closure of these systems

in the chromatography column or TFF filter elements. However , these operations can still achieve and maintain low bioburden closed processing through other appropriate controls without the risk of ingress of environmental contaminants. This allows such operations to take place in unclassified environments. In addition, functional closure of these systems allows for concurrent manufacture with multiple systems in a ballroom setting. 5.4.1 Closing Systems Used in L ow Bioburden Processes The definition of a closed system from Annex 1 of the EudraLex [7] provides valuable insights into regulatory expectations for establishing and maintaining a closed system, referred to as sterile manufacturing. Equipment used in low bioburden operations does not necessarily require sterilization to be closed or isolated from the manufacturing suite environment. Single-use components can be assembled in an open manner (non-aseptically, i.e., without use of aseptic connectors) IF the fully integrated and assembled single-use system can undergo CIP processes (with or without caustic agents) to remove latent contaminants from all product-contact surfaces, meeting the low bioburden condition defined by the process requirement. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 61 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities According to the definition in Annex 1, once closed, the process system “are not opened until the conclusion of an operation” [7]. This means that all connections made to the closed systems need to be closed (as described in Section 5.3.3), and none of the feeds into the closed system (e.g., product, buffers, gases) or effluents from the system (products, waste, samples) should contribute to the contamination of the closed system from the environment. Solutions added to a closed system after functional closure (whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden) need to be sterile filtered or demonstrated to show that they will not compromise the bacteriostatic state of the operation. This same approach also applies to closed stainless steel multi-use systems that are used in low bioburden operations. It is worth nothing that achieving appropriate CIP conditions (TACT) for system closure may be more feasible with rigid, integrated stainless steel piping and component systems. 5.4.2 Closed Syst em Examples The section provides examples illustrating components that are successfully used to assemble, achieve process closure, tether closed systems together after functional closure, and isolate closed systems from neighboring systems and operations. These discussions include both single-use and multi-use closed systems. It is important to emphasize that the effectiveness of these components in maintaining the integrity of the closed system is contingent upon their appropriate design, installation, and configuration. It is strongly recommended that the final configuration conforms to the current ASME BPE [62]. Example 1: Multi-use Stainless Steel Bioreactor Sharing CIP Circuits with Other Bioprocess SystemsIt is critical that a closed bioreactor maintains its integrity for a successful aseptic cell culture operation. Appropriate isolation of the bioreactor from concurrent CIP operations on other systems is required. The ASME BPE [62] recommends the use

Example 1: Multi-use Stainless Steel Bioreactor Sharing CIP Circuits with Other Bioprocess SystemsIt is critical that a closed bioreactor maintains its integrity for a successful aseptic cell culture operation. Appropriate isolation of the bioreactor from concurrent CIP operations on other systems is required. The ASME BPE [62] recommends the use of block and bleed valve arrangements as depicted in Figures 5.3 and 5.4. The intention of these arrangements is to provide two redundant points of isolation between the CIP and bioreactor systems, with the added layer of protection of the bleed. This design aims to ensure that if the outer block valve fails under pressure, the bleed valve will relieve the pressure on the inner block valve, mitigating the risk of bleed into the aseptic bioreactor. Alternatively, another approach involves the use of transfer panels to select different process flow paths, as depicted in Figure 5.5. The transfer panel provides complete isolation of the process flow path from the pressurized CIP operations by disconnecting the bioreactor completely from the CIP circuit. When the bioreactor is reconnected to the transfer panel, the design must be appropriate (ASME BPE [62] conforming) to ensure the assembled circuit does not constitute a source of contamination to the bioreactor during or after CIP. As further explained in Chapter 6, when properly designed and configured, CIP alone can effectively close many low bioburden operations. CIP alone is typically insufficient for subsequent long duration operations, unless they are inherently bacteriostatic or bactericidal. This includes process hold functions. For systems used for non-bacteriostatic operations or aseptic operations, SIP is typically required after CIP. Figure 5.3: Block and Bleed Valve Example Used with permission from ITT, www.itt.com. Example 3: Closed Aseptic Connections to Closed Systems As previously discussed, any tethers to a closed system cannot compromise the integrity of the closed system. Contaminants introduced into the closed system by the solutions feeding the system constitute a breach of the closed system. Similarly, introducing contaminants during the connection process (e.g., open connections, non-aseptic connections, inappropriate cleaning, and sanitization following connection prior to product contact) also constitutes a breach of integrity of the closed system.
