ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 65 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities These points of potential failure could compromise the successful closure and maintenance of a closed system for the duration of the process or operation. When the integrity of a closed system is breached, it becomes an open system potentially at risk of contamination from the manufacturing environment. The following represents further evaluation of these potential failure modes on closed systems which are relevant to both single-use and multi-use systems: • Vents – Bioprocess systems are typically pressurized. This positive pressure within the process zone serves to mitigate the risk of ingress of contaminants from the manufacturing environment. Any vacuum condition on a closed system should be considered a risk, even if the closed system is protected with a sterilizing grade filter . Sterile-grade vent filters are typically used to provide a further layer of protection. However , potential failure modes such as improper seating or sterilization (whether gamma-irradiated or thermally sterilized) of vent filters need to be carefully evaluated. • People – Operator errors remain an area of concern. Automating process closure operations can lead to fewer errors, and automated operations are typically easier to monitor, thereby providing a higher degree of detectability of a closure process failure. Measures should be put into place to mitigate the risks of human error. • Additions and Transfers – Most failures that result in the compromise of closed system integrity occur during transitions and transfers. Closed bags that store sterile-filtered solutions and remain untouched can remain bioburden-free without risk of compromise for extended periods, even years. Examples include blood bags, stable buffer solutions, cell banks, and others. Failures in connections (whether sanitary clamp end connections, aseptic connecting devices, tubing welds, steam docks, sterile filtration, multiport valves, etc.) will compromise the integrity of the closed system. Improper orientation of the sampling port can create dead legs, preventing proper SIP or CIP. Failure in gamma irradiation can compromise the quality of single-use tubing and aseptic connecting devices. It is essential to ensure that feeds going into a closed system do not harbor contaminants that could potentially compromise the quality of the process zone within the closed system. With very few exceptions, (e.g., perhaps processes of very short duration), all solutions fed into a closed system should be sterile filtered or thermally sterilized. • Waste – Connecting a closed bioprocess system to a waste system represents a significant risk. W aste systems are typically connected to multiple systems, some of which can pressurize the waste system, potentially causing a reverse flow condition into the closed bioprocess system. Appropriate measures need to be incorporated into the design to mitigate the risk of waste back-flowing into a closed bioprocess system due

W aste systems are typically connected to multiple systems, some of which can pressurize the waste system, potentially causing a reverse flow condition into the closed bioprocess system. Appropriate measures need to be incorporated into the design to mitigate the risk of waste back-flowing into a closed bioprocess system due to over-pressurization. • Sampling – Sampling from a closed system will compromise its integrity if the sampling assembly is not appropriately designed or properly assembled. Single-use sampling devices need to be supplied in a certified sterilized state (e.g., gamma-irradiated). Steam-sterilized multi-use sample valve assemblies need to designed and installed correctly in the proper orientation to allow for successful CIP and SIP. Improper orientation of the sampling port can create dead legs, preventing proper CIP or SIP. • CIP – Bioprocessing systems should be designed, fabricated, and assembled in conformance with the ASME BPE [62]. Following ASME BPE guidelines provides a high level of assurance that the system can be cleaned or conditioned appropriately. Biophorum [63, 65] and ASME [62] have demonstrated that CIP performed on systems conforming with the ASME BPE can reduce latent bioburden on product-contact surfaces by 3 to 6 log or higher. Although insufficient for sterile or aseptic processes, pre-use conditioning of a closed system by CIP (with or without sanitant) alone represents a functional closure operation suitable for most low bioburden operations. Moving an open process operation from a Grade D into a Grade B environment only reduces the potential or theoretical latent bioburden levels by less than 1.3 log. Pre-conditioning operations are notably superior for the purpose of functionally closing a process system. However, the key to success is the appropriate control of the CIP conditioning conditions, including the elimination of dead legs as stated in the ASME BPE. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Page 66 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •SIP – As previously stated, only stainless steel bioprocessing equipment conforming with the ASME BPE [62] should be used for multi-use sterile or aseptic bioprocessing operations (note: appropriately sterilized anddesigned disposable assemblies may be used for single-use aseptic or sterile applications). SIP can successfullyreduce theoretical bioburden loads on product-contact surfaces and within the process zone by 6 to 12 log orhigher. As with CIP, successful SIP depends on appropriately designed equipment and suitable SIP conditions.The ASME BPE recommends SIP at temperatures between 121°C and 130°C under saturated steam conditions(15–24 psig). All temperatures throughout the process zone (i.e., within the SIP boundary) need to be within 2°Cof each other during the dwell phase, to detect air pockets, dead legs, or zones of condensate pooling. The limitof 130°C ensures that elastomers or other temperature-sensitive components (e.g., sterile filter elements) remainuncompromised during the steaming process. Following the dwell phase, the closed system process zone shallbe protected from ingress of contaminants by pressurizing the process zone with sterile air to mitigate the risk ofa

of condensate pooling. The limitof 130°C ensures that elastomers or other temperature-sensitive components (e.g., sterile filter elements) remainuncompromised during the steaming process. Following the dwell phase, the closed system process zone shallbe protected from ingress of contaminants by pressurizing the process zone with sterile air to mitigate the risk ofa vacuum condition during a controlled system cooldown. •Leaks – Any leak originating from the process zone constitutes a breach of the closed system integrity. Arisk assessment needs to be performed in the event of a leak, as such breaches could result in the ingressof contaminants from the environment. It is important to note that the risk of a contaminating event due to abreach of integrity of the closed system is equal whether the system is housed in a classified cleanroom or inan unclassified environment. However, the level of contaminants and the variety of contaminating species willbe higher in the unclassified space. This needs to be considered in the risk assessment. All layers of protectionwithin the closed system should be evaluated. As an example, large scale fermenters rarely employ vent filtersto protect the process zone. This is because microbial fermentation operations are typically performed at veryhigh aeration and venting rates (0.3–2.0 VVM). Also, microbial species typically produce antibiotics that protectthe culture from contamination in the production phase. A leak under these conditions may have little effect.However, other bioprocesses are very sensitive to the ingress of contaminants, and a leak could result in a lossof product. •Duration – Most systems and operations are planned with a specific duration. The validation of a process typically includes duration as a Critical Control Parameter (CCP). When a process exceeds its validated duration,the process is potentially compromised. Exceeding processing time limits can result in system failure and thepotential introduction of contaminating organisms, chemicals, and/or product modifications that may affect theproduct quality. 5.5.1 Closed Systems V erification and Validation Verification/validation of a system closure is different for single-use disposable systems and for multi-use stainless steel systems. It is also different for closed systems used in sterile/aseptic operations and those used in low bioburden operations. However, the underlying driver for all closed systems is the same: a risk-based approach to process control that effectively isolates the process zone from the surrounding manufacturing environment. Extensive training as well as qualification of CIP (and cleaning) and SIP (sterilization) is required for system closure of a multi-use stainless steel system used in aseptic operations. A high degree of automation is required to ensure that critical operations are always performed in a systematic and reproducible way that conforms to the criteria used in the qualification of the system. Single-use systems offer a configurable closed system design, where the responsibility for ensuring clean and sterile assemblies lies with the supplier of the single-use components, assemblies, and systems (e.g., bags, tubing assemblies, filter elements, mix/hold bags, etc.). Prior to releasing the single-use systems for use in a GMP manufacturing operation, the documentation accompanying these systems must be verified. Single-use system manufacturers should be audited to confirm a quality plan is in place and to verify the

and systems (e.g., bags, tubing assemblies, filter elements, mix/hold bags, etc.). Prior to releasing the single-use systems for use in a GMP manufacturing operation, the documentation accompanying these systems must be verified. Single-use system manufacturers should be audited to confirm a quality plan is in place and to verify the single-use assemblies are being fabricated in a systematic, reproducible way and as expected. If the fabrication process requires a cleanroom environment, the environment should be verified to ensure it meets the stated cleanroom criteria. It is important to note that single-use systems require intense operator intervention. The success, integrity, and reliability of the single-use assembly heavily depend on the skill of the operators performing the setup and operation of the equipment. An operator certification program is highly recommended to ensure that operators are trained adequately to mitigate the risk of process execution failures. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 67 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Independent of the technology used, various methods have been used for validation and verification of system and process closure. Strategies for closed system verification, closed system qualification, and closed process validation include: •Use of Growth-Promoting Media Challenges – This approach can be used to confirm the integrity of all process systems, including challenges related to aseptic connection, SIP, low bioburden (or sterile) bulk drugsubstance fill, etc. This test is essential for testing operator training and equipment performance over the durationof the operation. •Filter Integrity Testing (FIT) – The BioPhorum [66] has performed a risk assessment providing a model that helps drug substance manufacturers determine where pre-use or post-use FIT should be performed in theirbioprocessing operations. •Pressure Integrity Testing – In stainless steel systems, pressure decay testing is used as a measure of systemintegrity. Single-use systems can typically only be pressurized to very low pressures (i.e., 2 psig or less), so otherintegrity testing protocols are used such as helium pressure testing with leak detection or Container ClosureIntegrity Testing (CCIT). •CIP/System Pre-conditioning/Functional Closure Validation – CIP is used for system cleaning and pre- conditioning. Although bioburden reduction is achieved, the cleaning is focused on removal of particulates andresidual soil and detergent to prescribed limits within cleaning validation studies. Once cleaning is achieved,the sanitization and sterilization would be accomplished through system SIP, as needed, for operations suchas indicated for bioreactor operations. Chromatography cleaning and sanitization would be performed withbioburden reduction steps to prepare the circuit for operation. Functional closure is obtained through use ofpresterilized single-use components and/or preassembled, sterile connections to the closed systems throughsterile assemblies or SIP and steam locked connections. There are increasing applications for the use of Process Analytical Technology (PAT). Online PAT can serve as a valuable tool to ensure that a process is running within a defined design space and that layers of protection have been applied (see Chapter 4 on CCS). For stainless steel closed systems (bioreactors, vessels,

increasing applications for the use of Process Analytical Technology (PAT). Online PAT can serve as a valuable tool to ensure that a process is running within a defined design space and that layers of protection have been applied (see Chapter 4 on CCS). For stainless steel closed systems (bioreactors, vessels, etc.), maintaining positive pressure and conducting pressure testing can provide evidence of circuit integrity and provide confidence in the closed system. The breach of a closed system, as identified through unexpected process deviations, is an ongoing concern so systems should be in place to detect process deviations [67]. For low bioburden API processing, verification of a closed process should also include verification of controls implemented to constitute the closed system [68]. 5.5.2 Risk Assessment Risk assessment is the cornerstone of process closure design and to the establishment of the process zone. As discussed in Chapter 3, there are numerous risk assessment platforms such as FT A, FMEA, and HACCP. These platforms can be used to evaluate the risks of failure and potential harm, and subsequently develop and implement risk mitigation measures that can be evaluated through the qualification and validation program. While mitigating known or anticipated process or system risks can improve process expectations, it cannot prevent all potential breaches of integrity of a closed system. For this reason, all breaches in closed system integrity need to be evaluated by risk assessment. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 69 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 6 Operations 6.1 Introduction This chapter addresses the operational aspects of a biopharmaceutical manufacturing facility. It highlights the impact of facility and equipment design decisions on manufacturing operation. Conversely, this chapter also emphasizes how choices in single-use versus multi-use technologies as well as operational choices can influence the environmental requirements of the manufacturing suites in which the assembly, cleaning, sanitization, conditioning, maintenance, and process operations of these systems are performed. The biopharmaceutical industry has made important technological advances, enabling manufacturing facilities to deliver drug substances of greater consistency and quality and that are, ultimately, safer for the patient. As presented in Chapter 5, closed process systems effectively isolate drug substance manufacturing operations from the environment. Process monitoring and control can then focus on the process equipment, effective process closure, and highly reproducible operations. These advances are especially important in the increasingly relevant context of personalized medicines, smaller product volume requirements, and higher titers, where facilities need to produce smaller volume product batches but much higher numbers of batches. This chapter incorporates adjustments to certain terms and definitions used in previous editions of this Baseline Guide. In this third edition, the term closed

context of personalized medicines, smaller product volume requirements, and higher titers, where facilities need to produce smaller volume product batches but much higher numbers of batches. This chapter incorporates adjustments to certain terms and definitions used in previous editions of this Baseline Guide. In this third edition, the term closed systems refers to both single-use polymeric systems and multi-use stainless steel (as well as other materials of construction) systems designed for CIP/SIP. As previously stated in Chapter 5, it is important to note that all closed systems are functionally closed, whether by conditioning (e.g., buffer flushing), cleaning and sanitization (e.g., CIP with or without sodium hydroxide), or sterilization (e.g., SIP, gamma irradiation, VHP) as appropriate (e.g., aseptic versus low bioburden processes), so the term closed is used universally in this Guide. Single-use polymeric systems used in aseptic operations are typically fabricated in cleanroom environments (to minimize the level of viable and non-viable contaminants during fabrication), packaged, and sterilized by irradiation. Multi-use stainless steel systems used in aseptic operations are typically designed to be cleaned by CIP before sterilization and then sterilized thermally by SIP. While other examples exist, the underlying principles are the same regardless of the technology used. The term, briefly exposed operation, has led to confusion within the industry and has consequently been replaced with the term controlled-condition open processing which is elaborated upon in this chapter. The term process zone (defined in Chapter 4), which is the processing environment that can influence or affect the process and product, is also introduced. In a closed system, the process zone is limited by the defined process boundary. In an open process, the process zone could include anything in the manufacturing suite. These concepts will also be discussed in this chapter. 6.2 Operations and GMPs The goal for all manufacturing processes is to consistently and repeatedly yield a high-value, high-quality product that is safe for patients. This concept constitutes the underlying principle of GMP. Large molecule manufacturing is typically performed in aqueous solutions. As such, these solutions are at risk for viable (microbial and viral) and non-viable (chemical and inert particulate) contamination. Viral, microbial, and endotoxin monitoring and control are therefore crucial to ensuring product quality and patient safety. The drug substances themselves are also at risk for degradation, necessitating monitoring and control measures. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 70 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Advances in technologies and systems applicable to large molecule manufacturing have provided biopharmaceutical companies with novel, more efficient, more robust, and ultimately safer ways to manufacture therapeutic agents. Given these new advances, regulatory agencies have raised their standards in terms of regulatory expectations and measure of quality. Improvements in collaboration and sharing of information between biopharmaceutical companies have resulted in a higher level of understanding of best practices. These best practices have been captured in a series of equipment and

new advances, regulatory agencies have raised their standards in terms of regulatory expectations and measure of quality. Improvements in collaboration and sharing of information between biopharmaceutical companies have resulted in a higher level of understanding of best practices. These best practices have been captured in a series of equipment and operating standards and practices such as the ASME BPE [62], the ISPE Baseline Guide series [69], ISPE Good Practice Guide Series [70] and BioPhorum consensus white papers [71]. These resources encompass strategies for operations, monitoring, and control that are designed to improve the quality profile of all drug substance manufacturing processes. Process fingerprinting, P AT, and a profound understanding of process CQAs are quickly becoming regulatory expectations. The focus for quality assurance is not simply on final drug substance testing, but rather on the entire supply chain and manufacturing process including opportunities for operational improvements. The vast majority of modern biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities are designed and built to support multi- product operations. Although facility costs typically represent a relatively small contributor to the overall product cost of goods, GMP manufacturing facilities capital costs typically exceed hundreds of millions of dollars (US$). These high costs require that facilities be flexible and sustainable to accommodate a variety of products and programs. Strategic approaches or levels of decisions to deliver flexible facilities are discussed in Chapters 7 and 8. Product-to-product changeover within confirmation that any residual product in the process equipment is removed and does not a facility can be a laborious and time-consuming endeavor. Product changeover requires documented contaminate the subsequent product beyond acceptable limits. Organizations must develop appropriate equipment design and cleaning strategies to mitigate the risk of carryover as part of their product changeover program. The downtime incurred during changeovers reduces the operational time available for product manufacturing. Organizations can use platform processes for similar types of products to minimize changes required for product changeover. Platform processes typically involves fewer automation changes to bring a new product into a facility. Automation changes can be quite time-consuming and complex. Appropriate segregation of systems and operations is necessary to ensure that process integrity and product quality are maintained. Multi-product facilities require a higher level of complexity in terms of segregation. Multi-product facilities can run campaigned operations, where the facility is dedicated to multiple batches of a single product simultaneously for a period of time, or concurrent operations, where the facility is manufacturing two or more products simultaneously. While campaigned operations may represent a lower overall risk, manufacturing multiple batches of the same product simultaneously can create potential risk to product quality if appropriate controls are not in place. In general, campaigned multi-product manufacturing operations require adequate temporal segregation of the unit operations at a minimum. Concurrent multi-product manufacturing operations require adequate spatial segregation to mitigate the risk of product-to-product (or process-to-process) crossover contamination. 6.2.1 Mitigating the Risk of Cross-Contamina tion in Campaigned Multi-product Operations •The risk of cross-contamination in campaigned multi-product operations can be successfully mitigated with appropriate controls in place. These include: •Appropriate changeover procedures between product manufacturing campaigns, including appropriate clearance of

These include: •Appropriate changeover procedures between product manufacturing campaigns, including appropriate clearance of intermediates and microbial contaminants. •Achieving appropriate product clearance (validated and verified) between product manufacturing campaigns. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 71 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities • Effective cleaning and sanitization of product-contact surfaces and pathways for multi-use systems by automated and validated CIP and SIP operations between product manufacturing campaigns. Cleaning validation strategies should be based on a risk-based approach and include a rationale for elastomer change-out (e.g., gaskets, diaphragms, hoses) as part of product changeover. While a complete changeover of these components can be an acceptable approach, it might involve significant effort and prove unnecessary if cleaning validation demonstrates acceptable removal of contaminants between batches. • The use of single-use systems ensures that the product-contact surfaces and pathways are replaced between product manufacturing campaigns. 6.2.2 Mitigating the Risk of Cross-C ontamination in Concurrent Multi-product Operations The risk of cross-contamination in concurrent multi-product operations can also be successfully mitigated with appropriate controls in place. These include: • The use of closed systems where possible. • Appropriate spatial segregation of manufacturing suites (including appropriate pressure cascades, air handler zoning, and room classifications). Where post-viral clearance operations are open, segregation of these operations from pre-viral areas is recommended. • Appropriate changeover procedures between equipment, system, or suite use. • Achieving appropriate product clearance (validated and verified) between equipment, system, or suite use. Clearance should be demonstrated by the removal of product, components, and waste residues from process equipment. Clearance procedures should be appropriately tested to ensure compliance with regulatory expectations. Cleaning endpoints should be established using scientific reasoning. • Use of dedicated equipment. • Effective cleaning and sanitization of product-contact surfaces and pathways by automated and validated CIP and SIP operations between equipment or system use. • Unidirectional GMP flows of personnel, equipment, materials, and waste. • The use of single-use systems mitigates the risk of contaminated product-contact surfaces of the equipment. 6.3 Appropriate Segr egation of Bioprocess Operations Segregation of bioprocess unit operations can be achieved in a number of ways. Performing a risk assessment is essential to comprehensively evaluate the risk of contamination of a system by environmental contaminants and by neighboring operations (see Chapter 3 for detailed information on risk assessments). Multiple strategies can be used to segregate bioprocess unit operations. In the example of a monoclonal antibody manufacturing process, segregation may include: • Upstream

Page 72 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities • Process support areas from product-contact processing areas - Warehouse areas - Media and buffer preparation areas - Weigh/dispense and other powder handling areas - Decontamination, parts washing, autoclaving, and CIP areas - Column packing and resin slurry areas • Open processing areas from closed processing areas • Low bioburden drug substance manufacturing operations from sterile drug product formulation and filling operations These strategies along with other strategies or options for defining manufacturing silos within a biopharmaceutical facility are discussed in Chapters 7 and 8. Also, the type of segregation, whether logistical or environmental, is discussed in detail. The segregation of functional operations and further segregation of environments should be based on risk assessments, rather than relying on legacy strategies that are frequently inappropriate or unnecessary in biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities using modern technologies. Most biopharmaceutical processes start with the cultivation of live organisms. This involves a variety of equipment such as shake flasks, roller bottles, cell factories, rocking motion single-use bioreactors, and computer-controlled, stirred tank single-use or multi-use bioreactors/fermenters. Successful cell culture and microbial fermentation operations must use axenic cultures. It is essential that the system can operate within defined process operating parameters. It is equally important that the system provides an impermeable barrier to the ingress of adventitious organisms from the environment, including from neighboring operations. Achieving this barrier requires an effective pre-use (i.e., pre-inoculation) sterilization of the process zone and appropriate barriers to the ingress of environmental contaminants throughout the cell culture (or fermentation) process. These aspects will be discussed further in this chapter. The presence of large volumes of active cultures potentially presents a risk of contaminating downstream process operations, which involves removal of impurities (including viable cells, cell debris, secondary metabolites, and media components) from the desired product. The presence of live cells also presents a risk of contamination and the proliferation of adventitious agents such as viruses. Due to this concern, most cell culture–based product purification processes remove viable cells early. Following removal of viable cells, the product-containing solution is often moved to another manufacturing area designated “downstream processing” to isolate the subsequent purification operations from upstream operations that involve viable cells. Once viable host cells are removed from the process stream, the risk of uncontrolled viral propagation is essentially mitigated. For this reason, open downstream operations where the product stream is subject to the ingress of viable contaminants should be effectively segregated

purification operations from upstream operations that involve viable cells. Once viable host cells are removed from the process stream, the risk of uncontrolled viral propagation is essentially mitigated. For this reason, open downstream operations where the product stream is subject to the ingress of viable contaminants should be effectively segregated from manufacturing areas containing large concentrations of host cells. The intent of this physical segregation is to mitigate the risk of reintroducing viable cells into an otherwise cell-free process stream. Once host cells are removed from the downstream process solutions, a key goal of the product purification process is to remove potential viral contaminants from the process stream. A series of orthogonal purification steps (i.e., both virus-inactivating and virus-clearing/removing) are used to provide a theoretical reduction in virus load that typically exceeds 12 log. T o mitigate the risk of reintroducing virus from a “pre-viral” operation into an open post-viral operation downstream, separate pre-viral and post-viral manufacturing areas are often designated. The dividing line between pre-viral and post-viral points in the downstream process may vary between products, processes, or even between manufacturing companies. However, this dividing line should always be after an operation of significant theoretical virus clearance. Multiple points of segregation may also be warranted in some situations. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 73 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities A risk assessment should be performed to confirm where segregation is warranted and how it should be implemented (see Chapter 3 for more details on risk assessments). The EudraLex Annex 2 paragraph 57 states: “In cases where a virus inactivation or removal process is performed during manufacture, measures should be taken to avoid the risk of recontamination of treated products by non-treated products.” [2] The risk assessment should also detail how the segregation should be implemented. Closed process systems, when properly implemented, can provide appropriate segregation of pre-viral from post-viral operations. Process boundaries and process closure operations that are effective against the ingress of microbial contaminants may not necessarily be effective against viral threats. The risk assessment can confirm if the closed process system has been implemented appropriately . Buffer and media preparation are core areas in many bioprocessing facilities. These areas are designed to provide solutions of appropriate quality to downstream low bioburden operations and to provide sterile solutions to the aseptic upstream process operations. The presence of microbial and endotoxin contaminants in these solutions could compromise or cause failure of the bioprocess they are intended to support. To mitigate this risk, segregation of open media and buffer preparation operations from the active bioprocess unit operations is often recommended. In addition to this contamination risk, the solution preparation operations are essentially powder hydration operations. The removal of powders from the biopharmaceutical manufacturing area is desirable from both environmental cleanliness and bioburden control perspectives. Powder spills can contribute to bioburden and particulate events in cleanroom

unit operations is often recommended. In addition to this contamination risk, the solution preparation operations are essentially powder hydration operations. The removal of powders from the biopharmaceutical manufacturing area is desirable from both environmental cleanliness and bioburden control perspectives. Powder spills can contribute to bioburden and particulate events in cleanroom environments. Similar to solution preparation operations, weigh/dispense operations involve powder handling areas that should be segregated from non-powder areas. To mitigate the risk of spreading powders, weigh/dispense operations should be performed in downflow booths with appropriate dust collection. The regulatory expectation is that sampling and dispensing of powders for buffer and media preparations do not compromise the quality of the powder . For this reason, the dispensing and weighing of these materials should be performed in an appropriate environment. Typically, a HEPA-filtered ventilation of a downflow weigh/dispense booth is prescribed. Given that powder handling will likely generate a high level of particulates, the stated quality of the downflow booth environment should only be monitored under static conditions. Appropriate cleaning and sanitization of the downflow booth should be performed between each powder handling operation to mitigate the risk of crossover contamination. Appropriate segregation of soiled equipment from manufacturing operations is required to mitigate the risk of carryover contamination. Although temporal segregation of soiled equipment and components from clean components is theoretically possible, it is rarely practical. For this reason, CIP operations for stationary equipment and pass-through parts washers are typically recommended to ensure clean and soiled equipment are appropriately segregated. Soiled depth filters represent a high risk of compromising the quality of the environment in which they are housed, during disassembly and cleaning operations. The handling of used depth filters as well as TFF filter elements needs to be carefully planned. Column packing operations typically include open resin slurry activities that are subject to the contaminants present in the environment. Unpacking and cleaning a used column can also compromise the quality of the environment where these tasks are performed. It is recommended to segregate these functions to both protect the open neighboring operations from carryover contamination from the soiled chromatography column as well as to protect the open clean resin slurry and column packing functions from neighboring operations. Given that resin slurry and column packing operations are typically open, a Grade D or C environment is often designed based on the results of the CCS (whether packed in place or in a dedicated column packing suite). Although segregation of manufacturing suites can provide a high level of logistical and environmental process segregation, closed systems (as described in Chapter 5) provide equal, if not greater, process segregation with the added benefit of providing the highest level of protection from environmental contaminants. This protection is discussed in Section 6.4. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. Page 74 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 6.4 Operational Bioburden Contr ol Measures Control of bioburden in all bioprocess unit operations is a regulatory requirement [14, 16] and is crucial for ensuring product safety. Microbial contamination and proliferation lead to the generation of secondary metabolites, many of which could compromise product safety to patients if not removed by the purification process. Understanding the critical control attributes of all bioprocess unit operations for the effective, reproducible, and low-risk manufacture and purification of the product is a fundamental requirement. Aseptic operations, such as axenic cell culture operations, require a process zone free of bioburden. Where possible, a closed system should be used for aseptic operations. Where closed systems cannot be used, only environments or process zones that meet the bioburden and particulate criteria of Grade A environments are appropriate for open aseptic operations. Technological advances and improved process understanding since the publication of the second edition of this Guide have enabled the widespread implementation of closed bioprocess systems in biopharmaceutical manufacturing. The improvements in SUT and a better understanding of CIP and SIP of traditional multi-use systems allow biopharmaceutical manufacturers to effectively isolate their process zones from the manufacturing suite environment housing these systems. Process closure involves two main steps: (1) removal of latent bioburden and contaminants in the process zone to a level appropriate for low bioburden or sterile/aseptic operations and (2) mitigation of ingress of contaminants post-closure and during process contact. CIP, SIP, and/or other sanitization operations (e.g., irradiation of single-use systems) can reduce latent bioburden 3 to 6 log (or more) prior to process contact. Furthermore, effectively closed and integrated systems are designed to mitigate the risk of ingress of environmental contaminants into the process zone after process contact. This protection far exceeds the potential benefit of moving a bioprocess unit operation into a classified cleanroom. For instance, moving a process from a Grade D environment into a Grade B environment only improves the bioburden load by 1.3 log/m³. Every possible effort should be made to maintain a bacteriostatic state for all bioprocess unit operations. Maintaining a low bioburden state in downstream operations provides a higher level of assurance in manufacturing a product of high quality. Manufacturers should define bioburden specification for all low bioburden unit operations based on CQA and CPPs. The United States Pharmacopeia (USP) [72], European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) [73], Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) [74] has a specification of <10 CFU/100 mL for WFI. In most cases, modern aqueous unit operations (following host cell removal) should be able to meet or exceed this specification. Where process unit operations are open, a cleanroom of a classification commensurate with the risk to process should be used to house the open operation. Typically, a Grade C environment is suggested. Chapters 7 and 8 will provide information on how to achieve and maintain the classification of cleanrooms housing

Where process unit operations are open, a cleanroom of a classification commensurate with the risk to process should be used to house the open operation. Typically, a Grade C environment is suggested. Chapters 7 and 8 will provide information on how to achieve and maintain the classification of cleanrooms housing open bioprocess unit operations. Manufacturing suites in which the product might be exposed need to be designed for rigorous cleaning procedures, including disinfectants capable of removing/denaturing/inactivating both environmental contaminants and the product itself. If the product is itself a live organism, the premises must be able to withstand fumigation with VHP or equivalent. This is particularly important with viral products because it is difficult to test for their absence by traditional swabbing methods. Multi-product facilities add another layer of complexity, but success is achievable. Product, equipment, people, material flows, and the related premises must ensure that after the validated inactivation stage, the risk of contamination with the producing organism is effectively eliminated. Similar to all pharmaceutical manufacturing, the equipment must be qualified, maintained, and calibrated to ensure its suitability for intended purpose as well as operational reliability. However, beyond operational performance, bioprocess equipment should also be validated for cleaning and sanitization procedures to ensure process streams maintain a bacteriostatic state and remain low in bioburden. The process is at risk from all sources of contamination within the environment including operators, equipment, materials, and activities. Part B depicts a closed bioreactor that has been appropriately cleaned and sterilized prior to inoculation. All additions to the closed system are made aseptically. Part C depicts an open operation performed in a closed environment. The isolator is closed by cleaning and VHP . All additions to the closed system are performed aseptically. From a contamination control perspective, performing a biopharmaceutical manufacturing process in a medium that is impervious to contamination, such as a bactericidal solvent, is ideal. However, most bioprocesses are aqueous based, requiring additional layers of protection. Closed systems provide the highest level of protection for the bioprocess against contaminating elements in the manufacturing environment. When processes cannot be closed, housing these operations in a closed environment such as a closed isolator is highly effective for process protection. When closed systems or closed environments are not practical, care must be taken to protect the environment, such as by housing the operation in a classified cleanroom. Page 76 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Figure 6.2: Layers of Protection to a Bioprocess 6.5 Mitigating the Risk of Contamination during Manufactur e of a Biopharmaceutical Drug Substance A comprehensive CCS is required to mitigate the risk of contamination of a biopharmaceutical API. Both latent contaminants and the potential ingress of contaminants into the process zone during manufacture of the API need to be considered in a bioburden control strategy. The key to understanding how to protect a product from contamination is summarized by the following three questions: 1.What latent contaminants are present in the process zone? 2.How can the risk of contaminants within the process zone be mitigated? 3.How can the risk of ingress of contaminants into the process zone be mitigated? By definition, only contaminants within the process zone can affect a process or product. The process zone is bound by a process zone boundary. In closed systems, the process zone is confined by the boundary of the equipment, whether the walls of a bioreactor or other bioprocessing equipment, including connected piping systems and system connections. The boundary could also be the interior of a cleaned and VHP-treated isolator. The process zone encompasses all components that could come into contact with the process or product. This includes the surfaces, liquids, and the atmosphere that potentially harbor contaminants that could directly contact the product during the processing activity. In closed systems, the process zone boundary separates the process zone from the manufacturing environment. In open process systems, the process zone includes the equipment as well as the manufacturing environment housing the equipment (see Figure 6.1). Controls must be in place to limit contaminants within the process zone, regardless of whether the process operations are open or closed. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright © 2024 International Society for Pharmaceutical Engineering. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 77 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities By closing a process the process zone is limited consequently limiting and/or more easily controlling the risks to the process. Closing a process can therefore help mitigate the risk of product contamination. Through process closure, contaminants in the manufacturing environment are effectively removed from the bounded process zone prior to product introduction. In closed systems, the process zone boundary blocks the ingress of contaminants from the manufacturing environment. For example, closed integral bioreactors effectively prevent the ingress of contaminants present in the manufacturing

process closure, contaminants in the manufacturing environment are effectively removed from the bounded process zone prior to product introduction. In closed systems, the process zone boundary blocks the ingress of contaminants from the manufacturing environment. For example, closed integral bioreactors effectively prevent the ingress of contaminants present in the manufacturing suite during aseptic cell culture operations. For this reason, modern bioreactors can operate aseptically in unclassified environments. To achieve this state, the bioreactor must be properly prepared before inoculation. For single-use bioreactors, this requires effective sterilization of the bioreactor bag (e.g., gamma irradiation), proper installation without compromise to the system integrity, and addition and inoculation operations that are all performed aseptically. For multi-use bioreactors, this also requires effective CIP followed by effective SIP operations. Following inoculation, the bioreactor must maintain its aseptic state by preventing the ingress of contaminants from the manufacturing environment for the duration of cell culture operation and potentially into subsequent process steps. In certain situations, not all unit operations within a bioprocess can be closed. Where operations cannot be closed, the process zone extends beyond the bioprocessing equipment. Open cell culture operations, such as the inoculation of open shake flasks in a BSC, illustrate this scenario. The HEPA-filtered airflow velocity created by the BSC is intended to protect the environment housing the open operation against contaminants from operators and the broader manufacturing area. The BSC is further protected by housing this equipment in a classified cleanroom with limits on viable and non-viable contaminants. Operators are gowned in presterilized gowns, And the BSC surface is manually cleaned and sanitized before use. Transitions into and out of the BSC involve spraying gloves, equipment, and materials using sanitizing agents such as ethanol to ensure protection. Furthermore, the number of people and operations within the manufacturing suite is limited to further protect the integrity of the BSC environment. The manufacturing suite is protected with airlocks to limit the volume of contaminants entering the suite through operators, raw materials, and equipment. This example exemplifies the complexity, difficulty and risk of protecting the Grade A environment that is required to mitigate the risk of contaminating aseptic processes. Only one deviation in the overall operation can potentially compromise the integrity of the process, with human error being the most likely failure. Manual risk mitigation measures are variable and less robust than automated CIP/SIP operations for removing contaminants from the process contact surfaces of closed bioprocess equipment. Removing the human element from a risk mitigation strategy results in a more robust and reliable approach. 6.6 Impact of Open Processing on Oper ational Choices As presented in Chapter 5, closed process operations are isolated from the environment. As such, these operations can typically be performed in a controlled non-classified environment appropriate for the proper performance of the equipment. Closed processing environments should be designed according to the drug substance manufacturing process and equipment requirements. Chapter 7 shows that the design of drug substance manufacturing facilities involves numerous factors and considerations, including aspects such as location, GMP flow, HVAC, and segregation. Open process operations are affected by the

equipment. Closed processing environments should be designed according to the drug substance manufacturing process and equipment requirements. Chapter 7 shows that the design of drug substance manufacturing facilities involves numerous factors and considerations, including aspects such as location, GMP flow, HVAC, and segregation. Open process operations are affected by the environment housing those operations. Open aseptic operations should be performed in environments that meet Grade A particulate and bioburden criteria. If the open aseptic operation is performed in a closed Grade A process zone, such as in a closed isolator, the environment housing this isolator does not require a Grade C or B classification; Grade D classification is adequate. However , if a BSC, Restricted Access Barrier System (RABS), or open isolator is used to provide the Grade A environment, the room housing these systems requires appropriate conditions and controls to ensure the integrity of the Grade A environment is maintained throughout the aseptic operation. This includes appropriate room classification, suitable gowning, effective BSC sanitization, appropriate BSC transitions, proper equipment adjacencies, controlled personnel movements, appropriate suite access transitions, etc. As stated previously, failure in any of these control systems can result in a breach of integrity of the Grade A conditions. Page 78 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Open low bioburden operations are also affected by the quality of the environment housing the open systems. This includes viable and non-viable contaminant levels within the environment, number of personnel and personnel movement, gowning quality, neighboring systems and operations, etc. Open processing requires appropriate protection to ensure consistent, repeatable, and reliable quality outputs. For typical open low bioburden bioprocesses, a Grade C environment is recommended. During open operations, the Grade C environment should be maintained at all times. Depending on the risk of the operation, such as in a growth-promoting condition, process closure (e.g., by sterile filtration or thermal sanitization) should be performed as early as possible to mitigate the risk of propagation of bioburden. For open controlled-condition operations, where there is low risk of propagation of bioburden, a Grade D (or even non-classified) environment may be adequate. Examples of controlled conditions include chemico-physical conditions such as the use of bacteriostatic or bactericidal solvents (e.g., alcohol-containing buffers), low or high pH conditions, low or high temperature conditions, and short duration (formerly called brief) operations. A risk assessment should be performed to qualify the appropriate conditions (see Figure 6.2). 6.7 Preparation and Assembly of Equipment for Closed Processing 6.7.1 Single-use Systems Single-use systems have become the choice of many drug substance manufacturers, especially for small scale operations (typically ≤ 2000 L cell culture volumes) and Contract Development and Manufacturing Organizations (CDMOs). The drivers for using single-use systems include speed to market due to simpler facility designs, lower process water consumption (no need for CIP or SIP), and mitigation of carryover contamination risk due to

small scale operations (typically ≤ 2000 L cell culture volumes) and Contract Development and Manufacturing Organizations (CDMOs). The drivers for using single-use systems include speed to market due to simpler facility designs, lower process water consumption (no need for CIP or SIP), and mitigation of carryover contamination risk due to replacement of product-contact surfaces between batches and no cleaning validation requirements. In addition, CDMOs prefer single-use systems because equipment functions as a consumable that is easier to quantify (financially) when compared to the expenses associated with cleaning and steaming operations required on multi-use systems. Single-use systems are typically fabricated in controlled conditions with low particulate and bioburden levels. After assembly of the closed system, the single-use system is packaged and sterilized (e.g., gamma-irradiated) prior to delivery to the drug manufacturer with a Certificate of Assurance that verifies its sterility and integrity. To maintain the integrity of single-use systems that are closed (note: single-use systems that are not closed require system closure after assembly), connections to other closed systems must be performed aseptically, either by using aseptic connecting devices or other aseptic connection methodologies that can be performed in unclassified environments. This applies to single-use systems used both in aseptic operations as well as in low bioburden operations. If the connection of one closed system to another closed system is not performed aseptically, the combined and integrated “super” system is no longer considered closed. Functional closure would need to be reestablished. As presented in Chapter 5, the functional closure can be achieved in a number of ways. 6.7.2 Multi-use Systems Multi-use systems are the traditional choice for drug substance manufacturing, especially for larger scale operations (> 4000 L cell culture volumes) and many unit operations where appropriate single-use options are limited, not available, or not practical. These systems have gained renewed interest since the COVID-19 pandemic due to supply chain challenges. Multi-use systems are designed to be used across multiple batches and products. Between operations, the equipment is cleaned/sanitized by CIP to mitigate the risk of carryover contamination and then may be further sanitized/sterilized (low bioburden/aseptic) to remove latent bioburden prior to subsequent product contact. To achieve adequate levels of cleaning and sanitization quality, multi-use bioprocessing equipment needs to be designed for effective CIP and, if required, SIP. The ASME BPE [62] provides equipment design standards as well as operational recommendations for achieving effective CIP and SIP. All multi-use bioprocessing equipment used in the manufacture of human therapeutic products should conform with the ASME BPE. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 79 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities CIP operations should be based on the TACT principle. The design of bioprocessing equipment should avoid dead legs, following the guidelines outlined in the ASME BPE [62]. Product-contact systems, equipment, components, and piping need to be designed to achieve adequate turbulence for consistent, reproducible, and effective cleaning. The choice of

CIP operations should be based on the TACT principle. The design of bioprocessing equipment should avoid dead legs, following the guidelines outlined in the ASME BPE [62]. Product-contact systems, equipment, components, and piping need to be designed to achieve adequate turbulence for consistent, reproducible, and effective cleaning. The choice of solvent for CIP is critical for the successful cleaning of multi-use bioprocessing systems. For the vast majority of CIP operations (e.g., support and downstream), CIP without caustic (e.g., WFI, Purified Water) is likely an appropriate solvent. For other difficult to CIP systems, such as bioreactors, a stronger caustic-based solvent is likely required. Other surfactants or cleaning agents may also be required for certain systems. For systems used in aseptic operations requiring SIP, bioprocessing equipment needs to be designed to meet the recommended parameters/conditions for sterility assurance as specified by the ASME BPE [62]. Additionally, the steam used must comply with the applicable compendial and regulatory requirements for steam quality. To achieve successful SIP, all points within the closed system’s SIP boundary must attain and maintain the specified temperature (e.g., 121°C or 130°C) for the specified duration (e.g., 15 to 30 minutes) under saturated steam conditions. The ASME BPE offers guidance on monitoring and controlling SIP operations to ensure their effectiveness. The recommended approach for SIP control includes maintaining all temperature points within the SIP boundary to within 2°C of the corresponding values on the saturated steam pressure/temperature table, for the full duration of the hold/dwell phase of SIP. This provides assurance that all product-contact surfaces undergo SIP under saturated steam conditions. This distinction is critical since it is not the temperature that kills microorganisms, but rather it is the energy of enthalpy when steam condenses that kills microorganisms. Following a successful SIP to close the bioprocessing system, systems conforming to ASME BPE are also designed to maintain positive pressure within the closed system. This serves to prevent a vacuum condition within the process zone, thus mitigating the risk of ingress of environmental contaminants prior to and during the process. Alternative non-thermal sanitization/sterilization agents and systems are also available for multi-use bioprocessing systems. These include methods such as irradiation and chemical agents (e.g., chlorine dioxide, ozone, hypochlorite, peracetic acid, etc.). It is important that all cleaning and sanitization agents and systems are compatible with the bioprocessing equipment, whether single-use or multi-use. 6.7.3 Operational Options f or Typical Aseptic Bioprocess Unit Operations The options for aseptic bioprocess unit operations are notably limited. To achieve the desired axenic culture, the media and systems need to be sterile prior to inoculation with a pure culture. Aseptic bioprocess unit operations should be performed in closed systems or in an environment that meets the bioburden and particulate criteria of Grade A. The vast majority of large scale cell culture operations employ closed systems. The exception might arise in the case of very large microbial fermentation operations where the culture produces high concentrations of antibiotics (or has antibiotics included in the media), creating a controlled-condition operation that is essentially impervious to environmental contamination. Options are available for closed small

culture operations employ closed systems. The exception might arise in the case of very large microbial fermentation operations where the culture produces high concentrations of antibiotics (or has antibiotics included in the media), creating a controlled-condition operation that is essentially impervious to environmental contamination. Options are available for closed small scale cell culture/fermentation operations. While most cell banks consist of small volumes (1–5 mL) of culture cryo-preserved in small vials, some cell culture–based drug substance manufacturers choose to develop high density (25–100 mL) cell banks that are cryo-stored in cryogenically-compatible bags that can be aseptically connected directly to a rocking motion single-use bioreactor or stirred tank bioreactor. These closed initial inoculation operations can be safely performed in a non-classified environment. Even when high density cell banking is not an option, some manufacturers choose to perform the initial open inoculation operation in a closed isolator, followed by subsequent cell expansion manipulations using closed shake flasks. These operations can be performed within the same closed processing environment used to house the closed stirred tank bioreactors. If a BSC (open) is used for critical open aseptic cell banking operations, it is recommended to have a minimum of Grade C, and possibly Grade B, background to mitigate the risk of compromising the environment within the BSC. While there is debate about the necessity of Grade D or C environments to house closed isolators, it is not a regulatory requirement for drug substance inoculum preparation operations. It is, however, essential for sterile drug product manufacturing. In sterile drug filling operations, the samples used to determine the quality of the overall batch may not always be representative of the batch. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Page 80 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Given the risk to patient, additional controls must be in place to provide the highest level of assurance that batch integrity is maintained. The inoculum prepared in a closed isolator will be transferred into a growth-promoting bioreactor pre-filled with medium. The bioreactor monitoring systems are highly effective at detecting a breach of culture integrity, especially after multiple days of cultivation under ideal growth conditions. Aseptic sampling of the culture pre-harvest, monitoring of parameters (dissolved oxygen, oxygen consumption, and pH), and batch fingerprinting all provide robust tools for detecting and diagnosing a possible contamination event. This in situ monitoring capability far exceeds the detection capability of the Grade A isolator chamber for detecting contamination in cell culture operations. Companies performing cell banking operations should perform a risk assessment to properly evaluate the choice between isolators and BSCs for establishing zero bioburden environments. A compromise in a cell bank’s integrity could result in months of delays in biological drug substance manufacturing operations. 6.7.4 Operational Options for T ypical Low Bioburden Bioprocess Unit Operations Many options are available for effective control of bioburden in low bioburden operations. This section presents examples that describe

environments. A compromise in a cell bank’s integrity could result in months of delays in biological drug substance manufacturing operations. 6.7.4 Operational Options for T ypical Low Bioburden Bioprocess Unit Operations Many options are available for effective control of bioburden in low bioburden operations. This section presents examples that describe how the approach for design, setup, and operation of a typical bioprocessing unit operation can impact the manufacturing suite needed to house this operation. 6.7.4.1 Weigh/Dispense and Solution Prepara tion Operations The primary sources of viable (and non-viable) contaminants in a solution preparation operation are often the dispensed solids themselves and the solvent into which the solids are dissolved or suspended. Therefore, the most important consideration is preventing the propagation of bioburden during these operations. This is done by mitigating the risk of production of secondary metabolites and endotoxins that could affect the process or ultimately product quality and patient safety. Prior to hydration, assuming appropriate packaging and storage, the risk of microbial proliferation is minimal. For this reason, it is recommended to dispense the solids in a clean environment, preferably under HEPA filtration with effective dust control and with operators donning appropriate gowns. Performing an appropriate cleaning of surfaces and tools between dispensing operations can mitigate the risk of mix-ups and crossover contamination. This environment should be monitored and controlled to a particulate and bioburden specification, but only under static conditions between dispensing operations, given that open powder handling can skew dynamic/active particulate monitoring data. Typically, dispensing powder within a well-designed downflow booth housed in a Grade D environment is a recommended practice. An example weigh/dispense downflow booth is shown in Figure 6.3. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 81 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities To mitigate the risk of microbial ingress and propagation during the hydration process, different approaches may be used. An example is the use of protected containers with sanitary fittings for sanitary connection to the preparation system (note: sanitary, not aseptic, as neither the powders nor the solvent are sterile). It is important that the mixing vessel be appropriately cleaned and sanitized prior to the addition of solvent and powders to keep latent bioburden to a minimum. The solution preparation should be controlled to limit microbial proliferation during the operation. This can be achieved in many ways such as temperature control, pH control, the order of component additions (i.e., some compounds promote growth more than others), and more. However, the most critical parameter to control is the duration of the operation. The duration of a solution preparation should always be limited. From the moment the first gram of powder is added to the solvent, a medium for potential microbial growth is created. Until the risk of latent viable contamination is removed (e.g., by sterile

control is the duration of the operation. The duration of a solution preparation should always be limited. From the moment the first gram of powder is added to the solvent, a medium for potential microbial growth is created. Until the risk of latent viable contamination is removed (e.g., by sterile filtration or thermal sterilization), the solution is at risk for microbial proliferation and endotoxin production. This concern is especially critical for cell culture media. Not all buffers used in bioprocessing are bacteriostatic or bactericidal. Solutions such as strong acid or base solutions are bacteriostatic and may be stored for long durations without sterile filtration. However , if used in aseptic operations, they will also need to be sterile filtered before use. A risk assessment should be performed for all solutions to determine the shelf life pre and post-sterilization, whether by sterile filtration or thermal sterilization. Some companies have validated the post-sterile filtration hold durations of certain buffers for periods exceeding 3 years. Numerous companies have sodium hydroxide distribution systems that are never sterile filtered and are directly used into low bioburden operations. With appropriate controls, distributed WFI contains very low bioburden and is frequently used in cleaning, sanitization, and system conditioning operations. However, when used in aseptic cell culture operations, WFI-based solutions need to be sterile filtered. Controlling the conditions of a solution preparation operation is critical to the success of these operations. When protected containers are used for powder additions, a Grade D environment is typically recommended. The use of protected containers also provides the added benefit of containing the spread of powder that occurs with open powder additions, thereby minimizing the amount of cleaning required between formulations. Most solutions formulated in bioprocessing operations are highly water soluble. Exceptions, such as cell culture and especially fermentation media, may contain silicone-based antifoam agents or lipids or other constituents that are not as soluble. For multi-use solution preparation systems, the majority of the formulations can be cleaned and sanitized by CIP without caustic. Typically, these ASME BPE [62] conforming systems with no dead legs can be cleaned using appropriate compendial process water (e.g., WFI or Purified W ater). This process results in a reduction of residual solution to less than 1 ppm and likely less than 1 ppb within a relatively short CIP cycle. In cases where more complex solutions containing lipids, minerals, or other difficult to clean components are formulated, a more aggressive cleaning solvent or series of solvents may be required to achieve the better than 1 ppm residue. For larger formulations, the use of protected containers for powder addition is not always possible or practical. Remote powder additions may be an option. In-line mixers with direct powder transfer capability are available, allowing for in-line powder additions from a different location than the top of the mixing vessel. These in-line mixer/ blenders can even be located in a room adjacent to the room housing the mixing vessel. In this scenario, the room housing the in-line mixer where the open powder addition occurs could be a classified cleanroom (e.g., Grade D or

than the top of the mixing vessel. These in-line mixer/ blenders can even be located in a room adjacent to the room housing the mixing vessel. The solution preparation vessel receiving the solution components could be housed in a non-classified environment, as the product-contact surfaces are not exposed to the environment after cleaning and sanitization operations prior to and during solution preparation. As stated previously, closed production bioreactors are the primary option for cell culture-based manufacturing. While clarification operations can be initiated directly from the bioreactor, many manufacturers prefer to turn over the bioreactor for subsequent batches or products as quickly as possible. For this reason, the transfer of the axenic culture into a designated harvest vessel is often preferred. A traditional approach involves lowering the temperature of the broth in the harvest vessel to temperatures below 10°C to create a bacteriostatic state. Effective CIP, occasionally coupled with SIP, of the harvest vessel and transfer piping prior to the transfer helps to minimize latent bioburden. Once sufficiently chilled, the broth is transferred into a clean vessel used to supply the clarifying centrifuge. In this setup, the clarification operation is considered open during harvest since the environment could potentially influence the bioburden quality of the product stream.

Page 84 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities cooling of the broth, along with the duration of the operation and subsequent hold, are controlled factors that could influence the impact of the open operation. Such operations typically take place in a Grade C environment to limit the level of potential environmental contaminants. To eliminate the influence of the environment on the quality of the harvest, an alternative approach involves using a sterile vessel for harvest (e.g., gamma-irradiated Single-Use Mixer (SUM) or SIPed multi-use vessel) and aseptically transferring the bioreactor contents into the harvest vessel. This constitutes a closed operation that could be performed in a non-classified environment. The duration of this harvest hold would only be limited by the stability of the product and not by latent bioburden. Following transfer of the axenic cell culture into the harvest vessel, many options for clarification can be considered. An informal survey of multi-use centrifuge suppliers indicated that half of the workhorse disk stack centrifuges sold in the biopharmaceutical industry are CIP-capable and SIP-capable (all are CIP-capable). However, further surveying of drug manufacturers using disk stack centrifuges for cell culture clarification operations showed that SIP is utilized infrequently between batches. SIP is only sometimes used after maintenance operations and occasionally between product campaigns. If the centrifuge is fed from an open source, the value of SIP is limited, except to minimize the level of latent bioburden on the centrifuge. Very low bioburden levels on the centrifuge can already be achieved by CIP only. In closed systems, SIP of the centrifuge, transfer lines, and clarified supernatant receiver vessel could maintain process closure. As an alternative to SIP-capable multi-use centrifuges, single-use continuous discharge centrifuges using gamma-irradiated flow paths and receiver SUM could also be used. As depicted in Figures 6.5 and 6.6, contemporary large scale/high cell density mAb clarification operations (> 2000 L) combine the benefits of centrifugation with depth filtration for higher clarification efficiency. Centrifugation canbe sized, designed, and operated to achieve a high level of clarification (90–98% cell/cell debris removal) at nearlyunlimited capacity. Depth filters can achieve > 99% clarification, albeit at limited capacity. By combining centrifugationwith depth filtration, the depth filter surface area required to achieve > 99% clarification is reduced significantly , potentially up to a 20-fold decrease. As of the publication of this Guide, sterile gamma-irradiated options for depth filters are not available. For this reason, the material harvested from the bioreactor is exposed to contaminants on the depth filter . To mitigate the risk of impact on product quality, depth

potentially up to a 20-fold decrease. As of the publication of this Guide, sterile gamma-irradiated options for depth filters are not available. For this reason, the material harvested from the bioreactor is exposed to contaminants on the depth filter . To mitigate the risk of impact on product quality, depth filters should always be conditioned prior to application of the product stream. Several strategies can be used for this conditioning step, including the use of cleaning/sanitizing agents, hot WFI, sterile buffers, etc. applied at temperatures, concentrations, velocities, and pressures that are compatible with the depth filtration media prior to product contact. The choice of strategy should be based on risk assessment and validation of the conditioning operation. The conditioning operation needs to reduce the contaminating agents (e.g., bioburden, leachables, extractables, and other chemical and particulate contaminants) to manageable and acceptable levels in accordance with the risk assessment. Along with the conditioning and preparation of both depth filters and centrifuges, it is possible to design these systems to mitigate the risk of ingress of bioburden and other contaminants from the environment. This constitutes system closure (note: closed but not necessarily aseptic). Therefore, with the appropriate system design, assembly, and pre-use operations, controlled clarification operations can be performed safely in non-classified environments. To further mitigate the risk and impact of contamination during clarification operations, the centrifuge and depth filters are typically coupled to a sterile filtration step. By sterile filtering the depth filter filtrate into a sterile gamma-irradiated SUM or SIPed stainless steel vessel, the overall process is closed. The sterile filtrate is “cell free” for downstream purification operations. By integrating the clarification to sterile filtration, the centrifuge can be considered a “pump” and the depth filter a “pre-filter”, where exposure of the product to these systems is of extremely short duration and carries minimal probable impact. This impact can be verified by microbial and endotoxin testing. It is important to note that the use of surge vessels between the bioreactor, centrifuge, depth filter, or sterile filter could extend the duration of the exposure beyond being considered short. If surge vessels are used, their design and use should be carefully evaluated and monitored. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 86 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Chromatography resin or media is typically supplied in a bacteriostatic or bactericidal solution (e.g., sodium hydroxide, ethanol, benzyl alcohol). This media is supplied in drums. The storage solution is removed in an open decanting operation and replaced with a slurry or packing buffer. In an open operation, one or multiple drums of media are then transferred into a slurry vessel and the pooled media is slurried. The suspended media is transferred to a clean and assembled column, typically using an automated column packing process. Given the need for open manipulations, column preparation operations are typically performed in a classified cleanroom (e.g., Grade C). As an alternative, it is common for drug substance manufacturers to use pre-packed columns (typically ≤ 45 cm in diameter , though columns up to 80 cm are available) that are supplied sanitized with certification. In order to mitigate the risk of latent bioburden or the risk of ingress of environmental contaminants during operations, design features should be in place and operations performed, prior to product contact. Chromatography operations often have long durations, often exceeding 24 hours. These operations are also often typically performed in conditions that support microbial growth (e.g., growth media, pH 7, temperatures of 18–37°C). For this reason, ingress of bioburden should be prevented and the level of latent bioburden should be minimized. As previously stated, the column is typically the most vulnerable point for latent contamination. The column needs to be designed for sanitary connections and system sanitization. Figure 6.9 illustrates columns with flow bypass options. Figure 6.9: Pre-packed and Pack-in-house Chromatography Columns with Bypass Option The bypass is essential for appropriate closure. The valve arrays depicted on the inlet and outlet of the column allow for systematic closure of a) the chromatography system initially, followed by b) sanitization of the system inclusive of the column media. Columns should be supplied to the processing area in this configuration, with the inlet and outlet valves nearest the column in the closed position. This valve array eliminates the need to open the system to attach the column to the chromatography skid. When the column is attached to the chromatography skid, the skid is initially CIPed at the appropriate velocity, chemical concentration, temperature, and duration (TACT) to effectively remove latent contaminants in the chromatography piping systems, including the column connection points. As stated in Section 5.5, contaminant levels

to the chromatography skid. When the column is attached to the chromatography skid, the skid is initially CIPed at the appropriate velocity, chemical concentration, temperature, and duration (TACT) to effectively remove latent contaminants in the chromatography piping systems, including the column connection points. As stated in Section 5.5, contaminant levels can be reduced by 3 log or more when applying the appropriate CIP conditions. In an ASME BPE [62] conforming system (without dead legs), achieving a 5 log reduction should be feasible. This level of reduction is typically adequate for low bioburden operations and surpasses the improvement attainable by moving the chromatography operation from a Grade D to a Grade B environment, even without the pre-CIP operation. After completion of the CIP for the piping system, the bypass is closed, and a subsequent sanitization of the system including the column media is performed to remove any latent contaminants that are potentially in the column media. It is important to note that sanitization of the column cannot be performed at the velocities that are specified for the chromatography piping system. This distinction is why the pre-CIP operation for the piping is performed prior to this step. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved.
