ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 87 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Many buffer and product feeds are required for a chromatography operation. As previously shown in Figure 6.7, these feeds could include: •Sanitization buffer •Equilibration buffer •Product load •Wash buffer •Elution buffer •HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) solutions •Dilution solution •Regeneration buffer •Storage buffer All of these feeds could contribute to the ingress of bioburden into a chromatography operation. To mitigate this risk, chromatography systems are typically configured with pre-column sterile filters. However , where the buffer or solution is not bacteriostatic and are stored for long durations, sterile filtration of the formulated solution or product into a sterile hold vessel is highly recommended. Additional considerations for chromatography operations are as follows: •All connections to the chromatography system should be of sanitary design with post-connection CIP capability or aseptically connected. •If the chromatography eluate is not processed immediately, it should be sterile filtered prior to holding for long durations. •If the chromatography system is compatible with elevated temperatures, SIP could be used to sanitize the chromatography skid while in bypass mode, allowing for aseptic connection of the pre-sanitized column to the skid. 6.8 Monitoring Operations Biopharmaceutical manufacturers are responsible for ensuring the safety of their products. This is achieved through a process that is robust, reliable, repeatable, and consistent. Measures must be taken to prevent, eliminate, and/or appropriately mitigate process risks that could impact quality. The CQAs of the product need to be understood. CPPs need to be developed, defined, controlled, and monitored. The critical limits for each CPP need to be established and need to be controlled to within the established limits during manufacturing. It is useful for improved understanding of bioprocessing operations. This information can be digitized and used to expedite decision-making and increase the consistency of process streams to improve production and increase yields. PAT provides continuous monitoring and control of processes that demonstrate the quality of the product. When PAT is used during product development stages, it aids in attaining earlier enhanced process knowledge (QbD), facilitates improved regulatory submissions, and streamlines technology transfer to clinical and commercial operations. As an example of the evolution of systems monitoring, consider the development in monitoring of WFI and Purified Water systems. In the 1980s, only 10% of WFI and Purified Water systems incorporated Total Organic Carbon (TOC) measurement, whereas TOC monitoring is now standard practice in all WFI and Purified Water systems. It is considered unacceptable to not install it. These systems monitor parameters such as TOC, conductivity, and temperature, providing online data and trends than can preemptively signal potential issues through trend analysis, alert levels, and critical alarms. 6.8.2 Capacity, Scheduling, and Maintenance 6.8.2.1 Capacity and Scheduling Capacity refers to the maximum achievable production of a plant. Capacity for biopharmaceuticals is determined by various parameters such as the cycle time of the production bioreactors, titer, and downstream processes. Bottlenecks need to be identified and resolved to increase capacity . The throughput of a facility can be dependent on the mix of products produced in downstream processes, as these are usually specific to each product. For multi-product facilities, the downtime for changeover can consume operational time, potentially impacting annual output. Thus, product changeovers need to be efficient and quick. Scheduling impacts facility capacity. Operational models should be used to optimize a facility for specified needs and changes to the product mix or other constraints. Facility bottlenecks and capacity limitations should be identified and addressed. Support functions may be some of the first bottlenecks encountered and should be included in operational models. Support functions may include: Buffer and media preparation and supply • CIP • SIP • Critical utilities, e.g., WFI or Purified Water Modeling tools (such as digital twin) and scheduling software can be useful in determining the optimal routine pace of production, which can contribute to the balanced allocation

models. Support functions may include: Buffer and media preparation and supply • CIP • SIP • Critical utilities, e.g., WFI or Purified Water Modeling tools (such as digital twin) and scheduling software can be useful in determining the optimal routine pace of production, which can contribute to the balanced allocation resources. A predictable routine provides consistency to operations (e.g., planning for capture chromatography to consistently start on the same day and shift) as well as allowing for an efficient routine to be established. This predictability not only supports incremental improvements but also quality stability which can help to minimize risk. Simulation and data analysis can help accelerate technology transfer from development to full scale manufacturing. Modeling also can be used to guide development when making decisions about processing strategies, e.g., a reduction in a buffer. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 89 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 6.8.2.2 Operability and Maintainability Manufacturing, quality, maintenance, operational technology, health and safety, and other operational functions need to be involved early in the facility and process design stages and in the definition of the user requirements. It is essential that adequate space be provided for equipment and instrument access, removal, and maintenance, e.g.: •Sensors that need to be calibrated •Valves, motors, filters, etc. Any specific requirements for portable and non-dedicated equipment also need to be accounted for . Space should be provided for storage, clean-out-of-place, and steaming where required. Equipment status and usage history should be collected and readily available; the use of bar-coded labeling technology or QR code alongside electronic usage and maintenance records may be beneficial. Using standardized and modular facility designs can help to reduce errors and improve cross training, particularly when similar processes are implemented across multiple facilities. Standardization should be balanced against opportunities for improvements in technology or equipment design. Mirror image designs should be avoided, as this approach can lead to confusion and operator errors due to the device locations. Prepare for operational upsets (excursions) in advance and establish recovery procedures. Recovery procedures should include: •A structured investigation to determine the possible impact of the upset on product quality •Identification of root cause and assignment of associated corrective actions •Documentation of the investigation and decisions •Follow-up to ensure completion of agreed upon actions and effectiveness checks, as necessary Maintenance plays a critical role in operations as it can impact the quality and performance of biopharmaceutical processes. The shift towards maintenance excellence programs emphasizes performance-based maintenance activities over traditional time-based practices. For example: •Vibration monitoring tools can aid in predicting equipment failures in advance, allowing for planned shutdowns and maintenance before parts reach the point of failure. •Focusing on the replacement of process elastomers (hoses, diaphragms, gaskets) involves off-line studies to determine their life expectancy and relying on sufficient history to replace these components when appropriate,avoiding unnecessary costs and downtime associated with time-based replacement.

allowing for planned shutdowns and maintenance before parts reach the point of failure. •Focusing on the replacement of process elastomers (hoses, diaphragms, gaskets) involves off-line studies to determine their life expectancy and relying on sufficient history to replace these components when appropriate,avoiding unnecessary costs and downtime associated with time-based replacement. The use of connectedmachines provides data on the number of cycles performed by each element and allows for predictivereplacement of elastomers, decreasing operational costs and increasing efficiency . Equipment should be decontaminated and cleaned prior to opening. Page 90 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Options for phased operation shutdowns to allow for periodic maintenance should be considered during design. This could involve segregating operations appropriately or duplicating equipment to minimize the need for a complete shutdown. In general, closed systems can allow for increased maintenance without facility shutdowns. For example, instrument calibration can be performed when a system is available rather than waiting for a complete area or facility shutdown. In ballroom settings with non-classified spaces, the restrictions for access by maintenance technicians can be reduced, minimizing the time required to enter the processing area. Additionally, there might be a decreased requirement to wall-off (partition) parts of equipment, making more of the system available for troubleshooting or maintenance within the same space. Where shutdowns may be necessary, partial shutdowns rolling through the facility can be scheduled as the last batch of product moves through the process. Months of lost production required to bring a facility back up from a cold start should be avoided. For example, facilities may have multiple seed preparation spaces, each maintained separately to ensure an active seed culture is always maintained and available. Redundant generation systems for critical clean utilities, such as WFI and pure steam, can be provided; however , maintenance on facility distribution systems may require periodic shutdowns. Having redundant in-line critical instrumentation or the option to remove them for calibration while the system is operational is recommended. For further information, see the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Maintenance (Second Edition) [75]. 6.9 Supply Chain The drug substance bulk production and drug product fill finish operations may be situated at separate facilities and different geographical locations. The limited stability of biopharmaceutical products can add complexity to the product supply chain and drug product distribution. Bulk drug substances can

Maintenance (Second Edition) [75]. 6.9 Supply Chain The drug substance bulk production and drug product fill finish operations may be situated at separate facilities and different geographical locations. The limited stability of biopharmaceutical products can add complexity to the product supply chain and drug product distribution. Bulk drug substances can be frozen to extend its shelf life. Freeze-thaw technologies can be implemented using disposable bags or freeze-thaw vessels for larger volumes. Single-use systems may be preferred over freeze-thaw vessels as vessel management (shipping, cleaning, and steaming) can be more complex. Shipments should be closely monitored and controlled to ensure required temperatures are not exceeded. For further information, see the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Cold Chain Management [76]. Single-site production can simplify the supply chain as there is only one site to manage and only one facility to operate and maintain. This approach can provide decreased overhead costs. However, business continuity risks are increased with a single-site production facility, for example: Pandemics and various disasters (both natural and man-made) could disrupt the ability to supply the market. •Contamination issues, such as viral contaminants, can result in significant downtime. •Regulatory issues could delay release of the product to the market. •Labor issues have a higher potential to be disruptive. Organizations should establish a strategy to ensure continuity of product supply. Modular designs can facilitate quick duplication, replacement, or relocation of manufacturing areas, if needed, to address facility performance issues or changes in demand. Sharing capacity among various organizations can allow for a degree of shared risk in capital investment. Facility planning and investment often occur four to six years prior to the need for commercial output. When a product is delayed, discontinued, or exceeds planned demand, costly idle capacity might arise or a market shortage may develop. Employing a flexible design allows facilities to be repurposed for another product, e.g., under a contracted arrangement to manufacture product from another organization. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 93 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7 GMP Layout Approaches 7.1 Introduction This chapter focuses on the facility aspects of product protection and the specific requirements of CPPs and product CQAs on facility layouts. Facility layouts can be varied while still ensuring both patient safety and product quality . This chapter discusses approaches behind layout opportunities and clarifies how manufacturers can leverage new possibilities deriving from enhanced process understanding by using innovative tools and process closure to facilitate a preferred project solution. The result of the facility conceptualization phase should be a layout that aligns with the project goals while also addressing key project drivers, such as: •Product types and production capacity •Product CQAs and systems CPPs •Approaches for product protection, process closure, and product exposure •Determining the environmental grade of processing rooms based on product exposure to the immediate room environment •Segregation requirements that drive HVAC zoning and pressurization strategies •Production processes, scales, and technologies •Facility and process flows •Maintenance, cleaning, and finishes for facility and equipment •Hazards related to process, products, and intermediates, including biosafety requirements for vaccines, conjugates, potent compounds, and other hazards •The need for aseptic processing (refer to Annex 1 [7]) for drug substances that cannot be sterile filtered •Considerations for changes in product, process, and facility over time, including: -Flexibility -Adaptability -Expandability -Change of purpose (e.g., introducing new products, platforms, or production scales) -Evolving regulatory requirements -Evolving operational requirements -Code compliance, life safety, and ergonomic aspects -Business and economic factors For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Page 94 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The documents that can be assembled before commencing the design phase include: •Production and operational philosophy •User Requirement Brief (URB) that summarizes the production and facility requirements •Process description •Process Block Flow Diagrams (BFDs) •Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs) •Process Closure Analysis •Equipment list •Production schedule •Preliminary material balance •Stated regulatory basis 7.2 Good Manufacturing Practices GMP regulation “provides for systems that assure proper design, monitoring, and control of manufacturing process and facilities.” [77] These guidelines have been developed to safeguard the manufacturing process, the product, and, ultimately, the patient. They include recommendations for all aspects of drug and drug substance manufacturing, ranging from material handling to facility design.

for systems that assure proper design, monitoring, and control of manufacturing process and facilities.” [77] These guidelines have been developed to safeguard the manufacturing process, the product, and, ultimately, the patient. They include recommendations for all aspects of drug and drug substance manufacturing, ranging from material handling to facility design. The guidelines are not prescriptive by design, allowing drug and drug substance manufacturers to develop innovative process and facility designs. The ultimate goal is to design facilities with appropriate environmental controls, process controls, facility flows, etc. that will effectively mitigate the risk of contamination of the manufactured products. This goal is especially important for manufacturing processes that include open operations. A common mistake made in modern facility design is when the designer reads the GMP guidelines as “standards” and applies the same standards to all manufacturing environments, regardless of specific process requirements. Improvements in bioprocessing equipment design have resulted in the ability to isolate the process zone (as defined in Chapters 5 and 6) from the manufacturing environment in nearly all operations used in biopharmaceutical drug substance manufacturing. Recent regulatory guidelines [7] encourage drug substance manufacturers to adopt closed systems and operations as the most effective means of protecting the process and product from environmental contaminants, thereby protecting the patient. For this reason, the industry is transitioning from traditional facilities based on open processing to facilities based on closed process unit operations, which transfers product protection away from the manufacturing environment to the equipment bounding the closed process zone. Closed systems provide product protection and physical segregation from the manufacturing suite environment, including personnel and neighboring activities and systems sharing that environment. The application of traditional GMP strategies, originally implemented for open processing, may no longer be necessary or add value to a facility based on closed processing product protection. This chapter presents a GMP application gradation that considers the degree of product exposure and risk to the patient. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 95 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7.3 Basics of Product CQAs and Sy stem CPPs A bioprocess unit operation typically requires control over many integrated sub-systems. System CPPs are designed to ensure that product CQAs are achieved and maintained. The system CPPs are process variables that must be controlled within defined parameters/ranges in order to support the associated CQA that they are aligned with. CPPs are not equipment design attributes or components. The environmental CPPs are particulates and viables in the immediate room environment. Closed system CPPs do not determine the room environmental grade. The degree of product exposure to the room environmental CPPs does determine the room grade. The environmental quality of a manufacturing suite, as indicated by its cleanroom classification, is designed to ensure control of viable and non-viable particulate limits in an effort to limit the potential exposure of products to these environmental contaminants. Such exposure can occur

room environmental CPPs does determine the room grade. The environmental quality of a manufacturing suite, as indicated by its cleanroom classification, is designed to ensure control of viable and non-viable particulate limits in an effort to limit the potential exposure of products to these environmental contaminants. Such exposure can occur during open operations such as open processing, additions, sampling, cleaning, or sub-system connections. In the context of open operations, environmental quality constitutes a CPP. Many other CPPs impact the success or failure of a given operation, most of which are not environmentally based. For example, a successful cell culture operation in a bioreactor requires the control of numerous CPPs (as illustrated in Figure 7.1) including contamination control (other than from the environment), pH control, temperature control, dissolved oxygen control, mixing control, and CIP and SIP procedures. These other CPPs are not influenced or affected by the quality of the environment. Even a classified cleanroom, including those meeting Grade A criteria, cannot prevent contamination of a bioreactor from a contaminated feed. In processes that are not closed, the product is exposed to the surrounding manufacturing environment. Both engineering and environmental controls are therefore required on the basis of QRM principles. These QRM principles should take into account the principles and requirements from relevant sections of Annex 1 [7] when selecting environmental classification cascades and associated controls. For products exposed to the environment, the environmental conditions are a CPP , and viable and non-viable particulate levels need to be controlled and monitored. The HVAC system is commissioned, and the room environment is qualified (validated). Specific process requirements dictate the appropriate environment for drug substance manufacturing operations. However, once determined, Grades A, B, C, and D must conform to their respective classification specifications, whether the suites are used for drug product or drug substance manufacturing. Grade C remains Grade C. Strategies for airlock, pressurization, Air Handling Unit (AHU) zoning, etc. should be consistent with the guidelines. An exception may arise in the monitoring of Grade A, where drug substance manufacturing often employs more advanced process monitoring methods compared to drug product filling operations. Note: The reference to Annex 1 [7] refers only to the environmental grade system focusing on particulates and viables as described in items 4.27 to 4.31, and not to the full Annex 1 document. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 97 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7.4 Environmental Grades The four grades of cleanroom/zone are defined in Annex 1 [7] for the manufacture of sterile products. However, as stated in the Scope section of Annex 1: “some of the principles and guidances, such as […] cleanroom classification […] may be used to support the manufacture of other products that are not intended to be sterile such as […] low bioburden biological intermediates, but where the control and reduction of microbial, particulate and endotoxin/pyrogen contamination is considered important.” For the manufacture of drug substances, the decision regarding cleanroom classification should be based on risk assessment and justified in the CCS. A summary of the four cleanroom/zone grades as described in Annex 1 [7] is as follows: •Grade A – The critical zone for high-risk operations, such as aseptic processing. •Grade B – For aseptic preparation and filling, often serving as the background cleanroom for Grade A (when not an isolator). •Grade C and D – These cleanrooms are utilized for less critical stages in the manufacture of aseptically filled sterile products or as background environments for isolators. They can also be used for the preparation/filling of terminally sterilized products. •Grade D – The minimal background requirement for closed isolators (Annex 1 Section 4.20-i-a). In addition to these cleanroom/zone grades, unclassified spaces with varying levels of controls can be utilized for drug substance manufacturing, as based on risk assessment and justified in the CCS. These spaces include: •CNC with monitoring – For closed processing with monitoring levels established by the production company and based on a risk assessment •Unclassified without monitoring – For closed processing •Exterior – For closed processing outdoors with appropriate controls The assignment of an environmental level to each unit operation is determined through a risk assessment and QRM principles as outlined in Chapter 3. The facility design can accommodate various environmental levels. For example, if a

monitoring – For closed processing •Exterior – For closed processing outdoors with appropriate controls The assignment of an environmental level to each unit operation is determined through a risk assessment and QRM principles as outlined in Chapter 3. The facility design can accommodate various environmental levels. For example, if a process is assigned to a Grade B aseptic processing environment, it involves high product exposure and contamination risk. In such cases, an appropriate airlock approach with supporting facility flows is necessary to reduce the ingress of environmental contaminants into the cleanroom, which could contaminate an exposed product. Annex 1 [7] provides guidance for Grade B aseptic processing: •Annex 1 Section 4.11: The transfer of material, equipment, components, and waste into Grade A and B areas should follow a unidirectional process. If this is not possible, bi-directional time-based separation by procedureshould be considered and controls applied to avoid potential contamination of incoming items. •Annex 1 Section 4.12: Wherever possible, airlocks used for personnel movement into Grade A and B areas should be separated from those used for material management. Where this is not practical, time-basedseparation by procedure should be considered. Annex 1 does not specify unidirectional flow or the separation of personnel and material airlocks as requirements for Grade C and D areas. The use of unidirectional flows can be considered in the case of higher levels of biocontainment risks, or where they can improve organization and safety in high traffic areas. This is an example of the gradation application of GMP principles related to the room grade level and product protection. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 98 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7.5 Primary Sources of Product Contamina tion Potential sources of contamination are described in Chapter 4, Section 4.2.2. Facility design should address four of these potential sources of product contamination: •Raw materials •Crossover •Carryover •Environment (for manufacturing processes using open systems) – this includes all elements within the manufacturing environment including: -Personnel -Gowns -Equipment -Neighboring activities and operations -HVAC systems -Utility services -Access points The facility CCS used to address these contamination sources should be similar across all drug substance manufacturing schemes and platforms. However, for manufacturing processes using open systems, additional robust environmental controls are required. Contamination control aspects for closed systems include: •Raw Materials – The risk of contamination from ingredients used in solutions requires material testing and local protection at open handling locations. Consumables require proper wipe down and sanitization procedures.Operations for material preparation prior to introduction into a closed system should be appropriate. •Crossover – Although systems are closed, they may not be contained. The process could possibly contaminate the environment (e.g., breach), but the contamination of neighboring closed systems is effectively mitigated. The product is not at risk from the environment. Breaches of closed systems should be thoroughly investigated toconfirm there are no facility design implications. •Carryover –

they may not be contained. The process could possibly contaminate the environment (e.g., breach), but the contamination of neighboring closed systems is effectively mitigated. The product is not at risk from the environment. Breaches of closed systems should be thoroughly investigated toconfirm there are no facility design implications. •Carryover – Single-use closed systems pose no risk as they are not reused. Multi-use closed systems conforming to ASME BPE [62] are only at risk if cleaning and sanitization are inadequate, whether for aseptic orlow bioburden operations. In all cases, the environment does not constitute a risk and therefore no facility designconsiderations are required (assuming robust CIP/SIP operations and a robust bioprocess system design are inplace per ASME BPE). •Environment – There is no contamination concern as the product is not exposed to the room environment.The application of closed systems effectively mitigates the risk of potential contamination sources from both theenvironment and crossover. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 99 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Contamination control aspects for open systems (requiring additional controls) include: • Raw Material – The risk of contamination from ingredients used in solutions requires material testing and local protection at open handling locations. Consumables require proper wipe down and sanitization procedures. Environmental controls are required to limit the level of contaminants during weigh, dispense, and formulation operations. • Environment – Contamination can originate from the room environment or personnel, making environmental protection and proper gowning required. Contamination of systems and operations by the environment is inevitable; therefore, appropriate environmental controls, including HV AC and gowning, are required. • Crossover – Contamination that requires an exposed product and another contaminant to enter requires a proper segregation approach. Spills and aerosols generated by all operations in the vicinity can potentially affect neighboring systems and operations. Appropriate environmental and segregation controls are required. • Carryover – Contamination due to improper system preparation, such as manual equipment cleaning and sanitization between batches, Is a concern. Proper post-cleaning staging (often referred to as post-CIP hold times) is critical to mitigate the risk of latent bioburden proliferation. Understanding the source and impact of potential contamination for each unit operation is key prior to applying facility and equipment segregation concepts. 7.6 Segregation Application C oncepts Segregation concepts are part of the overall CCS. These concepts should address the sources of potential product contamination described in Section 7.5. Facility segregation concepts consist of four basic applications: • Physical (Spatial) – Physical segregation can take the form of a separate area, room, or a closed system. Process closure physically segregates the process zone (e.g., inside of a closed vessel such as a bioreactor) from the surrounding environment and neighboring systems. A closed system constitutes physical segregation. Multiple closed systems can coexist in a common ballroom, while open systems often require separate rooms, areas, HVAC systems, and sometimes buildings (e.g., for handling β-lactams)

zone (e.g., inside of a closed vessel such as a bioreactor) from the surrounding environment and neighboring systems. A closed system constitutes physical segregation. Multiple closed systems can coexist in a common ballroom, while open systems often require separate rooms, areas, HVAC systems, and sometimes buildings (e.g., for handling β-lactams) to be adequately segregated. Critical open process systems should normally be located in separate areas or rooms, potentially dividing open process steps into a series of separate processing cleanroom silos. To achieve true segregation of the environments surrounding these systems, these cleanrooms should be protected by airlocks. The concept of room pressure nesting and facility flows that are designed and arranged to mitigate the risk of compromise of the cleanroom environment, which could potentially lead to contamination of an open process, is used. An example of this concept is the implementation of dedicated ingress and egress personnel airlocks for unidirectional gowning and GMP flows for Grade B environments to mitigate the risk of crossover when handling infectious materials. • Environmental (Including Local Environmental Protection) – Environmental segregation involves the use of HEPA-filtered air to protect an exposed product and critical product-contact surfaces that cannot be closed. A typical application using environmental segregation is weigh/dispense operations, which include the handling of powders that can disperse easily during transfer from the supply container into a boat/kit/bag. By placing this operation in well-designed downflow booths with HEPA-filtered supply air and appropriate dust collection, the risk of dispersing powders outside of the downflow booth walls (crossover) is effectively mitigated. Additionally , proper cleaning between dispensing operations mitigates the risk of carryover . When appropriately designed downflow booths are configured and oriented (and where appropriate controls are in place), multiple weigh/dispense operations can be performed simultaneously within the same suite. Another example is the use of multiple isolators within a common ballroom for simultaneous critical aseptic operations while neighboring ongoing operations are taking place. Both of these examples could be su ccessfully implemented in Grade D environments. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 100 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Chronological (Temporal or Time-based) – Chronological segregation relies on time-based separation, where activities are separated by time, using the same physical area for more than one function. An example is campaigned production or a bi-directional Material Air Lock (MAL) with the ingress and egress operationsseparated by time. •Procedural (SOP-based) – Procedural segregation involves using defined methods as outlined in SOPs tocomplete a task. An example is an incoming MAL material wipe down procedure and concurrent gowning anddegowning in the same area controlled by SOPs. Facility design can apply these segregation applications differently for open processing compared to closed processing, and the facility’s CCS response may vary accordingly. Segregation application approaches for open systems include: •Physical – Application of separate areas or rooms to mitigate the risk of contamination of an exposed product,

by SOPs. Facility design can apply these segregation applications differently for open processing compared to closed processing, and the facility’s CCS response may vary accordingly. Segregation application approaches for open systems include: •Physical – Application of separate areas or rooms to mitigate the risk of contamination of an exposed product, through crossover or from the room environment. •Environmental – Establishing separate environmentally controlled zones and/or air handlers to reduce thepotential contamination of an exposed product via the room environment. •Temporal – Multiple functions using the same area but separated by time to reduce the potential crossovercontamination. •Procedural – Procedures implemented to correctly perform a task. Segregation application approaches for closed systems include: •Physical – Closed systems by definition are physically segregated, mitigating the need for separate areas orrooms. This can result in a production ballroom with multiple closed systems. •Environmental – Closed processing inherently provides protection from the room environment, mitigating theneed for environmental protection measures. •Temporal – Time-based separation is not needed to mitigate carryover between two closed operations. •Procedural – Implementation of procedures to ensure correct execution of tasks remains important for closedsystem operations. The use of closed system technologies greatly reduces the need for facility-based area or room segregation approaches. It is important to establish the connection between the potential contamination sources to the facility segregation approaches prior to the application of facility flows. 7.7 Facility and GMP Flows A GMP flow diagram serves as a graphical representation of travel paths overlaid onto a facility floor plan, depicting how the facility is intended to operate. These diagrams are valuable tools for organizing facility layouts. Typically, GMP flow diagrams include, but are not limited to, the following: •Personnel: Personnel flow diagrams demonstrate how personnel enter, circulate through the manufacturingarea, and depart. These diagrams can also indicate gowning levels and transitions according to environmentalgrade levels, and should be harmonized with proposed gowning protocols. Potential environmental andcrossover contamination issues can be quickly identified with these diagrams. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 101 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Raw Materials and Consumables: Material flow diagrams illustrate the movement of raw materials and consumables from the receiving area in the warehouse through QC sampling, weigh/dispense, and into theGMP manufacturing areas via appropriate MALs. This diagram can reference QC testing procedures andtransitional surface cleaning and sanitization (material wipe down) SOPs for the MALs. Raw material crossovercontamination sources can be quickly identified with these diagrams. •Product: Product flow diagrams depict the flow of product from the manufacturing area back to the warehouse.This diagram can identify points of contact with incoming raw materials, samples, and waste. •Samples: Sample flow diagrams illustrate the locations for sample collection, their movement, and transitionsfrom the manufacturing areas to QC. sample control stations may be included to ensure proper sampleidentification and labeling prior to exiting production. incoming raw materials, samples, and waste. •Samples: Sample flow diagrams illustrate the locations for sample collection, their movement, and transitionsfrom the manufacturing areas to QC. sample control stations may be included to ensure proper sampleidentification and labeling prior to exiting production. This diagram can reference sampling collection andtransport SOPs. Areas of potential mix-ups can be identified with these diagrams. •Equipment: Equipment flow diagrams identify the flow of multi-use equipment that are not CIPed, includingportable equipment and the location of parts washing, clean-out-of-place, autoclaving, and steam-out-of-place equipment. This diagram can reference cleaning SOPs for each situation. Areas of potential carryovercontamination can be identified with these diagrams. •Waste: Waste flow diagrams identify different categories of waste (e.g., solid, liquid, infectious, contaminated,hazardous, biological, inert) and their flow through the facility , including discharge outside the facility. This diagram can reference waste handling and transport SOPs, typically including waste control and containmentmethods to ensure that waste does not contaminate the facility or create crossover issues with other materialsor personnel. Higher biosafety level waste may require decontamination prior to exiting specified containmentareas. Crossover contamination sources can be identified with these diagrams. The pursuit for an effective and efficient production facility includes ensuring that the correct staff and materials reach the appropriate unit operation at the right time to meet production schedules. These concepts are addressed in Lean manufacturing principles. In regulated facilities, GMP flows must address potential sources of contamination, including environmental, crossover , carryover , and raw materials. In facilities based on closed processing, the use of closed systems effectively resolves environmental contamination and crossover concerns. For example, in the case of a ballroom housing closed systems, personnel, material, waste, and sample flows typically do not represent a challenge to the room environment or product. In manufacturing environments based on open operations, crossover sources can pose challenges to the product. Flow diagrams need to demonstrate appropriate levels of product protection. Also, open processes require sufficient layers of protection to mitigate risks to the product. This adds a level of criticality to the facility organization. Reducing the environmental challenge to the cleanroom involves implementing pressure and air locking cascades with the appropriate PALs, MALs, and SOPs to control viable and non-viable particulate levels. Crossover concerns need to be addressed through the appropriate design of PALs and MALs with the additional operational SOPs where product exposure occurs. 7.8 Production Corridor Concepts In a typical drug substance manufacturing process, unsoiled personnel and materials proceed from the locker room and warehouse, respectively, to the manufacturing area through a production corridor. Two distinct strategies are used in GMP facility design: •Single multipurpose production corridor used for all GMP flows including both clean supply flows and used/ soiled/waste flows. Page 102 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities • Dual corridor approach with a production supply corridor and a return corridor . This dual corridor strategy is intended to segregate clean supply GMP flows from used/soiled/waste flows. In a single corridor strategy, the default approach is to accommodate all facility flows within a single corridor. This means that raw materials, freshly gowned personnel, and clean equipment and components must share the corridor with personnel returning from the manufacturing spaces, as well as waste, soiled process equipment, and product. It is evident that measures need to be in place to maintain the integrity of the clean state of people, materials, and equipment about to enter the manufacturing areas. At a minimum, personnel and materials entering a manufacturing suite must not compromise the quality of the process or product. Closed systems by definition protect the process and product because, in closed processing, the environment constitutes a no impact system. The process is isolated from the surrounding environment, including personnel, materials, equipment, etc. As stated in Section 7.5, manufacturing areas that house open operations require a higher level of controls. For open operations, a classified cleanroom is required. In a single corridor approach, segregation can be applied based on the principles of closure, providing physical (spatial) segregation to the corridor and to other items in the corridor , thereby preventing crossover contamination. The following measures are typically specified to ensure that all materials and personnel are in a universal unsoiled state within the production corridor: • Material and dirty equipment transport SOPs should define their protection, closure, or containment. • Personnel entering a room with a higher classification should gown appropriately when entering the suite and then remove the protective gowning layer when exiting the suite to re-access the production corridor in an unsoiled state. This approach addresses crossover concerns in the corridor. • Temporal segregation can typically be used to segregate the movement of waste and used (soiled) equipment after product and sample movement is complete. A two-corridor approach can also be used to segregate flows that cannot be adequately protected or contained, possibly representing potential sources of contamination. The flow of raw materials and consumables can be segregated from the flow of waste and soiled components and equipment as follows: • A production supply corridor could support the following flows: - Personnel - Consumables and raw materials - Work-in-Progress (WIP) and product - Non-product-contact trash including gown packaging, used media and buffer bags, etc. - Samples (if non-infectious) • A return corridor could be designated for the flow of inadequately protected, closed, or contained product-contact items representing a risk to the “clean state integrity” of the supply corridor , including: - Soiled process equipment - Product-contacted solid waste - Biosafety or infectious solid or liquid waste - Occupational exposure risk chemical solid or liquid waste For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 105 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7.10 Airlock Concepts Airlocks are part of an environmental CCS. They are a GMP requirement [7] between room grades, designed to mitigate the risk of compromising the cleanroom classification during transitions of people and materials between rooms of different classification. Airlocks are also used for containment purposes. For the specifics regarding pressure cascades for airlocks, refer to Chapter 9 which focuses on concepts related to airlocks. Airlocks typically serve specific functions and can include the following types: •PAL (Personnel Airlock) for gowning •MAL (Material Airlock) for cleaning and sanitizing the outer surfaces of containers and equipment and for staging prior to entering a cleanroom •AL (Airlock) as a combined PAL and MAL •WAL (Waste Airlock) dedicated to waste flow •TAL (Temperature Airlock) as a 2–8°C anteroom for a walk-in -20°C freezer There are several common approaches to airlock classifications, and the proper selection should be the result of a risk assessment documented in the CCS. Airlocks should be considered as transition spaces between

(Waste Airlock) dedicated to waste flow •TAL (Temperature Airlock) as a 2–8°C anteroom for a walk-in -20°C freezer There are several common approaches to airlock classifications, and the proper selection should be the result of a risk assessment documented in the CCS. Airlocks should be considered as transition spaces between classified rooms, rather than classified rooms themselves. As such, airlocks need to act as a buffer between rooms of different classification and provide protection from to the cleaner environment (and possibly containment for hazards). This perspective on airlocks is aligned with regulations and needs to be supported by design practice. In order to discuss more complex airlocks, it is useful to understand the four basic airlock configurations, which serve as the building blocks of these complex airlock approaches: •Sequential •Split classification •Maximum classification •Minimum classification These building blocks, as shown in Figure 7.4, can be applied to transitions both into and out of cleanrooms. Note that the classifications shown are provided as examples only. These principles are applicable to any single-step classification change that is bridged by airlocks. If multiple steps are involved, it may require multiple iterations or combinations of these airlock configurations. Page 106 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Each of these configurations has strengths and weaknesses, making each more suitable for certain applications. A summary of the advantages and disadvantages of each configuration along with implementation considerations is provided in Sections 7.10.1 to 7.10.5, and will focus primarily on entry airlocks in line with a similar focus within regulatory guidance. 7.10.1 Sequential Airlock Configuration Sequential airlocks (shown in Figure 7.5) are a common, low-risk approach that is generally accepted by most regulatory authorities. While some may argue that having two spaces to make a single-step change in classification is a required configuration for entering a cleanroom, it is important to note that this configuration is strictly optional. The origins of this approach might be from early versions (2008 and earlier) of EudraLex Annex 1 which stated that “The final stage of the changing room should, in the at-rest state, be the same grade as the area into which it leads” [78]. The use of the terms “final stage of the changing room” and the ambiguity regarding whether “in the at-rest state” referred to the airlock or the room into which it leads led some designers to add an extra space at the same classification as the room being entered, to the lower classification (or unclassified) airlock, which was common practice at that time. Nonetheless, the sequential design still satisfies the 2022 Annex 1 requirement for classification and separation of gowning or cleaning steps: “4.12 Airlocks should be designed and used to provide physical separation and to minimize microbial and particle contamination of the different areas and should be

common practice at that time. Nonetheless, the sequential design still satisfies the 2022 Annex 1 requirement for classification and separation of gowning or cleaning steps: “4.12 Airlocks should be designed and used to provide physical separation and to minimize microbial and particle contamination of the different areas and should be present for material and personnel moving between different grades.” [7] The advantages and disadvantages to the sequential airlock configuration approach include: •Advantages -Excellent separation of gowning or cleaning steps -Activities that may release particulates can take place in the lower grade -Environmental Monitoring (EM) approach is clear -Lowest inspection risk •Disadvantages -Most expensive approach to build and maintain -Requires more space than other approaches -Gowning is exposed to the lower grade environment Figure 7.5: Sequential Airlock Configuration (with Example Classifications) 7.10.2 Split Classification Airlock Configura tion Split classification airlocks (shown in Figure 7.6) have been in use for about as long as sequential airlocks, and for similar reasons. It is a common, lower risk approach that is generally accepted by most regulatory authorities, but can be confusing due to the presence of two classifications in a single room. Achieving greater cleanliness at one end of the airlock requires a mechanical and architectural configuration that flushes the airlock effectively from end to end. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 107 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Time delays can also be used to achieve recovery at the cleaner end prior to opening the door (this time delay is recognized in the 2022 version of Annex 1 [7]). Split classification airlocks generally feature a line of demarcation or crossover bench between areas with different classification designations within the space. This one-room approach to making a single-step change in classification is aligned with regulatory requirements. The split classification design provides separation of gowning/cleaning steps into different areas with different classifications but does not provide a wall between activities. The advantages and disadvantages to the split classification airlock configuration approach include: •Advantages -Good separation of gowning or cleaning steps -Activities that may release particulates can take place in the lower grade -EM approach is reasonably clear -Mid-low inspection risk -Less expensive and less space required compared to sequential airlocks •Disadvantages -Not always clearly understood -Effectiveness depends on design -Provides less separation of cleaning and gowning steps than sequential airlocks -Gowning is exposed to the lower grade environment Figure 7.6: Split Classification Airlock Configuration (with Example Classifications) 7.10.3 Maximum Classification Airlock Configur ation Maximum classification airlocks (shown in Figure 7.7), which have also been in use for about as long as sequential airlocks, are based on an interpretation of the language used in Annex 1 [7]. This common low-risk approach is generally accepted by most regulatory authorities. However, it presents challenges on two fronts. Firstly, the entry of personnel or materials from the lower grade space may cause an influx of contamination

airlocks, are based on an interpretation of the language used in Annex 1 [7]. This common low-risk approach is generally accepted by most regulatory authorities. However, it presents challenges on two fronts. Firstly, the entry of personnel or materials from the lower grade space may cause an influx of contamination into the airlock that challenges its environmental control to meet the higher standard. Secondly , the application of gowning or cleaning activities that take place near the less clean end of the airlock can also pose a challenge to environmental control. For these reasons, maximum classification airlocks may require an EM program that recognizes different in-use limits at the two ends of the airlock. Time delays can also be used to achieve recovery in the airlock prior to opening the door at the cleaner end (this time delay is recognized in the 2022 version of Annex 1 [7]). Maximum classification airlocks also benefit from a flushing ventilation design, similar to split classification airlocks. Like split classification, they may also feature a line of demarcation or crossover bench between the more and less clean areas within the space. Using one room to make a single-step change in classification is aligned with regulatory requirements. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. This less common, moderate-risk approach for entry airlocks has been successfully used in some facilities. It aligns with a careful reading of the 2008 version of Annex 1, which stipulated that “51 … The final stage of the changing room should, in the at-rest state, be the same grade as the area into which it leads.” [78] Since the Grade C airlock shown in Figure 7.8 meets ISO 7 in the at-rest state and Grade B meets ISO 7 in the in-operation state, this configuration might satisfy the older Annex 1 requirements. However, the 2022 version of Annex 1 clarifies that the airlock must meet the requirements for both particulate and viables before the door to the cleaner space may be opened: “4.12 … The final stage of the airlock should, in the ‘at rest’ state, be

the older Annex 1 requirements. However, the 2022 version of Annex 1 clarifies that the airlock must meet the requirements for both particulate and viables before the door to the cleaner space may be opened: “4.12 … The final stage of the airlock should, in the ‘at rest’ state, be of the same cleanliness grade (viable and total particle) as the cleanroom into which it leads.” [7] Therefore, the minimum classification airlock would need to meet the higher grade room’ s limits for viables to be considered an appropriate approach. This is one reason why it is not commonly used for entry airlocks. Time delays might be used to satisfy this requirement, again per the 2022 Annex 1 which states: “4.13 … Where required to maintain area segregation, a time delay between the closing and opening of interlocked doors should be established.” [7] The advantages and disadvantages to the minimum classification airlock configuration approach include: • Advantages - Least expensive to build - Takes less space than sequential airlocks For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. In this arrangement, an airlock that is one classification less clean may serve as exit-only . This design may even be used to bridge to a two-step less clean space. Since the door to the less clean space is only opened when that space is at-rest and the direction of travel is out from the cleaner space, the risk of introducing particulate into the cleaner space is low. Again, this application benefits from a flushing ventilation design and a line of demarcation or crossover bench within the airlock. Having only one room to make a two-step change in classification may draw some regulatory scrutiny, but is it aligns with regulatory guidance and has been used in many licensed facilities. Page 110 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Figure 7.9: Exit Airlock Configurations (with Example Classifications) The general expectation is that there is at least one step of entry airlock for each step of classification change between rooms. This expectation is clearly stated in regulatory guidance: Annex 1 [7]: “4.12 … i. Personnel airlocks: Areas of increasing cleanliness used for entry of personnel (e.g. from the grade D area to the grade C area to the grade B area).” WHO Technical Report Series No. 961 Annex 6 [79]: “11.7 … There should not be a change of more than one grade between airlocks or passages and changing rooms, i.e. a Grade D passage can lead to a Grade C airlock, which leads to a Grade B changing room, which leads to a Grade B clean room.” 7.10.6 Airlock Pressurization Schemes As for pressurization, the basic schemes are generally named per a paper by Manuel del V alle [80], as illustrated in Figure 7.10. Figure 7.10: Airlock Pressurization Schemes The cascade method is the classical pressurization approach, with air flowing from cleaner to less clean areas. The bubble and sink methods are used to provide containment with contamination control (see Chapter 9 for a more thorough discussion of pressurization approaches). These pressurization methods can be combined in various ways, in arrays of 2, 3, 4, or more steps. The number of steps and scheme for entry and exit do not necessarily need to match, and this can depend on the associated risks. Combining classification approaches with pressurization strategies, along with procedures for gowning, cleaning, decontamination, and degowning that are tailored to the facility’ s specific needs and risk profiles, yields an airlock scheme as part of the CCS. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 113 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7.11 Contract Production Considerations Contract Manufacturing Organizations (CMOs) may have business drivers that can influence and impact the facility layout. These can include: •Alignment with their clients’ facility design standards or expectations •Design to optimize cost per kilogram of product manufactured •Design to optimize cost per batch •Design to tailor and dedicate manufacturing suites to specific client preferences while operated by CMO employees •Design to tailor and dedicate manufacturing suites that are operated by CMO clients 7.12 Production Facility Configurations Production scale can drive the facility configuration. Three basic approaches and hybrids of these are: •Vertical Process Configuration: A vertical process equipment stack-up with large scale equipment penetratingfloors, resulting in a multi-level facility. Bioprocesses using bioreactors larger than 12,000 liters would beconsidered large scale. •High Bay Process Configuration: A high bay processing room with mid-scale equipment penetrating a platform within the room. This can apply for either a horizontal process or a vertical process with high bay rooms stackedon each other. Bioprocesses using bioreactors of 5,000 to 10,000 liters would be considered mid-scale. •Small Scale Horizontal Configuration: A lower ceiling height processing room with smaller process equipment and a horizontal process flow. Bioprocesses using bioreactors

for either a horizontal process or a vertical process with high bay rooms stackedon each other. Bioprocesses using bioreactors of 5,000 to 10,000 liters would be considered mid-scale. •Small Scale Horizontal Configuration: A lower ceiling height processing room with smaller process equipment and a horizontal process flow. Bioprocesses using bioreactors of 5,000 liters or less would be considered smallscale. Note that a ballroom could be used in all of these configurations. These configuration options are further described in the following sections. The purpose of these examples is to illustrate how facilities can be configured differently to facilitate various project drivers. There is no one-size-fits-all GMP layout. 7.12.1 Vertical Process Configuration A vertical equipment configuration used in a large scale process is depicted in Figure 7.13. The larger vessels penetrate the building floor level and are closely coordinated with the building floor-to-floor heights. Refer to Figure 7.14 for a vessel floor penetration example. Note that the mid-scale vessels typically do not have enough vessel side wall dimensions or height to penetrate a building floor level and do not have personnel access to the equipment from the lower level. The bottom of the vessel could be high up in the lower room and could require a platform to access the vessel. A high bay room approach for these can be a better solution. The smaller seed bioreactor vessels have a lower ceiling height requirement and are typically in a horizontal configuration, but the N-1 and N-2 bioreactors may need a platform, depending on the scale of the equipment. A ballroom example involves six 20,000 liter bioreactors and harvest vessels configured in upper and lower level ballrooms separated by a floor level, or with a floor section removed for visual connection and an open connecting stair, resulting in a single multi-level ballroom. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 115 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities A high bay room can facilitate a single upstream and downstream processing ballroom organized along a single platform. A potential equipment arrangement could be configured as follows: •Media preparation vessels are lined up, sitting through the platform. •Kitted media intermediate bulk containers are in front of the platform. •The N-2, N-1, and production bioreactors along with the harvest hold vessel are behind the media preparation vessels, on the same platform. The small scale seed reactors are situated on the platform. •The centrifuge, depth filters, and additional filtration skids are in front of this side of the platform, on the floor . •The platform continues to the downstream processing area. •The downstream product hold vessels align with the harvest hold and production reactors with the Chromatography 1 equipment adjacent to the harvest vessel. •The chromatography (chrom) skids, ultrafiltration/diafiltration (UF/DF) skids, viral filter , and bulk fill equipment are in front of the platform. The upstream and downstream process equipment are arranged on the same side of theplatform in the order of the process sequence. •The buffer hold vessels are on the platform behind the product vessels. •The buffer preparation vessels are behind the buffer hold vessels. •The kitted buffer intermediate bulk containers are in front of the platform on the floor . •Both media and buffer preparation areas are on the same side of the platform. Significant cost savings can be achieved through this approach, including a reduced footprint, lower environmental classification requirements, reduced gowning procedures, shorter (reduced) process piping, and the potential for super-skid equipment installation. Ltd., www.seruminstitute.com. 7.13 Mix-Up Prevention Preventing mix-ups is a GMP requirement (EudraLex Volume 4 Part II – items 4.11 and 4.13 [34]). It is focused primarily on implementing a product control strategy but can extend to an operational control strategy to improve quality, prevent production interruptions, eliminate failures, and increase production flexibility. The primary goal is to ensure the right materials are delivered to the right locations at the right time, all in a controlled state. Mix-up prevention methods can include: •Control by Area: For example, having a dedicated warehouse area for quarantine materials and a separate dedicated area for released materials. Alternatively, using individual pallet positions on the same pallet rack forquarantine and released materials. •Control by Identification: Employing various tools such as RFID technology, bar coding, color coding, and vendor-specific connectors to clearly identify and differentiate materials. •Control by an Automated Distribution System: Utilizing AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles) and AMR(Autonomous Mobile Robots) to eliminate inaccuracies or mistakes due to human error . Note that an AMR is a robot or cobot (collaborative robot) with an articulated arm mounted on an AGV. AGVs are increasingly incorporated into warehouse and packaging operations. Their application within production can yield significant benefits. Two examples are provided below . •Weigh/Dispense: Kitted Intermediate Bulk Containers (IBCs) are placed onto AGVs for travel to the media andbuffer preparation area. The AGV can meet with a lift that places the IBC above the vessel manway. When theoperation is completed, the IBC is returned

When theoperation is completed, the IBC is returned to the AGV, which then moves to a docking station for IBC washing.After the wash cycle, the AGV can move to a designated queue location before returning to the weigh/dispensearea when needed. These AGVs move sequentially to the preparation vessel filling station, where the media or buffer is transferred through asterile filter into the vessel. When filled, the AGVs move the materials to their use point. Incorporating an automatic movement system like AGVs not only reduces human error, but also improves overall safety and reduces the number of Full Time Employees (FTEs) required for manual material handling tasks. 7.14 Facility Aspects of Equipment and Facility Cleaning Items that require cleaning should be identified, and their cleaning protocols defined, to determine the quantity and type of cleaning equipment needed. There are two basic levels of facility cleaning: •Employing good general housekeeping practices for closed processing areas. •Implementing GMP level cleaning for open processing areas to mitigate the risk of carryover contamination. Adequate room cleaning and sanitizing should protect open processes from residual contaminants brought into the room by personnel, material, and equipment. The room finishes should be selected to be compatible withselected cleaning agents and conditions. In a multi-product concurrent production scenario in a closed processing ballroom, a catastrophic equipment failure that releases product into the room is considered a housekeeping issue. A leak from a product 1 system into the environment cannot contaminate an adjacent product 2 closed system. With closed processing, the product in system 2 is not at risk, allowing line 2 to continue production without interruption. Consequently , classifying the leak from system 1 as a housekeeping issue is appropriate, as it potentially affects product 1 and raises safety concerns. However, the risk of cross-contamination is essentially zero, with no impact on product 2. Open processes are typically segregated into silos or isolated manufacturing environments. A spill in a manufacturing environment using open systems is a significant event that interrupts production and can become a facility contamination issue that could migrate to contaminate another open system. This clearly raises a GMP concern. 7.15 Clinical and Commercial Production The FDA Guidance for Industry for Phase 1 Investigational Drugs was issued in July 2008 [81]. It defined GMP compliance as a step function with development and clinical phase 1 exempt from many GMPs, while requiring full GMP compliance for clinical phase 2 through commercial production. Clinical phase

Commercial Production The FDA Guidance for Industry for Phase 1 Investigational Drugs was issued in July 2008 [81]. It defined GMP compliance as a step function with development and clinical phase 1 exempt from many GMPs, while requiring full GMP compliance for clinical phase 2 through commercial production. Clinical phase 2 and 3 production and commercial production have the same GMP compliance requirements. Producing phase 2 drugs in a commercial facility is a matter of scale alignment, rather than being driven by regulatory segregation concerns. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 121 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 7.16 Vaccine and Biologic Hazard Facilities The US CDC, the US NIH, and individual national jurisdictions have established clear facility requirements for various levels of biosafety containment. GMPs apply to vaccine facilities. Biosafety requirements generally align with GMP regulatory requirements. However, a notable conflict arises in the requirement for biosafety BSL-1, BSL-2, and BSL-3 processing rooms which must maintain negative pressure in relation to the surrounding rooms, whereas non-BSL or General Laboratory Safety Practices (GLSP) facilities typically operate at positive pressure. This conflict can be resolved by using a double airlock approach involving a pressure sink and a bubble. It is important to note that the room’s environmental grade does not necessarily increase with the BSL level. A dedicated effort to close a BSL-3 process and segregate it from the room environment allows for a CNC environment. BSL-3 processes require secondary containment on a room basis. For closed processing, adding HEP As on the supply and return room air is a BSL-3 containment strategy and not a product protection requirement. Operator safety is very important in vaccine facilities; closed contained systems protect both the product and operators. The production ballroom concept is equally applicable to vaccine production, with the exception that it includes an additional equipment grouping known as the hot BSL zone, where live viruses or bacteria are handled. Figure 7.21 provides a comparison between traditional GLSP facilities, GLSP production often referred to as future facility or a ballroom, and a future facility concept with the BSL containment overlay . Adding the hot zone to the visual process graphic in Figure 7.2 can illustrate this separation. Whereas GLSP facilities often transition to single rooms with the application of fully closed systems, vaccine facilities have an additional requirement for BSL room segregation. An additional overlay can be added to reduce production interruptions. Where the virus active cell processing room is very large and involves many process steps, the room could be further subdivided to contain catastrophic spills into smaller cleanup zones. The benefit of smaller rooms is the reduced facility cleanup time. Figure 7.21: Comparative Facility Diagrams Conjugate processes are another example where safety is considered very important; closed systems protect both the product and operators. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 123 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 8 Architectural Design 8.1 Introduction This chapter provides insight into the many architectural issues that need to be integrated into a successful biopharmaceutical project. Readers will learn that the architectural design and delivery of a GMP biomanufacturing facility requires specialized knowledge and skills in numerous areas, including programming, building shell design, and the integration of regulatory requirements. Architects leading the core and shell design of a biomanufacturing facility must also understand the importance of integrating structural, mechanical, and utility system requirements along with the typical goals of a flexible and adaptable facility that will meet the manufacturer ’s long-term business needs. Building upon the GMP layout issues presented in Chapter 7, this chapter highlights the importance of integration of the whole facility, where the core process areas, as well as the support areas, are holistically interwoven into a comprehensive solution. This chapter also extends beyond the GMP core production areas covered in Chapter 7 to reinforce the idea that the architectural design of a compliant circulation system, production support areas (including QC and development laboratories), maintenance areas, and logistical support spaces (such as a GMP warehouse) all play a key role in the overall success of the project. This chapter offers information on building shell design considerations and the siting of the building in conjunction with a site master plan, and explains why these issues should be considered early in the project definition phase to avoid having to rework the design in subsequent

overall success of the project. This chapter offers information on building shell design considerations and the siting of the building in conjunction with a site master plan, and explains why these issues should be considered early in the project definition phase to avoid having to rework the design in subsequent design phases. This chapter also presents design issues and best practices for producing a well-defined project scope and the extensive planning activities necessary to define performance requirements early in the design process when the cost impact can be evaluated and alternatives can be proposed. The importance of a stage gate approach to sequential architectural activities and deliverables is presented to enable a better understanding of how a structured approached can help speed the early design process and reduce the need for rework in later design phases. 8.2 Architectural Design Criteria The architectural design criteria for biomanufacturing facilities vary greatly from product to product and the technology deployed to produce them. However, numerous design principles and criteria can help guide a successful design when applied thoughtfully. Examples of these design principles are as follows: •Parti: Ensure clear organization of the site and building. Examples include designating the front of the buildingfor people spaces and the back of the building for material and utility functions, organizing functions by floors(e.g., placing laboratories and manufacturing on one level and mechanical and utilities on another), and ensuringdistanced separation of hazardous areas from common areas. •Simple Circulation: Minimize turns and bends in circulation paths and hallways. Avoid elevation changes wherever possible. Size and design material circulation paths to support ease of movement of raw materials,WIP, and final products. Personnel circulation should be intuitive and safe to support egress requirements andefficient operations. •Glazing: Provide daylight access in all occupied spaces as much as possible, including production cleanroom spaces in the interior of the building. Provide visual access into production cleanroom spaces to eliminate theneed for non-production personnel to enter for viewing purposes. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Service areas should be located near points of use forefficiency and minimizing cost. It is important to maintain safe and clear access aisles since utility systems androuting of ducts and pipes can easily crowd these areas and reduce vertical clearances. Access and workspacefor maintenance personnel as well as transfer methods for tools and parts should be safe and ergonomic. •Safety: Materials posing physical and health hazards are common in biomanufacturing facilities. Emergency stations for safety showers, eyewashstations, and spill kits should be provided where appropriate. Even with multiple layers of cleanroom spaces andairlocks, clear and intuitive egress paths should be maintained. •Facility Lifecycle: Understand both immediate and future needs. •Flexibility and Serviceability: Plan for integration of future expansion as well as any equipment move in or out without extensive disruption to ongoing operations. •Security: Incorporate physical security and Intellectual Property (IP) protection in the design of the facility.Common security measures include access clearance requirements, security turnstiles and badge access atentry, security cameras, communication systems, site access barriers such as fencing and a guardhouse, alarmand monitoring devices, lighting systems, passcode-protected entrances, etc. •Pest Control: Design the perimeter of the building and all exterior door locations with proper landscaping,materials, and seals to support pest control. 8.2.1 Cleanroom Design Criteria: Materials and Finishes Exposed surfaces in cleanrooms need to be designed to help maintain a low air particulate count and to provide durability for the room’s operation over time. The main considerations that need to be addressed include: •Non-shedding: Finishes must not generate particulates. •Cleanable: Finishes need to be detailed to ensure that every surface is accessible for cleaning. The finishes also need to be non-porous and seamless with shapes and textures to avoid buildup of particulates and/or to notharbor bacterial growth. •Resilient: Finishes need to withstand cleaning chemicals and room operations without deteriorating, rusting,denting, cracking, or staining. Dents and chips can create uncleanable crevices for harboring bacteria and canalso result in cleaning solution getting under finishes, causing the finish to peel. Determining the correct finishes depends on many factors, including cleanliness level, room operation, cleaning regimens, user preferences, and budget. It is important to understand the cleaning regimen and how equipment and materials will be moved through the space prior to determining a finish scheme. All exposed areas in the cleanroom space need to be detailed in such a way to minimize the number of surfaces, tight corners, and crevices. Where seams and crevices are unavoidable, they should be continuously sealed with a cleanroom-acceptable sealant. The main elements and features of a cleanroom include: •Ceiling and Wall Finish: High performance wall coatings are required to withstand cleaning agents and chemicals used in the space. Available options include epoxy paint systems, claddings, and pre-built modular systems with uPVC (unplasticized polyvinyl chloride) finishes. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. Facilities •Flush Door and Window Frames: Flush single rabbet frames reduce the number of surfaces requiring cleaning. Doors and frames also need to have a cleanroom-acceptable finish. •Coved Corners: Interior corners should be coved to create a seamless transition. This makes it easier to clean,eliminates creases where contaminates could build up, and helps streamline airflow . •Wall Protection: Corner guards, bumper rails, and armor plates can be added to protect finishes. Damaged surfaces can be a source of particulate generation and a place for bacterial growth, and have weak spots wherecleaning chemicals can get under finishes, causing failure. •Integral Coved Base: Flooring should have an integral coved base to eliminate seams or joints where bacteriaor dirt could be trapped. •Flooring Finish: Flooring should be homogeneous, cleanable, chemical-resistant, and able to withstand any material or equipment moving through the space without damage. Flooring options include epoxy , vinyl, and rubber, depending on the use of the space. •Sloped Tops: Avoid any potential horizontal ledges by sloping horizontal surfaces. •Equipment and Furnishings: Stainless steel is ideal for cleanroom furnishings due to its cleanability and corrosion resistance. •Fixtures: All fixtures need to be of an acceptable finish and fully sealed with no gaps, ledges, or rough edges. 8.3 Early Phases: Alignment, Programming, and Concept Design Early engagement of key stakeholders is essential for defining overall project goals and scope requirements.
