Figure 8.4: Example of a Room Confirmation Sketch Image courtesy of CRB, www.crbgroup.com. 8.3.3 Concept Design Concept design begins with organizing the programmed functions into a cohesive spatial form. Adjacency diagrams and blocking and stacking diagrams are example deliverables of early concept design, where facility massing is formulated around allocated spaces for all required functions, critical adjacencies, and efficient flows. Often, various project drivers can overlap or even compete with one another . Prioritizing these conflicting criteria can be informed by stakeholder review and input, the Project Strategy Document, and the design basis identified and documented during the programming phase of the project. Biopharmaceutical facilities are dynamic environments in which people, materials, equipment, ducts, and pipes work in orchestration to consistently, efficiently, and safely produce therapies. For a facility to be able to operate successfully, it must be designed with these operational activities and dynamic conditions in mind. Concept design should also incorporate a future expansion strategy that can define an approximate volume of space and a location to support the project’s future needs, yet provide flexibility for future unknowns and minimal disruption to early phase operations. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 131 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities A finalized concept design package typically includes architectural deliverables such as a Project Narrative, Site Plan, Floor Plans, Building Sections, Classification Plans, and GMP Flow Diagrams (personnel, materials, waste, equipment, and samples). All supporting programming documents such as a Room List and Room/Area Confirmation Sketches are also included. 8.4 Site Design This section explores site features and conditions that should be considered for biopharmaceutical facilities. 8.4.1 Site Master Plan It is valuable to have a site master plan that accounts for growth and expansion strategies. Planning for future expansions can inform design decisions for immediate needs, whether it is deciding to centralize utilities and warehousing for a campus, to dedicate these functions to individual buildings, or to design a facility as a consolidated block of space or with spine connections to various functional blocks of spaces. Other important decisions can be implemented at the site level, including site security and access, and outdoor amenity spaces. 8.4.2 Site Flows Establishing logical, safe, and efficient flows throughout the site can greatly impact the day-to-day operations of the facility. For example, consolidating pedestrian flows creates opportunities for pleasant outdoor gathering spaces, while segregating personal vehicle flows from truck flows improves the safety of people walking to and from their cars. Such a design also aids efficiency, security, and access control for delivery trucks. Exterior utilities, such as bulk gas tanks, should be located in such a way as to allow easy access for servicing. 8.4.3 Parking Local zoning requirements provide the criteria for the minimum quantity of parking spaces as well as accessible and Electric Vehicle (EV) charging requirements. In addition, it is important to align with the anticipated headcount of the facility users. Understanding the level of manual operations for production that may drive up the headcount, as well as production schedule and shift changes, can provide a more realistic number of parking spaces that could potentially become an operational bottleneck for employees reporting to work. 8.4.4 Loading Docks Biopharmaceutical facilities may require more loading docks and surrounding staging spaces than typically provided for an office/laboratory building. It is important to understand GMP warehouse operations and volumes for incoming materials, product shipments, vendor-supplied materials, and even waste staging and collection strategies. This ensures that adequate space, quantities, and types of docks are provided. For example, a single-use manufacturing facility may need more docks than a stainless steel facility, to support the receipt of large volumes of single-use consumables, as well as the staging and collection of used plastic bags for disposal. It is also important to understand the flow of incoming and outgoing materials inside the warehouse area to locate, or potentially segregate, shipping and receiving docks. 8.4.5 Site Utilities Site

receipt of large volumes of single-use consumables, as well as the staging and collection of used plastic bags for disposal. It is also important to understand the flow of incoming and outgoing materials inside the warehouse area to locate, or potentially segregate, shipping and receiving docks. 8.4.5 Site Utilities Site utilities need to be sized and located to efficiently support production. Potential systems for consideration include emergency generators, bulk gas tanks with access for refueling, storage shed for hazardous materials, waste treatment system, steam generators, compressed air, refrigeration systems, etc. Utility distribution concepts, such as utilities risers and bridges or underground trenches, need to be considered as well. Fire-rated assemblies for protection and/or maintaining appropriate distances from adjacent buildings may be required per code or insurance underwriter’s requirements. Page 132 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 8.4.6 Sustainability Decisions made during site and building shell design can have considerable impact on sustainability goals for the project, including but not limited, to rainwater management, renewable energy, sourcing of raw materials, thermal comfort, and daylight. Refer to Chapter 10 on sustainability for more information. 8.5 Building Core and Shell Multi-story buildings require careful organization of the vertical core elements, such as stairwells, elevator shafts, utility shafts, and stacking of electrical rooms and restrooms at each level. For biopharmaceutical facilities, locating these core functions strategically to allow large, contiguous blocks of space could be beneficial for optimal arrangement of manufacturing and warehouse areas. 8.5.1 Building Shell Design Building shell design is an important part of the project, whether it is a greenfield design, upgrade of an existing facility, or working with an existing exterior. The building envelope not only conveys the identity, image, and vision of the company, but it also serves the practical functions of protecting the building from outside elements and maintaining comfortable and healthy indoor conditions. Planning for large openings with roll-up doors or knock-out panels at strategic locations around the building shell, along with pick points and adjacent laydown areas, to accommodate move in/out access for large pieces of equipment should be considered. 8.5.2 Building Structure The building structure is a key component of an integrated design solution. Column bay spacing, vertical heights, and structural systems and materials should be designed and selected to support the internal process and programmatic needs. While wide column bays may offer minimal column interference and plan flexibility , bay spacing should also be balanced with loading capacity requirements as well as the challenges and opportunities of long span structures. Long span structures can result in deep beams that reduce usable vertical clearance, or an open web truss system could allow ducts and pipes to fit through. Similarly, high vertical clearances may offer greater flexibility in stack-up options. However, a very tall vertical space may require a secondary structure at a lower elevation and with adequate

deep beams that reduce usable vertical clearance, or an open web truss system could allow ducts and pipes to fit through. Similarly, high vertical clearances may offer greater flexibility in stack-up options. However, a very tall vertical space may require a secondary structure at a lower elevation and with adequate loading capacity for suspended equipment, pipes and ducts, and cleanroom ceiling panels. Ensuring suitable vertical clearance (floor-to-floor , floor-to-roof) for ceiling heights, above-ceiling mechanical and utility distribution, and maintenance access is an important consideration when designing a new building shell or securing an existing building shell to fit out a biopharmaceutical facility . Without proper planning, future flexibility could be limited by the selected structural system or materials since modifying the structure can be difficult and costly. For example, different lateral bracing systems could offer various levels of flexibility. In addition, moment frame connections could eliminate the need for brace frames but require larger structural members and can be more costly. Shear walls and brace frames could potentially limit the locations of circulation and utility routing as well as interfere with future interior modifications. Concrete structures with rebars and tendons can pose challenges for future location of openings. As structural design and calculations begin, it is important to be aware of and discuss the business risk factors associated with the biopharmaceutical facility. For instance, the structural importance factor may exceed the minimum required by code, for the purpose of business continuity or to protect high-value products and assets. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 133 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 8.5.3 Materials Selection High performance exterior systems can significantly contribute to energy and sustainability goals. For example, exterior systems such as well-insulated cladding panels (high R-values), high efficiency windows, and cool roof (Solar Reflectance (SR) and Solar Reflectance Index (SRI)) systems can be energy efficient and require lower mechanical loads. Just as there are different clothes appropriate for various weather conditions, there are exterior materials and systems that appropriately correspond to the building’s site and climate conditions. For instance, while high thermal mass materials would be suitable for hot, dry climate zones, high impact resistant glazing may be required in hurricane-prone zones, as non-combustible materials may be needed in areas prone to wildfires. Furthermore, the building’s future expansion plans should guide the selection of exterior systems since some materials and systems are more easily modifiable and adaptable than others. 8.6 Codes, Permitting, and Insurance To ensure smooth and timely progression of construction, applicable codes, standards, and insurance requirements should be built into the design from the beginning of a project. 8.6.1 Codes and Standards It is important to understand that code requirements are the minimum expectation, and the project team should apply best practices and a reasonable risk-based approach when designing a new facility . Code requirements—such as maximum allowable quantities of hazardous materials and resulting

beginning of a project. 8.6.1 Codes and Standards It is important to understand that code requirements are the minimum expectation, and the project team should apply best practices and a reasonable risk-based approach when designing a new facility . Code requirements—such as maximum allowable quantities of hazardous materials and resulting control areas or H-occupancy strategies and life safety and egress requirements—should be planned from the earliest stages of facility programming and design. In addition to building codes, other standards and regulations may be applicable, such as those from the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) [82], Americans with Disabilities Act Accessibility Guidelines (ADAAG) [83], National Fire Protective Association (NFPA) [84], ISO [85], and American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) [86], as well as the manufacturing company’s own internal design standards and HSE requirements. 8.6.2 Permitting Permitting activities can impact project schedule. Due to the highly complex nature of these facility types, some city building departments may outsource the permit set review or require multiple reviews from different departments and disciplines, which may result in a longer permit approval process. Scheduling a pre-meeting with the city to introduce the project early, if feasible, can help with alignment on the permit approval process and minimal schedule impact. The facility must comply with applicable codes and standards, and its compliance must be clearly documented for permit submission. The designer is responsible for researching and understanding the permit application requirements and preparing the submission documents accordingly. Local departments (Authorities Having Jurisdiction (AHJ)) have lists of specific types of information and documentation to be submitted for a successful permit process. Page 134 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 8.6.3 Building Certifications, Insurance Underwrit er Requirements For projects pursuing a building certification3, supporting design decisions and documentation are required. While the building and fire codes are primarily focused on life and the safety of people, insurance underwriters are focused on preventing and minimizing damage to people and property. For this reason, insurance underwriters may have additional or more stringent requirements beyond codes and standards, especially as they pertain to protecting the property from potential adverse events. Especially for facilities where high volumes of hazardous materials are present, it is important to confirm the insurance underwriter’s requirements (such as for fire protection systems and for bulk solvent tank containment and its minimum distance from the building) that may be more stringent than the code required minimum. The designer should also aim to minimize potential business interruption or loss of product. For example, MFL (Maximum Foreseeable Loss) walls should be integrated into the design as early as possible, and a containment strategy should be in place for large volumes of sprinkler water. 8.7 Design and Delivery of Modular Facilities The term “modular” is defined in a variety of ways and can have different meanings to different people

walls should be integrated into the design as early as possible, and a containment strategy should be in place for large volumes of sprinkler water. 8.7 Design and Delivery of Modular Facilities The term “modular” is defined in a variety of ways and can have different meanings to different people and in different contexts. For the purposes of this Guide, modular implies efficiency through repetition of standard units and can be applied in two broader categories: design and delivery. 8.7.1 Modular Design The increasing variety of therapeutic modalities and the rapid evolution of manufacturing technologies pose a challenge for long-term facility operation. Biopharmaceutical production facilities require a high level of flexibility to ensure they remain valuable and relevant over their lifespans. They also must be amenable to reconfiguration with production lines that can be scaled up or scaled out as needed, as required by shifting market demands. The use of standardized, modular designs for manufacturing facilities increases the ability to adapt to market projections and scale out in response to changes in market demand without disrupting existing operations. When determining whether to implement a modular facility design, the following inputs should be considered: •Scale of the process •Product maturity (new product introduction may favor modular and mobile concepts due to lower capital costs and faster build times) •Speed to market (modular and mobile concepts offer the potential to reach the market sooner) •Containment requirements (high-containment biosafety levels drive modular and mobile concepts) •Uncertainty in demand (a modular and mobile SUT new product launch facility defers the need to make decisions and enables rapid capacity additions) •Flexibility in process platform and scale (modular and mobile concepts support the use of the facility for multiple processes) Cost considerations (modular and mobile concepts have lower capital costs and allow for some costs to be allocated to operational expenses, which can be covered when a product generates revenue) Also referred to as standardized design, modular design is an approach that is independent of actual materials and construction delivery. With this approach, the design modules can range between planning grids, rooms or suite modules, and even an entire facility. 3 Building certification options include Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED) [87], WELL Building Standard® [88], ENERGY STAR® [89], and Zero Carbon [90]. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 135 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 8.7.1.1 Planning Grid A planning grid of 4'0" (1.2 meters) squares can be an effective design approach for organizing the layout and promoting the use of repetitive planning blocks. This dimension also aligns with prefabricated cleanroom panel dimensions that are typically 4'0" in width. 8.7.1.2 Room/Suite Module Designing a facility around a standard room or suite module can provide the benefit of accelerated design (design and detail one meeting room, repeat multiple times throughout the facility), consistency (all offices are the same size and design), predictability

panel dimensions that are typically 4'0" in width. 8.7.1.2 Room/Suite Module Designing a facility around a standard room or suite module can provide the benefit of accelerated design (design and detail one meeting room, repeat multiple times throughout the facility), consistency (all offices are the same size and design), predictability and efficiency (production personnel can perform exactly the same SOPs across different suites), as well as more accurate allocation of space for future expansion spaces. 8.7.1.3 Standard Facility Design Where multiple facilities of the same or similar types are planned—whether for capacity , redundancy, or other business reasons—a standard facility design can provide unique benefits. For example, design and engineering documents from the first facility can be extensively leveraged for subsequent facilities, potentially yielding a shorter schedule and lower Total Installed Cost (TIC). A standardized facility approach offers centralized and streamlined opportunities for technology transfer, operator and maintenance training, deployment across various sites, equipment and parts logistics, and more. For a successful standard facility design approach, it is important to establish realistic expectations around modifications needed for site-specific conditions and requirements and to focus standardization efforts on key design parameters. For instance, two facilities in different countries may retain consistent room sizes, arrangements, and flows, while allowing for variations in materials and finishes. 8.7.2 Modular Delivery Modular and other Prefabrication, Preassembly, Modularization, and Offsite Fabrication (PPMOF) systems can provide an alternative to traditional stick-build construction. These systems are fabricated in a controlled environment at an off-site location and therefore are not dependent on variable weather or construction sequence of activities at the project site. Modular delivery can vary in function and scale, ranging from equipment skids, process skids, super-skids, wall and ceiling panel systems for both cleanroom and non-cleanroom spaces, prefabricated cleanrooms, electrical rooms, chiller plants, to heating plants. Modular solutions require early engineering finalization, which may not be practical for projects that have not yet finalized project requirements. Changes after the commencement of fabrication could impact cost and schedule. Modular components are typically only a partial solution in delivering a complete operational facility. In biopharmaceutical facilities, modular delivery is commonly applied in cleanroom areas. Key considerations for prefabricated cleanroom panel system modules are as follows: •The building structure should be capable of supporting all ceiling panels and above-ceiling equipment, ducts, and pipes. •Suspended, walkable ceiling panels can provide a second work plane during construction, allowing trades to work in parallel and at a higher workforce density. •Product and approved installers provide predictable and consistent results that are not reliant on local trades’ experience and talent. Page 136 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Key considerations for prefabricated cleanroom system modules are as follows: •The structural bay should be wide enough to fit multiple modules and properly spaced to minimize dead-space gaps between modules and columns. •Many of these modules can be designed to be fully stand-alone with all supporting utilities provided within the modular unit(s). Some are designed to be located outdoors. •The ability to relocate and potential tax savings could be attractive benefits. •The tops of the modules can be designed to provide a second level plane for maintenance access, which could eliminate the need for secondary steel within the building. •Cleanroom modules are fabricated in a climate-controlled factory that is specifically designed for this purpose. Multiple modules can be fabricated at the same time, resulting in efficiency , consistency, and speed. •Each module is preassembled and tested before delivery, and can be delivered worldwide. •Shipping cost, timeline for delivery, and the fabrication capacity of the modular vendor should be considered. •Aesthetics and style options may be more limited compared to prefabricated cleanroom panel systems or the stick-build option. •These modules are fitted with their own flooring, which results in higher finish floor elevation compared to the building finish floor elevation. Stairs and ramps at the perimeter access points should be considered for material and people movement, accessibility requirements, and compatibility with adjacent spaces. •When installing prefabricated cleanroom modules inside an existing building, a constructability assessment should be performed to ensure that adequate laydown space, exterior opening, clear move in path and turningradius, and pick points are available for a successful installation. •Where daylight is required or desired within the cleanroom module, cleanroom window locations should be coordinated with the building shell glazing. For both cleanroom panel system and cleanroom system modules, it is critical that close coordination between the architect and modular vendor is maintained. Often, these prefabricated systems are treated as pieces of equipment and require a thorough review and input by the architect to ensure compliance with life-safety/egress, accessibility , and fire-resistive assembly requirements. Standardization can yield reductions in both cost and time. Through a QbD approach, many of the risks associated with the design and construction of customized facilities at each location can also be avoided. The growing availability of modular, advanced processing solutions makes this approach feasible. The use of modular systems allows for a Lean and integrated approach that leverages the specialized skills and capabilities of contractors, subcontractors, and systems vendors. Once a modular system has been identified for standardization, a best value analysis should be performed to ensure that it meets the specific process and overall project needs. This review loop is essential. When assessing the cost of a prefabricated cleanroom module, it is important to understand what is included in the estimate (e.g., design, fabrication, automation, transportation, Factory Acceptance Testing, installation, commissioning, and Site Acceptance Testing), since some of these items may be costed separately in a traditional delivery approach. Systems that will serve as platform standards should not only meet operating performance specifications,

to understand what is included in the estimate (e.g., design, fabrication, automation, transportation, Factory Acceptance Testing, installation, commissioning, and Site Acceptance Testing), since some of these items may be costed separately in a traditional delivery approach. Systems that will serve as platform standards should not only meet operating performance specifications, but also cost and delivery time requirements. In addition, having consistent facility and process designs ensures congruent product quality across all sites. Each delivery method offers different cost and schedule impact, and there is no one-size-fits-all solution. Each project should evaluate the most suitable delivery method based on cost and time drivers, as well as maintenance, aesthetics (look and feel), and asset management strategies. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 137 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9 Mechanical and Electrical Systems 9.1 Introduction This chapter addresses the mechanical and environmental control considerations unique to biopharmaceutical manufacturing. The design of HVAC, and other supporting systems specific to biopharmaceutical facilities, relies on evaluation of the factors pertaining to the risk for product contamination or adulteration by the system or the ambient environment. HVAC and environmental control should be part of an overall CCS and should complement or supplement other controls in place. The methods and considerations used in evaluation of these factors, both risks and protections, are discussed in Chapters 3 and 4. For detailed recommendations regarding system configuration and control, refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. The utilities needed for biopharmaceutical facilities are common to many manufacturing facilities, and include: •Lighting (e.g., general, task, and emergency) •Power (normal, backup, and uninterruptable) •Cooling water (e.g., condenser water, chilled water) •Process water (compendial and non-compendial) – refer to: -ISPE Baseline® Guide: Volume 4 – Water and Steam Systems (Third Edition) [12] -ISPE Good Practice Guide: Membrane-Based Water for Injection Systems [91] •Steam (e.g., pure steam, clean steam, plant steam) – refer to the ISPE Baseline® Guide: Volume 4 – Water and Steam Systems (Third Edition) [12] •Fire protection (wet, dry, or pre-action) •Compressed air (clean, dry air, and plant or instrument air) – refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Process Gases (Second Edition) [61] •Other gases (e.g., N 2, O 2, CO 2) – refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Process Gases (Second Edition) [61] •Liquid nitrogen (LN 2) •Process waste, vent, and waste treatment (collection, deactivation (kill), and neutralization) •Process exhaust treatment (e.g., filtration, adsorption, scrubbing, thermal oxidation) As in all facilities, Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing (MEP), and fire protection systems should be designed to meet local codes and regulations and to satisfy both good engineering practice and business needs. Throughout this chapter, the term HEPA (High Efficiency Particulate Air) is used to refer to a range of filter types from H-13 (IEST Type A, ISO 40E) to U-16 (IEST Type G, ISO 50E). Page 138 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.2 Mechanical Systems and En vironmental Controls Each step in the manufacture of a biopharmaceutical drug substance should be evaluated for risk and protection factors. This evaluation guides the selection of the mechanical systems and environmental controls required to ensure both product safety and a safe work environment. 9.2.1 Aerosol Control Aerosol refers to suspensions of particulate (e.g., inert matter, viable organisms, products, chemical powders) or droplets in air. Without aerosols, cross-contamination through the HVAC system or through air transfer between spaces (exfiltration or infiltration) is not possible. Additionally, aerosol generation within a certain space increases the risk of cross-contamination via movement of people or materials to other spaces. Therefore, the risk of cross-contamination is directly related to the creation of aerosols. 9.2.1.1 Kinetic Energy Process steps that add significant energy to a culture or product present an increased risk of generating aerosols and cross-contaminating other products or increasing operator exposure. Processes and equipment that, if breached, could allow product to become airborne include, for example, positive pressure and pumped transfers (for chromatography, filtration, or movement of material), centrifugation, and pressurized reactor vessels. Typical mitigations in areas of high aerosol risk include: •Increasing the air exchange rate to dilute aerosols in the space •Implementing enhanced filtration to remove aerosols from a recirculated airstream •Localizing air recirculation to minimize cross-contamination risk •Applying Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) to remove potential aerosols from the environment 9.2.1.2 Pre and Post-V iral Separation Multiple process steps are normally used to reduce the concentration of organisms, DNA/RNA, and virus-like particles from a culture. Due to the continuous nature of these processes, it may be difficult to precisely identify the “pre-viral” and “post-viral” steps; however, it is important to identify a point in the process where virus reduction is adequate to justify the separation of air systems. Air systems serving upstream open processing areas (e.g., open inoculum preparation and open harvest) are usually separated from downstream processes (e.g., purification) at the point where an acceptable reduction is achieved. Air systems are usually separated with the intent of reducing the chance of contamination of the final product (by introducing contamination at a late stage of the process); however , this may not be supported scientifically as it assumes that an aerosol is created and that it is not adequately filtered. Using closed processing during pre and post-viral separation can eliminate the need to use HVAC systems for the separation of these

a late stage of the process); however , this may not be supported scientifically as it assumes that an aerosol is created and that it is not adequately filtered. Using closed processing during pre and post-viral separation can eliminate the need to use HVAC systems for the separation of these process stages. In order to be considered “closed” for the purposes of eliminating pre and post-viral separation, the entire process within the subject space must be closed (as described in Chapter 5) from the time of sterilization to the time that the product leaves the room. Open connections and additions, other open operations, or unintended leaks could violate process closure, requiring the discussed separation. The CCS should consider the process configuration and potential failures in determining whether this separation is necessary . The ability of the product or organisms to become airborne, as well as the ability of treatment steps to remove viruses or virus-like particles, should be considered. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 139 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.2.1.3 Viral Product Separation Similar to pre and post-viral separation, separation of HVAC systems for viral product (e.g., viral vaccines) manufacturing may be justified. Areas where the cultured virus is present would be separated from those where the virus is inactivated or attenuated. In the case of vaccines, the pathogens may not be completely removed, but an acceptable level of inactivation may be achieved by one step or a series of steps. The point of inactivation is generally clearly identifiable in viral products. Where wild virus is used for analytical testing of these products, HV AC separation may also be justified to minimize the cross-contamination risk, as determined in the CCS. Viral products using active, unattenuated viruses (e.g., live virus vaccines and viral vectors) may not require this type of separation if there is no difference between the virus in the bioreactor broth and the final product. Where pathogens may be present in the starting material (e.g., apheresis materials from patients with known disease or where starting materials are not adequately screened), consideration of HVAC separation between processing of these materials and other materials should be part of the CCS. 9.2.1.4 Product Pot ency The handling of potent products comes with containment requirements similar to hazardous material handling and biocontainment. However, it is important to consider the actual concentration of the potent product at the particular stage of the process for both contamination control and safety. The potency of a biological product usually increases as it moves downstream through the purification process. Conversely , the potency of an API is usually reduced during formulation due to dilution by excipients. HVAC containment techniques for potent compounds are nearly identical to those for biocontainment and biosafety. The control of potent compounds to reduce the risk of cross-contamination with these materials to an acceptable level (mass per dose)

potency of an API is usually reduced during formulation due to dilution by excipients. HVAC containment techniques for potent compounds are nearly identical to those for biocontainment and biosafety. The control of potent compounds to reduce the risk of cross-contamination with these materials to an acceptable level (mass per dose) may include: • Closed processing and closed charge/discharge transfers • Negative pressure or double-walled closed RABS or isolators • LEV • Negative room pressurization relative to surrounding areas - Use of bubble and/or sink airlock pressurization • Task specific gowning, incorporating dedicated degowning areas and mist or decontamination showers • Treatment of recirculated or exhausted air to remove the potent compound from the airstream • Once-through (also called single-pass) HVAC • Dedicated facilities (with dedicated HVAC) Refer to Section 9.4 on Biocontainment for further information on containment. 9.2.2 Gowning The level of gowning can significantly impact room contamination and particulate/microbial performance. Personnel can be significant contributors to space contamination, due to shedding of both particulate and viable organisms. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 140 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The higher the level of gowning, the lower the demand for diluting airflow to offset the particulates and bioburden from operators. This becomes increasingly important as the space classification increases. Conversely, the higher the level of gowning, the lower the temperature of the space needs to be in order to maintain operator comfort. Lower temperatures may require additional airflow . Maintaining RH at lower room temperatures requires lower absolute humidity. Task specific gowning (gowning designated and donned for a particular task) is a useful approach to minimizing cross-contamination on the surface of gowns between operations. For further information on gowning and its relationship to space classification, see the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. For information on gowning practices for various classifications, refer to EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 – item 7.13 [7]. Note that the “trouser suit” indicated in this EudraLex document is understood to be an over-gown on top of cleanroom scrubs. When selecting gowning, consider contamination risk (operator to product and environment) and safety (product and environment to operator). The permeability of the gown (e.g., viral resistance rating, filtering, and water penetration rating) is an important part of gowning selection and CCS. 9.3 HVAC Design as Part of a CCS HVAC systems can be a source of both risk and protection factors to be considered as part of the CCS. Whether developing a CCS retrospectively or in parallel with design, the ability of HVAC systems and components to mitigate risks and maximize protection of product quality is fundamental to contamination control. To develop a meaningful and effective CCS, it is essential to understand the role of HVAC in controlling the manufacturing environment and the potential impact of that environment on product quality. 9.3.1 The Role of HVAC in Maintaining

risks and maximize protection of product quality is fundamental to contamination control. To develop a meaningful and effective CCS, it is essential to understand the role of HVAC in controlling the manufacturing environment and the potential impact of that environment on product quality. 9.3.1 The Role of HVAC in Maintaining Environmental Control HVAC systems can impact the manufacturing and testing environment in several distinct ways. HVAC systems can control the following: •Room temperature – via heating and cooling •Room RH – via humidification and dehumidification •Direction of airflow – via pressurization and differential airflow -Infiltration of aerosols into a room – via filtration and pressurization -Exfiltration of aerosols from a room – via filtration and pressurization •Migration of aerosols within a room – via control of room airflow patterns •Transfer of aerosols from room to room ¬– via system configuration, filtration, and exhaust •Room aerosol concentration – via dilution, filtration, and infiltration controls •Room molecular contaminant (e.g., fumes, vapors, and gases) concentration – via dilution, treatment, and infiltration controls For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 141 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities HVAC systems can provide protection against product contamination through the following means: •Establishment of a classified cleanroom environment with limited levels of: -Total particulate -Viable particulate •Filtration of the recirculated air from a space to remove aerosols •Pressurization of workspaces and airlocks to prevent migration of aerosols •Terminal filtration of supply air to prevent emission through supply ductwork in the case of supply failure •Cross-contamination controls to manage the risk of aerosol migration through the HVAC system -HEPA supply filtration and/or filtration within recirculation systems -LEV to remove potential aerosols -Localized recirculation (primary/secondary or small AHUs) -Dedicated air handling equipment -Once-through (single-pass) air Note: Total particulate is often erroneously referred to as non-viable; however, discrete particle counters measure all particles, viable or not. Only the latest generation of Resonant Mass Measurement (RMM) counters can discriminate between viable and non-viable particles. The “non-viable” particle limits specified in regulations, standards and regulatory guidance (e.g., EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7], FDA Guidance for Industry: Sterile Drug Products Produced by Aseptic Processing – Current Good Manufacturing Practice [92], ISO 14644-1 [93]) are actually total particle limits. Where filtration is used to mitigate aerosol migration, the reduction in potential airborne contaminants provided by filtration should be evaluated as part of the CCS. A quantitative assessment is both practical and recommended (see ISPE Baseline Guides [10, 38] for methodology). For a more comprehensive discussion of these basic functions and the systems/equipment used to achieve them, refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. The CCS should include an assessment of the impact of the environment and HVAC on each process step in order to understand the environmental control requirements of each space within a facility appropriately. 9.4 HVAC for Containment and Safety 9.4.1 Biocontainment for

The CCS should include an assessment of the impact of the environment and HVAC on each process step in order to understand the environmental control requirements of each space within a facility appropriately. 9.4 HVAC for Containment and Safety 9.4.1 Biocontainment for Biosafety Outside the Facility Biocontainment requires the prevention of the escape of airborne organisms from a space in order to protect public health. When assessing the biocontainment risk, the probability of the material becoming airborne should be considered. Liquids or solids will need added energy (e.g., by pumping) to allow the material to become airborne. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 142 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The need for biocontainment for biosafety outside the facility can drive risk mitigation approaches such as: •Primary process containment or closure •Secondary containment isolators or containment facilities •Treatment of the exhaust air from a space (e.g., scrubbing, chemical or thermal deactivation, or filtration) •Negative pressurization of spaces to prevent accidental emissions •Terminal filtration of supply air to prevent emission through the supply ductwork in the case of HVAC failure Filtration is a frequently used treatment for exhaust air or recirculated air , with a single HEPA filter typically used for most biosafety risks. For the greatest biosafety risks, double HEP A filters may be used to help provide assurance of containment. A properly installed single HEP A filter can provide a greater than 4 log reduction in airborne pathogens. The need for biocontainment may also drive redundancy in HVAC systems to ensure that pressure reversals and flow stoppages do not occur. Biocontainment should not drive decisions related to the use of recirculation or once-through ventilation. Once- through air provides no added protection to the environment outside of the processing area. 9.4.2 Biosafety Inside the Facility Biosafety requires the containment of airborne organisms within a space. The need for biosafety inside the facility can drive HVAC risk mitigation approaches such as: •Secondary containment isolation or containment facilities •Treatment of the recirculated air from a space (e.g., filtration) •Negative pressurization of spaces to prevent accidental emissions •Terminal filtration of supply air to prevent emission through supply ductwork in case of fan failure •LEV to remove potential aerosols •Localized recirculation (Primary/secondary or combined AHU) •Dedicated air handling equipment •Once-through (single-pass) air to remove any possible aerosol Filtration is a frequently used treatment for recirculated air, with single or double HEPA filters used to help provide assurance in reducing airborne pathogens, vectors, and genetic material. A properly installed single HEPA filter can provide a greater than 4 log reduction in airborne pathogens. T wo HEPAs in series (e.g., one in the return, one in the supply) can provide as much as an 11 log reduction in the mass of recirculated aerosols. The need for biocontainment also may drive redundancy in a HVAC system

The need for biocontainment also may drive redundancy in a HVAC system to ensure that primary containment is preserved, especially where isolators are used. When assessing this risk of potential airborne contaminants, the probability of the material becoming airborne should be considered. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 143 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The quantitative reduction in potential airborne contaminants provided by filtration should be evaluated as part of the risk assessment. At high levels of biosafety (e.g., BSL-3 and 4), once-through ventilation is frequently used. Although HEPA filters are effective against microorganisms, increasing levels of biosafety discourages the use of recirculation, favoring once-through ventilation. 9.4.3 Hazardous and Flammable Material Handling Hazardous or flammable material handling often requires dilution of the hazard within a space and containment of airborne hazards to prevent fugitive emissions from escaping a space, or monitoring of the concentration of the hazard, as required by code or design safety philosophy. Hazardous or flammable material handling can drive risk mitigation approaches such as: •Primary process containment closure •Treatment of the exhaust air from a space (e.g., scrubbing, adsorption, thermal oxidation, or filtration) •Negative pressurization of spaces to prevent accidental emissions •Redundancy to ensure pressurization in all conditions •Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) detection •Deflagration containment and venting •Once-through (single-pass) air to remove any possible flammable vapors •LEV to remove potential flammable vapors Fume scrubbing or carbon adsorption can be used to treat exhaust air . Hazardous or flammable material handling may also drive redundancy in HVAC systems to ensure that dilution of contaminants by fresh air and room pressurization are not lost due to equipment failure. When recirculating air that may contain a significant quantity of flammable vapors (alcohol wiped or sprayed for sanitization is normally not significant), LEL detection is recommended and may be required by code or standard. The use of large volumes of hazardous or flammable materials may discourage the use of recirculation, favoring once-through ventilation. When assessing this risk, the actual probability of the material becoming airborne should be considered. Flammable liquids and some other hazardous materials may have a high vapor pressure, emitting vapors without added energy; some liquids or solids will need to have energy added (e.g., by pumping) to allow the material to become airborne. The risk assessment for these spaces should include a quantitative assessment of the capability of the risk mitigation approach to mitigate emissions to an acceptable level. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 147 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.5.2 HVAC Design for Classified Spac es The classification level of an area (or separative device) drives multiple HV AC parameters. All other factors being equal (e.g., similar products, identical process steps in the same process equipment), cleaner spaces (lower number classification) require greater airflow and higher grades of filtration. 9.5.2.1 Filtration HEPA/ULPA filtration is broadly applied in biopharmaceutical facilities to minimize the risk of cross-contamination as well as gross-contamination. Table 9.4 summarizes the typical filtration and airflow direction for each classification. Note that these typical values should be modified as appropriate for product/process risk. Table 9.4: Typical Filtration and Flow Direction by Area Classification Classification Filtration Airflow Direction ISO 5 / Grade A HEPA / ULPA 45E, H14, Type C, F, or K Positive(1) ISO 7 / Grade B(2)HEPA / ULPA 45E, H14, Type C, F, or K Positive(3) ISO 8 / Grade C HEPA / ULPA 45E, H14, Type C, F, or K Positive(3) Grade D HEPA / ULPA 45E, H14, Type C, F, or K Positive(3) CNC+ / CNC 30E, HEPA / ULPA or MERV 14/15 Per design UC MERV 11 Per design Natural Ventilation N/A N/A Notes: 1.Where BSCs are used to provide a Grade A/ISO 5 environment, the area adjacent to the sash is negative to its surroundings, with negative pressure plenums on 4 sides and HEPA/ULPA filtration on a 5th side. 2.In some cases, the Grade A environment may be in an ISO 8/Grade C background. 3.Manufacturing areas can be negatively pressurized relative to a common corridor, if a clean corridor design is used. In this approach, the common space connecting workrooms is given the same classification as the work rooms and is positively pressurized, to push clean air into these rooms. This approach is essentially a sharedbubble airlock. 4.Pressurization approach may be impacted by the need for biocontainment. The use of bubble or sink airlocks, in lieu of cascade, is appropriate to maintain cleanliness, provide biocontainment, and control cross-contamination. ISO 8/Grade C classification can also be achieved using high efficiency non-HEP A filters; however, both regulations [7, 92] and industry practice suggest that HEPA filters should be employed for all sterile operations, starting at ISO 8. The following tables provide cross-references between common systems of filter classification to facilitate understanding of legacy facilities and to ease working across international borders. Page 152 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities The quantity of airflow required to meet the target classification varies based on a number of factors. Sophisticated cleanroom airflow calculators and Computational Fluid Dynamic (CFD) modeling can help designers to achieve effective and efficient designs that relate directly to the process design and architectural configuration. Accepted calculation methods for airflow in cleanrooms can be found in ISO 14644-16 [93]. The use of ventilation rate (or Air Change Rate (ACR)) has been dropped by ISO, but can still be used for planning. This approach is also embodied in the FDA 2004 Guidance for Industry [92], which contains a suggestion that 20 AC/hr should be adequate for ISO 8 and suggests that cleaner spaces should have higher ventilation rates. For a more detailed discussion on the design and construction of classified spaces, see the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, V entilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. 9.5.3 Physical Arrangement and Details of Construction The physical arrangement of spaces and details of construction can have a significant impact on HV AC system design. The adjacency of spaces with different classifications and biosafety needs can drive pressurization schemes and outside air usage. Similarly, the use of tight space construction can minimize the use of outside air for pressurization while maintaining good environmental control. Typical elements of concern include: •Doors and door seals •Walls •Ceilings 9.5.3.1 Airlocks Airlocks are normally interposed between spaces of differing classification to provide space for preparing to enter or exit a more highly classified area. Airlocks also enhance pressurization control by utilizing interlocked doors, which allows differential pressure to be maintained across the closed door . Gowning, cleaning, and disinfection activities can liberate particulate matter that might require dilution before the door to the cleaner space is opened. This step is taken to ensure that opening the door will not negatively impact the cleanliness of the cleaner room. Time delays between the opening of interlocked doors, based on qualification testing, can be implemented to enforce the necessary recovery time. However, proper airlock design aims to minimize or eliminate the need for these time delays by ensuring that clean air is always closest to the cleaner room door. Airlocks should normally be arranged to flush air from the cleaner end to the less clean end and have sufficient airflow to ensure quick recovery. Returns should be arranged to quickly remove any particulate matter that enter the airlock or is released therein, thus protecting the cleaner room and the garb entering the cleaner room. For a further discussion of the impact of architecture on HVAC design, refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. 9.5.4 Manufacturing Environment and Cleanliness Cascades The US [14] and the EU [16] or PIC/S [32] utilize similar approaches to space classification,

the cleaner room. For a further discussion of the impact of architecture on HVAC design, refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. 9.5.4 Manufacturing Environment and Cleanliness Cascades The US [14] and the EU [16] or PIC/S [32] utilize similar approaches to space classification, with the differences previously outlined in Table 9.1. The similarity in approach between the two predominant systems yields similar space pressurization schemes in the US and EU or PIC/S and WHO countries. The difficulty arises when attempting to explicitly satisfy both schemes. This hybrid approach has more pressurization steps and thus may place greater strain on buildings and systems. Extra pressurization steps may be added to enhance segregation, but care must be taken as this added pressure step can result in added and undesirable velocity at interfaces and increased leakage to uncontrolled spaces or at tubing pass-throughs, which may make control difficult. Neglecting a step(s) between classes, on the other hand, may be viewed as a deficiency and has been observed in regulatory citations (based on the experience of the authors). This is especially true with entry or bi-directional airlocks. Due to their function, exit airlocks may possibly be viewed as lower risk to the classified space. Pass-through boxes represent a slightly different case as these are usually exceptionally small when compared with the size of the cleanroom, may not

authors). This is especially true with entry or bi-directional airlocks. Due to their function, exit airlocks may possibly be viewed as lower risk to the classified space. Pass-through boxes represent a slightly different case as these are usually exceptionally small when compared with the size of the cleanroom, may not be frequently used, and are generally nearly airtight. The small size of pass-through boxes results in a comparatively small number of particles that may be introduced to a clean space by opening the door. The tight sealing nature of the boxes provides no motive force for the entering particles when the doors are open; the particles enter by diffusion and the action of operators on items in the box. However, this relationship may need to be adjusted to accommodate containment of aerosols, biosafety levels, and other risks. Negative pressurization of cleanrooms is generally to be avoided, especially for aseptic processing. To achieve these conflicting goals of safety and cleanliness, interposing airlocks with non-cascading pressure relationships are often employed. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 155 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.5.4.2 AHU Zoning In all manufacturing facilities, it is common to zone AHUs to separate: •Cleanroom classifications •Incompatible products and processes •Incompatible temperature and RH requirements For biopharmaceutical facilities, it may also be desirable to separate: •Seed operations •Inoculum preparation •Final bulk drug substance filling operations •High heat load or high particle load operations •Open upstream and downstream operations •Highly potent or sensitizing or otherwise hazardous compound handling areas -Beta-lactam antibiotic production -Refer to EudraLex Volume 4 Chapter 3 – items 3.6 and 5.28–5.20 [34] and FDA GFI [92] The primary/secondary AHU configuration allows for further segregation without significant cost. Whether the secondary units utilize fan coil units for recirculation or fan filter units, this system configuration allows zoning on a room-by-room basis. When documenting primary/secondary systems, consider showing both the area

and 5.28–5.20 [34] and FDA GFI [92] The primary/secondary AHU configuration allows for further segregation without significant cost. Whether the secondary units utilize fan coil units for recirculation or fan filter units, this system configuration allows zoning on a room-by-room basis. When documenting primary/secondary systems, consider showing both the area served by the larger primary air unit as well as the local recirculation units. 9.5.5 Resource Conserva tion and Sustainability Targets As much as 60% of the energy consumed by an average pharmaceutical facility is dedicated to the HVAC systems. Optimizing the selected critical parameters and acceptance criteria for HV AC can provide a significant reduction in both cost and greenhouse gas emissions. Refer to Chapter 10 for a detailed discussion of sustainability. 9.5.6 Product Value As product value increases, business drivers may push design toward a higher level of space classification to minimize the risk of a lost batch due to environmental contamination or due to an inconclusive investigation into potential contamination. 9.5.7 Product Medical Criticality An increased medical need for a product may push design toward a higher level of space classification to minimize the risk of a lost batch due to environmental contamination or due to an inconclusive investigation into potential contamination. Page 156 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.6 Monitoring •The Environmental Monitoring System (EMS) provides validated monitoring and alarming of critical environmental conditions through dedicated, validatable probes within the manufacturing environment. Considerations for the EMS are as follows: -This system, whether stand-alone or firewalled off from the Building Management System (BMS), should possess unique access control, security, change control, audit trail, calibration management, non-volatilerecord creation and report generation, and electronic signatures (if desired). It should be in full compliancewith 21 CFR Part 11 [1] with the capability to be qualified and Computer System Validation (CSV) validated. •While the EMS can share sensors with the BMS, any shared sensors should still be under calibration management. The systems should be isolated by an appropriate signal splitter or communication of the field datato the BMS from the EMS via an appropriate high-speed bus. •The EMS should be functionally tested and subject to IQ, OQ, PQ, and CSV. •Employing a system status indicator in both the cell processing room and at its entry is considered best practice. These status indicators can provide local notification if any environmental parameter is in alert or alarm status forappropriate action (a requirement for aseptic processing facilities). Additionally, they can prevent operations fromstarting if the proper conditions are not present. Typical EMS monitored parameters for biopharmaceutical HVAC systems include: •Temperature •Humidity •Differential Pressure (DP) •Particulate -During operation in Grade A as a minimum -Continuous in Grade A is recommended -Continuous at select locations in Grade B is also recommended •Airflow (optional) •Non-HVAC equipment monitoring (e.g., freezers, incubators, refrigerators, etc.) Typical monitoring devices for biopharmaceutical EMS

biopharmaceutical HVAC systems include: •Temperature •Humidity •Differential Pressure (DP) •Particulate -During operation in Grade A as a minimum -Continuous in Grade A is recommended -Continuous at select locations in Grade B is also recommended •Airflow (optional) •Non-HVAC equipment monitoring (e.g., freezers, incubators, refrigerators, etc.) Typical monitoring devices for biopharmaceutical EMS include: •Temperature – 100-ohm, platinum RTD with transmitter, 4–20 mA output •Humidity – Thin film capacitive grid, 4–20 mA output •Differential Pressure – Capacitance silicon diaphragm, 4–20 mA output, 0.5" WC range, 0.001" WC resolution without error (0.25% accuracy) •Airflow (optional) – Pitot tube with DP sensor as above or vortex shedding sensor with piezo electric signal element, or temperature compensated hot wire anemometer For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 157 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.7 Qualification of HVAC Sy stems Risk assessment is required to determine the elements of the HVAC system requiring qualification and validation for biopharmaceutical products. Typically, room level elements and controls or monitoring require qualification in these facilities. Typical critical components requiring qualification include: •Terminal HEPA/ULPA filters – supply and return (if any) •EMS •Typical HVAC qualification tests may include: •Terminal HEPA/ULPA filter efficiency or integrity testing •Room temperature and humidity verification •Room fabric test (leak test – optional) •Viable particle counts (in-operation and at-rest) •Total particle counts (in-operation and at-rest) •Recovery testing (from in-operation to at-rest limits, as appropriate – see ISO 14644-3 [93]) •Room air balance (or flow monitoring system) •Room DP (as part of the EMS) •EMS calibration and CSV (per 21 CFR Part 11 [1], EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 11 [34]) 9.8 Cost Considerations for HVAC Systems 9.8.1 100% Exhaust (Once-through) 100% exhaust (once-through) HVAC systems can produce acceptable results in many situations, but are rarely justifiable as protection against cross-contamination that can be provided by more sustainable means. The 100% exhaust systems are often chosen for spaces where vectors are manufactured or where other serious cross-contamination risks are present. Although this approach is safe, it is extremely expensive to operate and wasteful. 9.8.2 Constant Volume Reheat Constant volume reheat systems are often chosen for their stability. This approach is safe, but also extremely expensive to operate and wasteful. Adjusting AHU discharge temperature to satisfy both heat load and RH requirements with minimal reheat is a more efficient approach. 9.8.3 Central Station AHUs versus Primary/Secondary Primary/secondary systems recirculate air that is close to ideal conditions, minimizing filtration, overcooling, and reheating. These systems can also be operated on an as-needed basis, adjusting airflows as needed to meet environmental needs. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 158 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 9.8.4 Material of Construction Because sporicidal agents and VHP are frequently used to sanitize biopharmaceutical facilities, stainless steel is often specified for exposed surfaces and even recirculating HVAC components. However, galvanized steel is suitable for occasional exposure to hydrogen peroxide and can be used in duct systems. Furthermore, protective coatings can often make lower cost materials as resistant as stainless steel to attack by peroxyacetic acid or sodium hypochlorite. 9.9 Cleaning and Maintenance of HVAC S ystems Biopharmaceutical facilities may require gaseous decontamination between processing batches or between products to minimize the risk of cross-contamination. HVAC considerations for gaseous decontamination are as follows: •HVAC systems must be able to suspend flow to and from the areas being treated, to prevent dilution of the decontamination agent. Bubble-tight dampers are not normally required, but low leakage dampers shouldbe provided to ensure that hydrogen peroxide concentrations in adjacent areas does not exceed personnelexposure limits. •Local recirculation systems that serve only the area being treated may be used, but the materials of construction need to be carefully selected to ensure compatibility. •If fogging with peroxyacetic acid, contact between the fog and any copper components should be avoided; aluminum is more resistant, but not compatible unless properly treated. •HVAC systems should be arranged to allow aeration of the decontamination agent from the space before resuming normal use. Considerations for HVAC system cleaning are as follows: •HVAC supply systems that utilize terminal HEPA/ULPA filters do not normally require cleaning. -HEPA/ULPA filters are extremely effective against microorganisms and particulate matter. -HEPA/ULPA filters normally become more efficient as more particulates collect on the upstream side. •In supply systems without terminal filters, cleaning of ductwork that connects to cleanrooms can be considered, but sanitization is more likely to be needed since only clean air flows through ducts supplying a cleanroom. •Return or exhaust systems generally only require cleaning if exposed to process aerosols (e.g., buffer or media powders). The mass of matter that might accrete (become attached) to the interior of cleanroom ducts is normallyminiscule. HVAC considerations for room cleaning are as follows: •Because fogged or vaporized sanitants are commonly used in biopharmaceutical facilities, special sequences of operation are normally required. -The means to initiate sanitization HVAC modes (e.g., isolation, aeration and, if used, recirculation) should be available remotely from the space being sanitized. •HEPA filter life may be reduced by recirculation of sanitizing agents through these filters. •It is recommended to have a means for monitoring the level of sanitant in the air surrounding spaces that undergo gaseous or fogged sanitization. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 159 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Where HEPA filters are used to control the risk of pathogens or vectors in the exhaust, a Bag-In/Bag-Out (BI/BO) filter housing should be considered to minimize the potential escape of vectors or similar risks. An alternative to BI/BO construction is the gaseous decontamination of exhaust filters prior to removal. Even if not required by room classification, terminal HEPA filters can be fitted to high biological safety risk facility air supplies. This helps prevent aerosol migration through supply ductwork in case of a supply air failure or shutdown. The use of automatic dampers to achieve isolation may be an acceptable alternative, if so indicated by risk assessment. 9.10 Fire Protection •Attributes of fire protection systems specific to biopharmaceutical and vaccine facilities, as allowed by local code and authorities, include: •Use of cleanable sprinkler heads that are compatible with cleaning agents. Use of heads that are incompatible with the cleaning regime can lead to accidental release. •Sealing of concealed heads should be considered for reducing the risk of accidental release and increasing the capacity for cleaning. These heads can also minimize air leakage. •Use of pre-action systems to minimize the risk of accidental release in cleanrooms and biosafety areas. •Collection of sprinkler discharge may be required in manufacturing and biosafety areas. The collected discharge may require abatement prior to discharge. •Dry chemical extinguishing systems are typically only needed where volatile organic solvents are used. •Clean agent extinguishing systems (gaseous suppression) may also be used in high-risk/high-value areas, to ensure prevention of contamination of product by the fire extinguishing agent. -Clean agent extinguishing systems normally require HVAC isolation and room seal testing. Any means egress and notifications should be considered. Appropriately qualified personnel should be consulted whenemploying these systems. 9.11 Other Safety Concerns The use of liquid nitrogen and other asphyxiation hazards may require the use of oxygen depletion sensors for personnel safety. 9.12 Electrical •Attributes of electrical systems specific to biopharmaceutical and vaccine facilities include: •Cleanroom lighting that is fully sealed from the room side may be required for manufacturing areas. Top access should be considered to minimize the risk of contamination of these rooms. •Cleanroom phones and intercoms that are fully sealed from the room side are recommended for manufacturing areas. •It is generally beneficial to seal all conduits and penetrations from the room side in manufacturing areas. Page 160 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Within biosafety level 2+ (or BSL 3**) 3, and 4 areas conduits and penetrations from the room side in manufacturing areas should be sealed. •Biopharmaceutical processes are typically wet and the use of NEMA 3R, 4, and 4X closures may be beneficial within manufacturing areas. •Within biosafety areas, NEMA 4X wiring devices and liquid tight conduits are recommended. •Backup power should be considered for all biological product facilities. Loss of power to bioreactors and processing equipment for more than a brief period can be catastrophic and result in the loss of batches. This is generally a business issue, rather than a GMP issue, but in cases of medical necessity the loss can be morethan financial. •When evaluating the need for backup power, the need for HVAC during periods of power loss to ensure environmental control should be considered. Qualification of the period that the facility is capable of maintainingconditions without active HVAC systems and a study of the sequencing of the restart of all systems upon theapplication of emergency power are recommended. •The transition back from emergency power to normal power also should be evaluated. Standard transfer switches make an “open” transition from normal power to emergency power and back, requiring equipment torestart in both cases. It may be advisable to make the transition back to normal power a manual transition (ratherthan automatic) to minimize impact. Similarly, “closed” transition equipment, which is capable of transferring backto normal power without a loss of current for more than a few milliseconds, can be considered to minimize thisimpact. Paralleling equipment can be considered that would allow a facility to transition to backup power andback to normal power without loss of current, in a planned outage, offers the greatest flexibility but at the greatestcost. •Uninterruptable power supplies should be provided for critical control systems and equipment. The loss of data from process equipment can result in lost batches. While it is advisable to put EMS on uninterruptable power,it is not usual to put environmental equipment on these systems as the inrush current needed to start motorspresents a challenge to these systems. In addition, environmental control (HVAC) systems can usually withstanda short shutdown period. •Wiring means and methods within biopharmaceutical facilities vary, but the electrical services within production areas should be liquid tight and capable of withstanding the cleaning products normally used in theseenvironments. •Coated rigid conduit, stainless steel conduit and PVC conduit can be used, along with liquid tight flexible conduits. NEMA 4 and 4X enclosures may be used in wet areas. NEMA 12 may be used for powder handling orother process areas. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 161 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 10 Sustainability 10.1 Introduction 10.1.1 Definition of Sustainability Applicable to Biopharmaceutical F acilities In a broad sense, sustainability means ensuring the future of human life on planet Earth. This description encompasses many threats to sustainability, such as climate change, fresh water shortages, contamination of air, water, and soils as well as the irresponsible use of finite resources (such as fossil fuels and scarce elements). The ISPE Handbook: Sustainability [96] broadens this definition further to include issues of equity , defining sustainability as “use of resources, in an environmentally responsible, socially fair and economically viable manner, so that by meeting current usage needs, the possibility of its use for future generations is not compromised.” In the design and operation of biopharmaceutical facilities, there are many opportunities to enhance sustainability. These include considering the carbon footprint of materials of construction and lifetime energy use as well as waste generation of both the manufacturing facility and the auxiliary buildings such as laboratories and offices. In addition to the facility itself, sustainability also applies to the product. There are ways to increase sustainability in raw materials, processing, packaging, and the final product; each of these aspects has its own carbon footprint. Enhancing sustainability is the responsible choice. Refer to the ISPE Handbook: Sustainability [96] for a more thorough discussion of topics presented below. 10.1.2 Net Zero Energy and Net Z ero Carbon The term net zero has been applied to both energy and carbon. Net zero carbon means that a facility is designed and operated in a way that carbon dioxide emissions to the atmosphere are not increased as compared to what carbon dioxide emissions would be without the facility. Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas that contributes to global warming, with all of its associated negative impacts. A net zero carbon facility is naturally more sustainable. Net zero energy means that a facility generates only as much energy as it uses. It may or may not increase sustainability, depending on how the facility generates its energy. For example, if the facility has its own gas-fired combined heat and power plant, it could generate all its own energy but it would also be adding carbon dioxide to the atmosphere. On the other hand, if the facility generates all of its energy on-site with renewable sources, such as solar and wind, then it would be both net zero carbon and net zero energy. Net zero carbon and net zero energy are noble goals, but are not so easy to achieve when creating new facilities. Minimizing energy usage might be a more practical goal for energy use in a new facility. Since net zero carbon more directly addresses sustainability, it may be a better goal for a facility and product manufacturing. As a stretch goal, consider

not so easy to achieve when creating new facilities. Minimizing energy usage might be a more practical goal for energy use in a new facility. Since net zero carbon more directly addresses sustainability, it may be a better goal for a facility and product manufacturing. As a stretch goal, consider net negative carbon facilities that sequester or displace more carbon emissions than they produce. 10.2 Process/Facility Interface – Reducing Space and Energy Intensity 10.2.1 Environmental Control and Process/Facility Design Environmental control accounts for most of the energy use in typical biopharmaceutical manufacturing facilities. Careful selection of process technologies aimed at minimizing the impact of the indoor environment on product quality can significantly reduce the energy required for environmental control. Similarly , strategic facility design can also minimize the size of any critical environments that are required, thus reducing energy use and potentially carbon footprint. Page 162 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 10.2.2 Closure Fully closed processes are not dependent on the surrounding environment, thus reducing or eliminating the need for highly classified environments. A risk assessment can show what, if any, environmental controls are needed for closed processes. In cases where a process cannot be closed, smaller segregated spaces, microenvironments, or isolator technologies can be used to limit the amount of space required for the more energy intensive classified volumes. Chapter 4 discusses in more detail process closure and the methods to close a process. 10.2.3 Spatial Efficiency Spatial efficiency is a significant factor in sustainable facility design. Since the function of the facility is to contain the process, special consideration needs to be given to the CCS, discussed in Chapter 4, and the process equipment footprint. For closed processes, ballroom designs present a proven method for increasing spatial efficiency in biopharmaceutical facility design, by sharing circulation space between multiple unit operations. Spatial efficiency strongly contributes to sustainable design. 10.2.4 Biosafety and Biocontainment As some processes utilize biohazardous organisms, the facility’s environment can be considered one of the layers of protection from the escape of these biohazards. Increased pressure differentials, once-through air , and additional airlocks are sometimes included, making these facilities more energy intensive. Accordingly, alternative design features that limit the additional energy input should be considered, for example: •Air leakage reduction (sealed back boxes, sealed penetrations, gasketed doors) •Engineered leakage paths (to maintain the desired flow differential with low containment risk) •Local recirculation in lieu of once-through air (primary/secondary) •Filtration in lieu of once-through air (single or double HEPA or ULPA filtration) •Energy recovery from exhausted air (enthalpy wheels are typically not recommended due to contamination risk at higher BSL ratings) •Isolators or other microenvironments to minimize the size of the contained area Filtration is a frequently used treatment for recirculated air using both single and double HEP A filters to help provide assurance of reduction of airborne pathogens.

are typically not recommended due to contamination risk at higher BSL ratings) •Isolators or other microenvironments to minimize the size of the contained area Filtration is a frequently used treatment for recirculated air using both single and double HEP A filters to help provide assurance of reduction of airborne pathogens. A single properly installed HEPA filter can provide a greater than 4-log reduction in airborne pathogens. The quantitative reduction in potential airborne contaminants provided by filtration should be evaluated as part of the risk assessment. At high levels of biosafety (e.g., BSL-3 and 4) once-through ventilation is frequently used. Although HEPA filters are effective against microorganisms, higher biosafety levels discourage the use of recirculation, favoring once-through ventilation instead.
