ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 163 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 10.3 Process Sustainability Strategies should be used to avoid hazardous materials and minimize process energy needs. Route selection is engrained in the process development of small molecule synthesis and purification. For bioprocesses, speed to market is often a stronger driver than optimal yield or cost of goods. The resources to optimize the process only become available when there is certainty of a product’s success. ICH Q12 [97] can be used to develop a pathway for lifecycle improvements including those that improve sustainability, such as: •Reducing energy input (e.g., ambient holding and processing, filtration instead of centrifugation) •Reducing waste (e.g., limiting overages in raw materials and fills/samples) •Improving yield efficiency of the processes (e.g., fewer batches to meet output goals) Process designs that employ concentration steps early in the purification process can reduce buffer volumes and, thus, reduce water use. Often, more continuous processes replace higher short-term energy demand with a lower continuous demand that is more easily met using recovered waste heat or ambient cooling in lieu of combustion or refrigeration systems. For example, high cell concentration fed-batch and perfusion processes can maximize the utilization of both the energy and water invested in a process. Many processes also use CIP cycles to clean fixed stainless steel equipment. Cleaning of process equipment can be minimized using SUT; however, the carbon and waste footprint of the single-use systems should be compared to the carbon footprint of fixed systems and the savings of water and energy to determine if single-use is the more sustainable choice. 10.3.1 Green Chemistry Many bioprocesses take place in aqueous solutions and require nontoxic properties, limiting common solvents to low molecular weight alcohols and glycols. Opportunities to source these solvents from bio-mass derived sources instead of petroleum derived sources should be considered. In processes where the solvent is removed (e.g., evaporation of ethanol after lipid formation), consideration should be given to solvent recovery for reuse or energy capture. These cycles often use large quantities of water. Cycle development issues can also drive qualification teams to

of petroleum derived sources should be considered. In processes where the solvent is removed (e.g., evaporation of ethanol after lipid formation), consideration should be given to solvent recovery for reuse or energy capture. These cycles often use large quantities of water. Cycle development issues can also drive qualification teams to unnecessarily increase water quantities. Care should be given to evaluate alternatives to unnecessarily high-volume rinses. In the design of a new facility, environmental requirements should be carefully considered to avoid specifying cleanroom grades that exceed necessary levels. In addition, temperature and humidity requirements should have as wide a deadband as possible to avoid unnecessary energy use. Humidification should be used only where necessary for process considerations, because it may not be required for human comfort. As per the ASHRAE Standard 55 (Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy) [86], there is no lower humidity limit for human comfort, in general. Additional guidance on this topic may be found in other standards, such as ISO 10551 [98] and ISO 7730 [99]. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Page 164 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 10.4 Raw Materials 10.4.1 Sustainable Sourcing Bioprocesses are not commonly associated with significant environmental impact. Yet, there are still opportunities to ensure that raw materials are being sustainably sourced. For example, amino acids for cell culture media are traditionally derived by chemical synthesis (typically natural gas feedstock) but can also be derived by bio-mass fermentation or purification of food processing streams. Where possible, the distance travelled from raw material production to the manufacturing facility should be minimized. 10.4.2 Consumables – Sustainable Sourcing, Simplification, and Lower Impact Waste 10.4.2.1 Source Current technologies for consumables manufacturing relies on polymer chemistry derived from petroleum feed stocks. Opportunities for making more sustainable processes focus on the reduction of input materials/energy (e.g., less packaging, shorter transport distance) and simplification of consumables (e.g., using only the required number of sample bags on a system, modularization of systems to build exactly what is needed). In considering the adoption of SUT, a comparison could be made of the increased sustainability that might result from using multi-use technology instead. As stated in Section 10.3, the balance between clean energy and water use versus the environmental impact of SUT should be considered to determine the most sustainable option. 10.4.2.2 Disposal The primary disposal types suitable for single-use systems are landfills and incineration. In some locations, there is an opportunity to recycle the mixed plastic material. To improve sustainability, the following should be considered: •Reduction (e.g., minimizing tubing length) •Reuse (limited applications, e.g., chromatography resin) •Recycling (location dependent and may require on-site separation and/or decontamination) •Waste to energy Section 2.8 on waste management in the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Single-Use Technology [59] contains more detailed information on the handling of single-use system waste. 10.4.3 Packaging – Minimizing Materials and Sustainable Sourcing Packaging materials are necessary for

(location dependent and may require on-site separation and/or decontamination) •Waste to energy Section 2.8 on waste management in the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Single-Use Technology [59] contains more detailed information on the handling of single-use system waste. 10.4.3 Packaging – Minimizing Materials and Sustainable Sourcing Packaging materials are necessary for protecting the contents from damage (mechanical, heat, light, chemical). Advances in packaging materials allow for the same level of protection while reducing weight, improving recyclability, and/or improving biodegradability. Improving packaging configurations offers an opportunity to reduce the amount of packaging materials and space needed for transport and storage. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 165 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 10.5 Waste Management Biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities generate a large amount of waste in multiple forms including solids, liquids, and gases. Traditionally, much of the solid waste ends up in either a landfill or incinerators while liquid waste ends up in municipal waste treatment facilities. As the industry moves towards a more sustainable future, creative solutions to address the various waste streams generated will be required. Additionally , these solutions must address the entire facility lifecycle. Successful waste management requires a top-down commitment to ensure maximum benefits to the planet. However, doing what is right for the environment may not necessarily have the most attractive financial benefits (e.g., payback). 10.5.1 Solid Waste The types of solid waste generated in the biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical industry can be dependent upon the nature of the processes and the risks associated with them. The decision to use a stainless steel or single-use facility can have great impact on the quantities of solid waste generated. While the common types of solid wastes will exist in most facilities (e.g., cardboard, food waste, general waste, batteries, toner cartridges, etc.), the intent of this section is to encourage thinking about the types of solid wastes generated in the manufacturing facilities including, but not limited to: •Waste single-use system components, e.g., tubing, filters, bags. (which may or may not require decontamination/ inactivation) •Contaminated gowning, gloves, hair nets, shoe covers, etc. (which may or may not require decontamination/ inactivation) •Packaging materials including drums, containers, bags, bottles, etc. from raw materials or in-process intermediates •Spent process consumables such as chromatography resins or depth filters Whether a solid waste stream is hazardous or non-hazardous, there are technologies available to convert hazardous solid wastes to non-hazardous solid wastes which can then facilitate recycling options. These technologies should be investigated and suitability-for-purpose should be established as there are more options for disposal of non-hazardous waste. Biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical companies should reduce, reuse, and recycle solid waste streams wherever, and whenever, possible. For additional details on solid waste management, refer to the ISPE Handbook: Sustainability [96]. 10.5.2 Liquid Waste Liquid wastes generated in the biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical industry are primarily dependent upon the nature of the processes and the risks

should reduce, reuse, and recycle solid waste streams wherever, and whenever, possible. For additional details on solid waste management, refer to the ISPE Handbook: Sustainability [96]. 10.5.2 Liquid Waste Liquid wastes generated in the biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical industry are primarily dependent upon the nature of the processes and the risks associated with them. The decision regarding whether to use a stainless steel or single-use facility can have a huge impact on the quantities of liquid wastes generated. In the various types of manufacturing facilities and the different types of processes used, there are many different liquid waste streams including, but not limited to: •Liquid wastes that are hazardous, flammable, corrosive, toxic, radioactive, etc. •Liquid waste streams that require no treatment prior to discharge •Liquid waste streams that require a simple pH adjustment before discharge For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright © 2024 International Society for Pharmaceutical Engineering. All rights reserved. Page 166 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Liquid waste stream that requires Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) or Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) reduction prior to discharge •Liquid waste streams that require decontamination/inactivation to reduce the biological safety risk (BSL) •Solvent waste streams that can be treated on-site, recycled, incinerated, or shipped off-site for treatment By taking a lifecycle approach to biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities, including process development, the decisions made in the early development stages or at the final manufacturing stages each have an important role to play in developing sustainable solutions to liquid waste management. The following questions should be considered to determine how early decision-making can impact the overall sustainability of a given manufacturing facility: •Can development decisions be made to avoid the use of certain solvents or chemicals which may be difficult to clean or dispose of? •Can a given solvent be recycled on-site for reuse? •Can the amount of water usage be reduced? Does a different technology (e.g., SUT) allow for the use of less water? •Is the amount of CIP adequate or too much? If so, is the amount of rinsing water adequate or too much? •Can wastewater streams be captured and recycled or reused, even if treatment may be required? •Can energy recovery be used in waste treatment solutions? •Is there a water balance, and do we know where the water goes? Biopharmaceutical and pharmaceutical companies should reduce, reuse, recycle, and reclaim liquid waste streams wherever and whenever possible. For additional information on liquid waste management, refer to the ISPE Handbook: Sustainability [96]. 10.5.3 Water Reuse Opportunities for reuse of water in a biopharmaceutical facility should be pursued for both sustainability and cost savings. Water streams in a biopharmaceutical facility that could be reused, instead of being discharged to the sewer, include reject water for reverse osmosis deionized water generation, cooling coil condensate from HV AC systems, and rainwater. Reuse of these water streams can be for grey water systems, irrigation, and cooling tower make-up. 10.6 Process Energy Environmental requirements should

could be reused, instead of being discharged to the sewer, include reject water for reverse osmosis deionized water generation, cooling coil condensate from HV AC systems, and rainwater. Reuse of these water streams can be for grey water systems, irrigation, and cooling tower make-up. 10.6 Process Energy Environmental requirements should be carefully considered to avoid specifications that are more stringent than necessary. Cleanliness, temperature, and humidity requirements should be set as liberally as practical, to avoid unnecessary energy use. A thoughtful risk assessment, rather than a dogmatic approach to space classification, can assist in maximizing sustainability. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 167 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Seek opportunities for energy recovery whenever possible and feasible. If a heated biokill system is used, consider recovering the energy from the heated waste stream in a heat exchanger to preheat a hot water stream. If a fired process steam boiler is used, consider adding a flue gas economizer and/or a flash steam condenser to recover energy that is otherwise wasted. Wherever possible, using hot water heating (rather than steam) can be advantageous. Steam losses are generally much higher than hot water, and hot water may be generated from waste heat to avoid fossil fuel boilers. For example, heat recovery chillers can be a valuable source of hot water, enhancing overall facility sustainability. WFI generation via membrane-based solutions can present the most energy efficient solutions. Confirm that membrane-based WFI generation is permissible in the applicable pharmacopeia. Similarly , the most energy efficient solutions favor ambient storage and circulation over hot WFI. Ambient systems require more frequent sanitization (when compared with hot systems) and may utilize an oxidizer (e.g., ozone) or periodic heating to control the growth of organisms and minimize biofilm contamination. Refer to the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Membrane-Based Water for Injection Systems [91] for more information. For distillation-based systems, Multi-Effect Stills (MES) represent an opportunity for energy conservation. Although WFI quality water can be achieved with only one stage, the multi-effect design improves energy recovery within the system. MES are often limited in size, and Vapor Compression (VC) stills are used for larger systems. VC stills can replace steam energy with electrical input (from compressor) and can be very energy efficient. Because these operate at lower temperatures, they also require less pre-treatment of the water (as little as softening and carbon filtration) to further enhance water and energy efficiency. The amount of pre-treatment depends on the quality of the incoming water and the recommendation of the vendors. For sterilization systems, such as in fill/finish operations, strive to minimize energy consumption by comparing alternative methods, such as clean steam, dry heat, e-beams, and irradiation. 10.7 Facility and Infrastructure Over 50% of the energy consumed by an average

quality of the incoming water and the recommendation of the vendors. For sterilization systems, such as in fill/finish operations, strive to minimize energy consumption by comparing alternative methods, such as clean steam, dry heat, e-beams, and irradiation. 10.7 Facility and Infrastructure Over 50% of the energy consumed by an average pharmaceutical facility is dedicated to the HV AC systems. [100] Optimizing the selected critical parameters and acceptance criteria for HV AC can provide a significant reduction in both energy use and greenhouse gas emissions. In the design and operation of biopharmaceutical facilities, there are several ways to reduce energy consumption, such as lowering clean room airflows (i.e., ACRs), minimizing room size, using energy recovery systems, and reducing flow during unoccupied hours. Reducing energy consumption can increase sustainability by reducing fossil fuel consumption. Refer to Chapter 9 for a more detailed discussion on energy reduction measures. 10.7.1 Minimizing Energy Usage in HVAC In designing highly efficient HVAC systems, employing a few core principles can lead to further energy reductions and achieving sustainability goals. The core principles for energy reduction include, but are not limited to: •Classification of a space should be based on real risk. Overclassifying spaces should be avoided. •Over-filtering should be avoided. A single HEPA filter can provide a greater than 4 log reduction in airborne contaminants. •Where operations are not continuous, an idle mode should be considered to reduce airflow (and possibly relax temperature requirements) when not in use or when operations are closed. Page 168 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities •Considering advanced control techniques such as discharge air dewpoint control and adaptive static pressure reset. •Considering using recirculated air wherever practical. •Considering using heat recovery from exhaust streams. •Considering the use of fresh air and exhaust for heat rejection during winter months. •Considering local recirculation systems separate from primary pressurization air to allow recirculation to be shut down during idle periods. •Managing fresh air use to provide only the required amount of fresh air when outside conditions are not near indoor conditions: -Carbon dioxide control for offices -Minimum fresh air for laboratories, based on science and risk evaluation -Minimum fresh air for manufacturing, based on pressurization, dilution of flammable vapors or dilution of cleaning chemicals •Minimizing the use of simultaneous heating and cooling (cooling with reheating): -Zone air handling to areas with similar heat load and environmental needs -Design using primary/secondary systems zoned to cool to set point rather than overcooling and reheating -Utilize enthalpy energy recovery neutral air systems as primary for offices and laboratories 10.7.1.1 Classification and Airflow The level of space classification drives both airflows and filtration directly . All other factors being equal (e.g., similar products, in the same process equipment undergoing identical process steps), cleaner spaces (lower number classification) require greater airflow and higher grades of filtration. The greater airflow in cleaner spaces

and Airflow The level of space classification drives both airflows and filtration directly . All other factors being equal (e.g., similar products, in the same process equipment undergoing identical process steps), cleaner spaces (lower number classification) require greater airflow and higher grades of filtration. The greater airflow in cleaner spaces may be expressed in terms of the ventilation rate (or ACR). While this measure has been dropped by ISO, IEST, and others, USP <1116> [72] states: “Specifications for air changes per hour and air velocities are not included in ISO 14644, nor were they included in Federal Standard 209E. Typically, ISO Class 8/Class 100,000 rooms are designed to provide a minimum of 20 air changes per hour; ISO Class 7/Class 10,000 rooms are designed to provide more than 50 air changes per hour; and ISO Class 5/Class 100 clean rooms provide more than 100 air changes per hour . The design of some facility criteria may differ.” This approach is also embodied in the FDA 2004 Guidance for Industry [92], which contains a suggestion that 20 AC/hr should be adequate for ISO-8 and suggests that cleaner spaces should have higher ventilation rates. Using the ventilation rate principle to set air volumes (flow rates) in classified spaces is not efficient and often results in excessive energy use. To efficiently and sustainably design HVAC systems, the air volume utilized should be adjusted to accommodate factors such as population, gowning, ventilation effectiveness, and particle generation rate. Sophisticated cleanroom airflow calculators and CFD modeling can help designers achieve effective and efficient designs that relate directly to the process design and architectural configuration. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 169 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities 10.7.1.2 Humidification Humidification should be carefully considered as these systems consume both energy and water . These may be required for human comfort/health, control of electrostatic discharge (ESD), and process or equipment requirements. Careful consideration of only the required level of humidification is recommended. ASHRAE 55-2020 [86] and other standards, such as ISO 10551 [98] and ISO 7730 [99] offer additional guidance. 10.7.1.3 Flammable/Combustible V apors Flammable liquids and some other hazardous materials may have a high vapor pressure, emitting vapors without added energy. Alternatively, some liquids or solids will need to have energy added (e.g., by pumping) to allow the material to become airborne. A common approach to addressing these risks is to employ once-through ventilation. However, more energy efficient recirculation may be possible with appropriate LEL monitoring. 10.7.1.4 HVAC Utilities As discussed in Section 10.4, the use of hot water heating (rather than steam) can be advantageous. Consider waste heat as a potential source of heating energy. Heat recovery chillers can also be used to enhance sustainability and as a source of heating or reheating water. Generally, water cooled equipment is more efficient than air cooled equipment, but careful consideration of the sustainable balance

can be advantageous. Consider waste heat as a potential source of heating energy. Heat recovery chillers can also be used to enhance sustainability and as a source of heating or reheating water. Generally, water cooled equipment is more efficient than air cooled equipment, but careful consideration of the sustainable balance of energy , water use, and carbon footprint of the equipment is needed to ensure the choice in each application is sustainable. A significant portion of HVAC energy is consumed by pumps for chilled water, heating hot water, and energy recovery loops. Pump energy usage can be minimized by reducing pressure drops in the piping design, selecting the most hydraulically efficient pumps, increasing the system’s temperature differential, and using variable speed pumps with system pressure set points that are reset based on demand. For a more detailed discussion of HVAC, refer to Chapter 9. For additional discussion of these and other energy saving strategies, see the ISPE Good Practice Guide: Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning [52]. 10.7.2 Decarbonization When constructing a new biopharmaceutical facility, choices related to layout and materials of construction can significantly impact the carbon footprint of the facility itself, referred to as embodied carbon. The Carbon Leadership Forum [101] offers numerous tools that can be used to compare alternative buildings. In the operation of a biopharmaceutical facility, carbon emissions can be reduced by reducing the combustion of fossil fuels on-site. Typical examples are plant steam and hot water boilers fired by natural gas. Steam is typically used for process utilities such as WFI loop sanitization, clean steam generation, and humidification. Hot water is typically used for heating in HVAC systems. Backup electric generators fueled by diesel or natural gas are another source of carbon emissions. The obvious alternative for boilers is replacing natural gas combustion with electricity. Electric boilers to generate steam or hot water, heat pumps to generate hot water, or heat pumps with steam compressors to generate high pressure steam can be considered. Another option is switching to renewable gas, which is methane derived from wastewater plants, landfills, and animal waste; hence, it is not a fossil fuel. Although combustion of renewable gas generates carbon dioxide, it eliminates the emission of methane to the atmosphere that would otherwise have occurred, and it reduces greenhouse gas emissions, because methane has about 25 times more greenhouse effect than carbon dioxide. The Coalition for Renewable Natural Gas [102] can provide more details on this option. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. When considering these alternatives, it is important to accurately quantify carbon emissions, including both emissions on-site and emissions from the electricity grid off-site. Although it varies from place to place, a typical value for

generators, but there may be space and cost constraints making their use impractical, if not impossible. When considering these alternatives, it is important to accurately quantify carbon emissions, including both emissions on-site and emissions from the electricity grid off-site. Although it varies from place to place, a typical value for the percentage of grid electricity that is not generated by fossil fuel is around 50%. Plans are underway to generate more grid electricity from renewable sources, but that effort will take time in developing. The fossil fuel contribution from the grid can be decreased by on-site generation of electricity by wind or solar, or by contracting with a different electricity supplier that is more renewable. ASHRAE 105 (Standard Methods of Determining, Expressing, and Comparing Building Energy Performance and Greenhouse Gas Emissions) [86] is one way to quantify carbon emissions. ASHRAE 105 has a table that lists the sources of energy that a plant can use, both imported from off-site and generated on-site. For each source, there is an assigned Greenhouse Gas Emission Factor in units of kg of CO 2e/kWh. (CO 2e means greenhouse gas emissions in kg of CO 2 equivalent). When multiplied by the annual energy consumption for that source (kWh/yr), the total greenhouse gas emissions can be calculated (kg/yr). Comparison of different carbon reduction alternatives can then be quantified. For example, consider the replacement of a natural gas-fired steam boiler with an electric boiler . The emission factor for the natural gas-fired steam boiler would be approximately 0.26 kg/kWh. If all the electricity is from the grid, and if we assume the grid is about 50% fossil-fueled using gas turbine generators with 35% thermal efficiency, then the emission factor for the electric boiler would be 0.30 kg/kWh, due to the grid. Therefore, in this example, an electric steam boiler would actually increase overall carbon emissions. However, the electric boiler lends itself to the benefit of future grid improvements. These complexities and uncertainties can easily complicate decisions regarding sustainability . In another example, suppose a natural gas-fired hot water boiler is replaced with an air-to-water heat pump that has an average COP of 3.0. The emission factor for the natural gas-fired hot water steam boiler would be approximately 0.23 kg/kWh. The emission factor for the heat pump would be 0.10 kg/kWh, due to the grid, which results in reduced carbon emissions. For a discussion of the pros and cons of various strategies for decarbonizing an existing biotech facility, refer to Bozenhardt and Bozenhardt, 2022 [103]. 10.7.3 Facility Construction A few simple, although not necessarily easy, principles are recommended to enhance sustainability in construction: •Utilize local materials whenever possible •Utilize renewable or recycled materials whenever possible •Minimize the use of volatile organic compounds in construction •Utilize sustainable or renewable energy wherever possible •Minimize waste from packaging and scrapping •Design for energy efficiency •Select materials and equipment with a lifecycle view of their environmental footprint •Reuse of existing facilities, sites, and systems is usually more sustainable than building new •Design in a manner that is harmonious with the surrounding environment: -Consider sun

ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 171 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities - Consider the impervious area and impact on rainwater - Consider the impact of site selection on workforce energy use - Consider opportunities for energy generation to offset use In selecting architectural materials, the sustainability of the material itself and its overall supply chain should be considered. Generally, locally sourced materials have a lower carbon footprint than those from a remote source. Similarly, renewable and recycled materials are usually more sustainable than synthesized or extracted materials. The preceding principles can be challenging in the construction of cleanrooms since biopharmaceutical cleanrooms are designed to minimize the possibility of microbial growth on finishes and substrates. Locally sourced natural materials, such as wood and bamboo, are sustainable choices for offices and laboratories, but are not suitable for cleanrooms. For instance, natural rubber is a potential finish in cleanrooms and can be sustainably sourced. However, it may not be the most sustainable choice due to transport issues for example. Recycled plastics such as polyethylene and PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) and solid surfaces containing recycled aggregates (e.g., concrete, epoxy flooring, epoxy counters) may be useful. Management of waste generated during construction is another way to contribute to the sustainability of a facility, reducing the environmental impact and waste generated by construction. 10.7.4 Refrigerants Refrigerants are used in cooling equipment for HVAC (e.g., chillers and direct expansion air handlers) and processes (e.g., lyophilizers). It has been recognized that many of these refrigerants, when leaked into the atmosphere, damage the ozone layer. To address this problem, the Montreal Protocol [104], an international treaty, was adopted in 1987. As a result, refrigerants with high Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) were phased out, beginning with chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs, e.g., R-11 and R-12) and later with hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs, e.g., R-22 and R-123). These were primarily replaced with hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs, e.g., R-134a, R-404A, R-497A, and R-410A). But then it was recognized that the HFCs had high Global Warming Potential (GWP), which can be quantified with reference to carbon dioxide. For example, R-134a has a GWP value of 1430, which means that one molecule of R-134a has 1430 times the GWP of one molecule of carbon dioxide. To solve this problem, new refrigerants have been developed, such as HFOs (hydrofluoro-olefins, e.g., R-1234yf and R-1234ze) and HCFOs (hydrochlorofluoro-olefins, e.g., R-1233zd), and HFO blends, which have ultra-low GWPs and are non-ozone depleting. The schedule for adopting these new refrigerants varies from country to country .

To solve this problem, new refrigerants have been developed, such as HFOs (hydrofluoro-olefins, e.g., R-1234yf and R-1234ze) and HCFOs (hydrochlorofluoro-olefins, e.g., R-1233zd), and HFO blends, which have ultra-low GWPs and are non-ozone depleting. The schedule for adopting these new refrigerants varies from country to country . Consider the GWP of any refrigerants selected for a new facility when estimating improvements in sustainability. Note that even though the GWP of a refrigerant may be many times higher than carbon dioxide; the amount of refrigerant that is released to the atmosphere is negligible compared to carbon dioxide since refrigerant circuits are tight, closed systems. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 187 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Appendix 4 SUT Single-Use Technology TACT Time, Action, Chemistry, Temperature TAL Temperature Airlock TFF Tangential Flow Filtration TIC Total Installed Cost TOC Total Organic Carbon UC Unclassified UF UltrafiltrationULPA Ultra Low Particulate Air UPS Uninterrupted Power Supply UPVC Unplasticized Polyvinyl Chloride URB User Requirement Brief URS User Requirement Specification US United States USP United States Pharmacopeia UV Ultraviolet VC Vapor Compression VHP Vaporized Hydrogen Peroxide VVM Vessel Volumes per Minute WAL Waste Airlock WFI Water for Injection WHO World Health Organization WIP Work-in-Progress 14.2 Definitions Aseptic Operations Processes that are devoid of measurable (detectable) bioburden. Aseptic operations generally require sterilization of the environment, equipment, and process solutions to achieve the sterile state prior to use. Aseptic Preparation/Processing (EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7])The handling of sterile product, containers and/or devices in a controlled environment in which the air supply, materials and personnel are regulated to prevent microbial, endotoxin/pyrogen and particle contamination. Aseptic StateA state free of contaminating organisms. Distinguished from sterile where sterile is free of any live organisms, where aseptic is free from contaminating organisms. For example, a bioreactor is sterile and aseptic before

which the air supply, materials and personnel are regulated to prevent microbial, endotoxin/pyrogen and particle contamination. Aseptic StateA state free of contaminating organisms. Distinguished from sterile where sterile is free of any live organisms, where aseptic is free from contaminating organisms. For example, a bioreactor is sterile and aseptic before inoculation, and aseptic only after inoculation). For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright © 2024 International Society for Pharmaceutical Engineering. All rights reserved. Page 188 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Appendix 4 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Aseptic Transfer Material transfer designed and performed to prevent bioburden contamination from the environment.Axenic StateA pure culture that includes the intended living organism but is entirely free of all contaminating organisms.BactericidalCondition that kills bacteria (e.g., antiseptics, antibiotics, high temperature, pH extremes, radiation).BacteriostaticInhibiting growth of bacterial organisms without necessarily killing them or their spores. Increase of bioburden is only mitigated for the duration of the operation under bacteriostatic conditions. Bioburden ControlA means of limiting or regulating microorganism populations to a level below acceptance criteria.Bioburden-FreeA condition where bioburden is not detectable. Bioreactors used in cell culture or fermentation operations should be sterile prior to use for consistent and reproducible performance. BSC, RABS, and isolators should provide an environment free of bioburden for consistent and reproducible aseptic operations. Sterile product manufacturing testing and qualification guidelines are by necessity more stringent and prescriptive than that of API manufacturing and therefore are not required. Closed Process Contaminants detected in process streams arising from insufficient removal of contaminating components from previous manufacturing steps or batches. Maximum allowable carryover residues should be estimated and meet defined criteria. Carryover is typically a result of insufficient cleaning, sanitization, rinsing, and/or conditioning. For further information, see the ISPE Baseline® Guide: Volume 7 – Risk-Based Manufacture of Pharmaceutical Products (Second Edition) [38]. Closed SystemA system that isolates the process zone from its surrounding environment and prevents ingress of environmental contaminants during product contact. The process zone is limited by the equipment. System closure requires two steps: 1.Removal of latent contaminants within the process zone to a level commensurate with the process requirements, whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden, prior to process contact 2.Prevention of ingress of environmental contaminants during process contact Note: A closed system is not necessarily a sterile system. It is closed to the appropriate condition whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. ISPE Baseline® Guide: Page 189 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Appendix 4 Contamination The introduction of an undesired level of impurities of a chemical, microbiological, or foreign matter (e.g., particles) into or onto raw materials, intermediated, or API during production, sampling, packaging, repackaging, storage, or transport with the potential to adversely impact product quality. Note: This definition is based on ICH Q7 [5] definition, with

of an undesired level of impurities of a chemical, microbiological, or foreign matter (e.g., particles) into or onto raw materials, intermediated, or API during production, sampling, packaging, repackaging, storage, or transport with the potential to adversely impact product quality. Note: This definition is based on ICH Q7 [5] definition, with the difference that “undesired introduction of impurities” has been refined with “introduction of an undesired level of impurities”. The Annex 1 [7] definition does not include chemical matter.Contamination Control Strategy (CCS) (EudraLex Volume 4 Annex 1 [7]) A planned set of controls for microorganisms, endotoxin/pyrogen and particles, derived from current product and process understanding that assures process performance and product quality. The controls can include parameters and attributes related to active substance, excipient and drug product materials and components, facility and equipment operating conditions, in-process controls, finished product specifications, and the associated methods and frequency of monitoring and control. CrossoverCross-contamination. Contamination of a system by components or contaminants found in a neighboring system. Crossover typically occurs with open processes sharing environments. Crossover also can occur when there is a breach of integrity of a closed system in an environment shared with an open process or when there is a breach of integrity of two closed processes. The latter is highly unlikely to occur in well-designed systems. Circulating CIP system and glass washers can be a source of crossover if improperly designed. Dead Leg (ASME BPE [62]) A space where system design and operating conditions result in insufficient process fluid flow, presenting a risk for particulate, chemical, or biological contamination.General ContaminationContamination of a product or processing area by materials from the ambient environment outside a controlled environment. General contaminant may be understood as “dirt” which has penetrated clean areas form the ambient environment. This does not include particles or organisms from other processes.Harm (ICH Q9 [6]) Damage to health, including the damage that can occur from loss of product quality or availability.HazardA real or potential condition, situation or agent that can cause harm to people, product, facility, or process. A hazard can cause damage or loss of a system, equipment or to the environment. The potential source of harm. (ICH Q9 [6] based upon ISO/IEC Guide 51 [85]) Low Bioburden (operation) Process where the level of bioburden is limited to user-defined criteria (as determined by risk assessment) (e.g., maintaining the bioburden in chromatography eluate to <10 CFU/100 mL). For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. Downloaded from https://guidance-docs.ispe.org/ by Adrian Fernandez on April 11, 2024. For personal use only. No other uses without permission. Copyright © 2024 International Society for Pharmaceutical Engineering. All rights reserved. Page 190 ISPE Baseline® Guide: Appendix 4 Biopharmaceutical Manufacturing Facilities Open Process (System) A process that is exposed to the environment and therefore requires environmental conditions to mitigate the risk of contamination from the environment. In the case of an open process, the environment represents a critical aspect and can have a direct impact on product quality and patient safety. Open sterile and aseptic operations must be performed in an environment where the probability of

conditions to mitigate the risk of contamination from the environment. In the case of an open process, the environment represents a critical aspect and can have a direct impact on product quality and patient safety. Open sterile and aseptic operations must be performed in an environment where the probability of contamination is acceptably low (i.e., an environment meeting the bioburden requirements for a Grade A space). Open bioburden-controlled processing may be performed in an ISO Grade 8/EU Grade C or EU Grade D environment as appropriate for the unit operation. Process ClosureProcess closure is achieved through a cleaning, sanitization, and/or sterilization process that is appropriate or consistent with the process requirements, whether sterile, aseptic, or low bioburden. These systems need to remain closed during production within the system. As a result of the process closure, the environment does not represent a critical aspect of the closed process. A closed system must be validated to show that sufficient layers of protection have been implemented to mitigate the risk of contamination from the environment. Transfers into or from these systems must also be validated as closed. Process ZoneThe environment that includes all elements (e.g., raw materials, chemicals, products, contaminants, personnel, waste) that may contact the process or product in an open or closed system. In a closed system, the process zone is limited to the equipment/component that isolates the process zone from the surrounding environment. In an open system, the process zone includes the surrounding environment. Quality Risk Management (ICH Q9 [6])A systematic process for the assessment, control, communication and review of risks to the quality of the drug (medicinal) product across the product lifecycle. SanitizationOperation that reduces the level of bioburden.Sterile/Sterility Free from living organizations. The Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) requirements are different for drug substances and drug products.Sterilization Form of sanitization that results in a bioburden-free (sterile) condition. The different sterilization methods used in bioprocessing generally include thermal sterilization (121°C/15 minutes or higher), sterile filtration (0.2 µm or better), chemical sterilization (e.g., VHP, chlorine dioxide, ozone) or irradiation (e.g., gamma, e-beam, x-ray, UV) to achieve the sterile state. Surrounding Environment (Manufacturing Environment)The space around the equipment/component housing a process. It includes the conditions, equipment, personnel, materials, and waste occupying that space. In open processing, the surrounding environment constitutes part of the process zone. In closed processing, the surrounding environment is outside the process zone. For individual use only. © 2023 ISPE. All rights reserved. # Sheet: Layout 설계 원리 | 항목 | 원리 | 전체적 배열 컨셉 | Layout 에서 가장 첫 번째 단계로 고려할 조건은 건축물의 원자재 반입구와 폐기물 반출구의 위치를 확인하는 것이다. 특히 높은 층의 경우 엘리베이터의 위치를 확인한다. 원자재 반입구에 가까운 쪽에서 공정을 시작하여 폐기물 반출구 쪽에서 공정이 완료되면 불필요한 물질의 동선을 최소화 할 수 있다. 그러나, 이것이 Room 배열의 가장 우선순위는 아니며 적용하고자 하는 제조공정의 특성에 맞게 변형 가능하다. 전체 배열은 크게 공정 구역과 보조구역으로 나눌 수 있다. 보조구역은 갱의실, 보관실과 같이 제조행위가 직접 이루어 지지 않는 구역이라 할 수 있으며, 청정등급은 Grade D, CNC 와 같이 낮은 등급을 갖으며 보통 입구에 가까운 쪽에 위치한다. 사람 및 물품의 동선은 보조구역을 거쳐서 공정구역으로 진입하게 되며, 점진적으로 청정등급이 높아지므로 사람은 갱의실을 통해 깨끗한 복장으로 착복을 하고, 자재 등 물품은 표면 소독을 통해 오염원을 제거하여 공정구역으로 반입을 한다. 일반적으로 Layout 의 중앙에 공급 복도 (supply corridor) 가 위치하며, 주요 공정 Room 을 거쳐 테두리 부분에 리턴 복도 (Return corridor) 가 위치한다. 즉, 사람, 공정물질, 폐기물 등으로부터 교차오염을 억제하기 위해 공급 복도 → 공정 Room → 리턴 복도의 순서로 편도 (one-way) 개념으로 사람과 물품의 이동이 이루어지게 된다. mAb 제조라인에서는 제조공정 상 Virus filtration 수행 전 공정 단계인 Seed 접종실, 배양실, 회수실, 정제1실은 이와 같은 편도 (one-way) Flow 를 따르는 것이 바람직하다. | Room 구성 | GMP 관리구역의 모든 Room 은 청소 및 동선 관리가 쉬운 구조인 직사각형 (정사각형 포함) 으로 구성하되, 공간 구조 상 필요한 경우 여러 개의 직사각형의 합으로 이루어진 도형으로 구성할 수 있다. 하나의 Room 은 하나의 오픈된 Room 공간을 갖는다. 만약, Room 이 벽체와 door 로 분리된 경우 각 분리된 Room 은 별도의 Room 명을 부여한다. Room 의 면적은 가로와 세로의 곱으로 표시되며, 단위는 일반적으로 설계 단계에서는 밀리미터 (mm) 를 사용한다. Room 면적에 Room 의 천정 높이를 곱하면 체적이 된다. 단, 각 Room 마다 우물형 천정 (well type ceiling) 이 있을 수 있으므로, 감안하여 체적을 구할 수 있다. Room 의 체적은 설계 시 청정등급에 따른 환기횟수를 결정하는 인자가 된다. 모든 Room 에는 도어가 있으며, Room 의 용도에 따라 한개에서 4개 이상의 도어를 설치할 수 있다. 일반적으로는 하나의 Room 에 입구 부분과 출구 부분이 있으며, 또는 입구와 출구를 공유하여 사용할 수 있으며, 이것은 Room 의 용도, 청정등급 및 공정 특수성에 따라 다를 수 있다. 공정 Room 은 목적에 따라 하나의 도어로 연결되는 부속 Room 을 갖을 수 있으며, 사람과 물품이 자유로이 왕래를 할 수 있다. | Room 크기 | Room 의 크기는 각 Room 내부의 장비 규격 및 배열에 따라 결정한다. 일반적으로 주요 제조공정 (배지 및 버퍼 제조, 배양, 정제 등) 을 처리하는 공정 Room 들의 합은 layout 전체 면적의 40~70% 를 차지하도록 구성하며 그 비율은 생산 최대 가동률 (다품목 생산, 공정 overlap 생산) 이 높은 제조라인의 경우 더 높게 가져갈 필요가 있다. 복도(corridor) 의 경우 길고 좁은 형태를 이루며, 폭(width) 은 일반적으로 2000mm ~ 3000mm 사이가 적당하나, 보통 복도는 전체 작업장의 큰 비율을 차지하므로 면적이 부족할 경우 그 폭을 2000mm 보다 작게 구성할 수 있으나, 가급적 최소 1500mm 이상은 확보하는 것이 바람직하다. 전실 (Air-Lock) 의 경우 그 용도에 따라 크기를 정할 수 있지만, 일반적으로 제조소의 전체 면적이 넉넉할 경우 가로 3000mm 세로 3000mm 이상의 크기를 확보하는 것이 바람직하며 면적이 부족한 경우 각각 사람과

차지하므로 면적이 부족할 경우 그 폭을 2000mm 보다 작게 구성할 수 있으나, 가급적 최소 1500mm 이상은 확보하는 것이 바람직하다. 전실 (Air-Lock) 의 경우 그 용도에 따라 크기를 정할 수 있지만, 일반적으로 제조소의 전체 면적이 넉넉할 경우 가로 3000mm 세로 3000mm 이상의 크기를 확보하는 것이 바람직하며 면적이 부족한 경우 각각 사람과 물건이 충분히 통과할 수 있는 정도의 크기가 되어야 한다. 천정의 높이는 일반적인 Room 은 청소 용이성 및 에너지 소비를 감안하여 2700~3000mm 가 적당하나, Room 에 배치되는 제조 장비의 높이를 고려하여 천정 높이를 결정한다. 단, Room 의 면적이 넓은 경우, 특별히 높은 장비의 천정 높이만 높이는 우물형 천정(well type ceiling) 으로 설계할 수 있다. 생산 배양기 (10,000L) 같은 경우, 배양기의 하단 스페이스는 기계실 (service area) 에 위치하게 하고, 배양기의 head space 만 배양실로 노출하여 청정도 관리구역의 공간을 save 할 수 있다. | Room 에 대한 청정등급의 부여 | Room 의 청정등급은 가이드라인 'EU GMP Annex 1' 을 따라 (1)Grade A, (2)Grade B, (3)Grade C, (4)Grade D, (5)CNC, (6)NC 로 구분한다. 바이알 충전과 같이 무균공정을 요구하는 공정의 barrier (Isolator, Rapps, Cleanbench, BSC 와 같은 laminar flow 층류장치)는 Grade A 를 적용한다. Grade A 층류 장치가 설치된, 둘러싼 Room 은 Grade B 로 적용한다. 주요 공정이 이루어지는 구역은 Grade C 또는 Grade D 를 적용한다. 만약 해당 Room 의 모든 제조공정이 외부와 밀폐된 폐쇄형 제조장비를 사용하는 경우 Grade D 를 적용할 수 있다. 배지 및 버퍼의 제조, 배양 공정, 정제 공정과 같은 주요 제조공정이 이루어 지지 않는 구역은 Grade D 또는 CNC 로 적용한다. Grade D 또는 CNC 청정등급은 자재의 보관 구역, 세척 구역, 갱의 구역, 공정검사 (IPC) 구역, 주요 제조공정 Room 이 아닌 지역의 사람 및 자재 등의 이동 통로에 적용한다. 제조행위 및 제조환경에 영향을 주지 않고 동선 상 상관없는 구역은 NC(None controlled area) 로 분류한다. Layout 에서 Room 의 배경색은 다음과 같이 표시하되, 제조 장비가 잘 보일 수 있도록 50% 투명하게 나타낸다. (1)Grade A: Green-diagnal Black, (2)Grade B: Green, (3)Grade C: yellow, (4)Grade D: Blue, (5)CNC: Gray-dotted Black, (6)NC: Gray | Room 의 배열 | Layout 은 크게 공정 구역과 보조 구역으로 나눈다. 공정 구역은 직접 제조 행위가 발생하는 제조공정 Room 과 세척 및 준비실과 같이 제조 준비를 위한 Room, 인원과 material 의 반입 출입을 위하여 동선 상 필요한 Room (복도 포함) 으로 구성된다. 보조 구역은 cell bank, 공정물 (Drug substance 포함), 시약 및 자재의 보관을 위한 Room 들과 공정 Room 의 배치는 제조공정의 흐름 (process flow) 을 우선으로 따른다. Room 내의 도어의 위치는 자재 및 사람의 출입실 동선이 짧은 위치에 설치한다. | 전실의 배열 | 전실(AL, Air Lock)은 청정등급이 바뀌거나 공정 중요도 구분 필요한 두 Room 사이에 위치해야 한다. 하나의 공정 Room 기준으로 편도(one-way) flow 가 적용되는 경우는 입구의 반대편 위치에 출구가 별도로 위치해야 하며, 전실도 마찬가지로 입구의 전실과 출구의 전실이 각각 배치되어야 한다. 전실은 사람의 출입을 위한 PAL (Personel Air Lock) 과 자재 및 제품의 출입을 위한 MAL (Material Air Lock) 으로 구분하며 하나의 Room 에는 최대 총 4개의 전실(PAL-in, MAL-in, PAL-out, MAL-out)이 배열될 수 있다. Grade B 청정등급의 공정 Room 은 one-way flow 로서 4개의 전실이 필요하며, Grade C 청정등급의 공정 Room 은 만약 편도 동선 (one-way flow) 의 조건이 라면 전체 공간이 넉넉한 경우 총 4개의 전실이 가장 좋으나,

으로 구분하며 하나의 Room 에는 최대 총 4개의 전실(PAL-in, MAL-in, PAL-out, MAL-out)이 배열될 수 있다. Grade B 청정등급의 공정 Room 은 one-way flow 로서 4개의 전실이 필요하며, Grade C 청정등급의 공정 Room 은 만약 편도 동선 (one-way flow) 의 조건이 라면 전체 공간이 넉넉한 경우 총 4개의 전실이 가장 좋으나, 공간이 부족한 경우는 PAL과 MAL 이 통합된 AL-in, AL-out 의 2개의 전실도 가능하다. 만약 Room 이 양방향 flow 인 경우 하나의 AL 도 가능하다. 전실은 양쪽 Room 의 청정등급이 바뀌지 않거나 공정의 중요도 구분이 필요 없을 경우 배치하지 않아도 된다. 전실(Air Lock)은 인접한 Room 기준으로 더 높은 차압의 Room > 전실 > 더 낮은 차압의 Room 순서로 공기가 흐르는 (1) Cascade 타입, 양쪽 인접 Room 기준으로 더 낮은 압력을 형성하는 (2) Sink 타입과 양쪽 인접 Room 기준으로 더 높은 압력을 형성하는 (3) Bubble 타입의 전실(Air Lock) 을 적절히 선택할 수 있다. 예를 들어 Grade C 구역과 Grade D 구역 사이의 전실은 Cascade 흐름으로 Grade C → 전실 → Grade D 순서로 공기가 흐르도록 설계할 수 있으나, 만약 Grade C 구역의 공기가 Grade D 구역으로 흘러 들어가면 안되는 경우 (Biological safety 이유 등) 전실(Air Lock) 을 Sink 타입 또는 Bubble 타입으로 설치할 수 있다. | 복도의 배열 | 복도 (corridor) 는 인원의 입실과 자재의 반입을 담당하는 공급복도 (supply corridor) 와 인원의 퇴실과 폐기물의 반출을 목적으로 하는 리턴복도 (Return corridor) 로 구분한다. 공정 Room 이 편도(one-way) flow 인 경우, 출구의 전실은 리턴복도로 연결되어야 한다. 일반적으로 복도는 공급복도와 리턴복도는 직접적으로 연결되어 있지 않다. 이는 청정등급이 낮은 리턴복도에서 청정등급이 높은 청정복도로 인원 또는 오염된 물품이 들어와 교차오염(cross contamination) 시키는 것을 원천적으로 예방하기 위함이다. 따라서, 공정룸을 거쳐 리턴복도로 퇴실한 인원은 리턴복도를 따라 보조구역 (Grade D 또는 CNC) 으로 나와야 한다. | 도어의 위치 | 도어(siwing door) 의 열림 방향은 가급적 열었던 문이 자연적으로 견고히 닫힐 수 있도록 Room 간 차압이 흐르는 방향에 대응하는 방향으로 열리도록 설치한다. 도어는 1000mm 를 기준으로 반영하되 그 용도에 따라 큰 물품이 드나드는 MAL 의 경우 더 큰 도어를 설치한다. | 제조 장비의 배치 | 제조 장비는 제조 공정이 진행되는 순서대로 배치하는 것이 바람직하며, 각 장비간 작업 및 청소 공간에 간섭이 없도록 충분한 공간을 두도록 하며 최소 1000mm 의 공간을 두는 것이 바람직하다. 제조 장비는 전기의 공급 및 가스 등 유틸리티의 공급이 유리한 벽면에 인접하여 설치를 하되 장비와 벽면의 청소 공간을 고려하여 최소 600mm~1200mm 정도의 공간을 띄우도록 설치한다. 만약, 천정형으로부터 전기 및 유틸리티를 공급받는 경우 장비를 벽면이 아닌 Room 중앙 부분에 설치할 수 있다. | 세척실과 준비실 | 세척실은 각 공정실의 Out 전실과 연결된 리턴복도와 경로가 통해 있으며, 세척실과 준비실은 인접하게 위치하여 패스쓰루 세척기 (Pass through COP) 를 공유하도록 배열한다. 주의할 점은 준비실은 공급복도와 경로가 통해 있기 때문에, 세척실과 준비실은 사람의 직접 왕래가 물리적으로 차단되어 있어야 한다. | NC 구역 | 상대적으로 중요도가 떨어지는 NC 구역이지만, 화장실, 사무실, 모니터링 및 제어실과 같이 반드시 필요한 기능의 Room 의 경우 공정구역 및 보조구역의 전체적 형태에 방해가 되지 않도록 별도의 영역을 구분하여 배치하는 것이 바람직하다. 단, 사무실, 화장실, 휴게실, 중앙 제어실이 별도의 층 또는 건물에 설치할 계획이 있을 경우 생략할 수 있다. | 차압(DP) 규정 | 각 Room 별로 차압을 표시한다. 차압은 Pa (pascal) 단위를 사용하며, 외부 대기압을 0 으로 기준하여, 각 Room 별 압력을 표시하여야

방해가 되지 않도록 별도의 영역을 구분하여 배치하는 것이 바람직하다. 단, 사무실, 화장실, 휴게실, 중앙 제어실이 별도의 층 또는 건물에 설치할 계획이 있을 경우 생략할 수 있다. | 차압(DP) 규정 | 각 Room 별로 차압을 표시한다. 차압은 Pa (pascal) 단위를 사용하며, 외부 대기압을 0 으로 기준하여, 각 Room 별 압력을 표시하여야 한다. 제조소의 Room 간 차압은 'EU GMP 가이드라인 Annex 1' 규정에 따라 교차 오염 방지를 위해 청정등급이 다른 Room 간에 최소 10~15Pa 의 압력차를 유지 필요하다. 더 높은 청정등급의 Room 은 인접한 낮은 등급의 Room 보다 높은 차압을 형성하도록 Room 의 급기와 배기를 조합하여야 한다. (Grade B > Grad C > Grade D > CNC > NC) 만약 동일한 청정등급의 Room 인 경우, 차압을 형성하지 않아도 되지만 만약 Virus Removal 전/후의 정제실1, 정제실2의 경우 같은 청정등급을 갖는 Room 이더라도 그 중요도에 따라 정제2실에 최소 0.5Pa 의 더 높은 차압을 정의할 수 있다. # Sheet: Room 범례 | [설계 기준 : mAb 8,000L culture Line 기준, 전체 layout 면적 3300m2 기준] | | | | | | | | | | | | 구분 | Room 명 | (영문명) | 청정등급 (Classification) | 권장 면적 (m2) | 면적 비율 (전체 대비) | 공정 구역 | 배지 조제실 | Media preparation | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 100 | 0.030303030303030304 | 공정 구역 | 버퍼 조제실 | Buffer preparation | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 200 | 0.06060606060606061 | 공정 구역 | Seed 접종실 | Inoculation | Grade B 또는 Grade C | 40 | 0.012121212121212121 | 공정 구역 | 배양실1 | Seed train | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 150 | 0.045454545454545456 | 공정 구역 | 배양실2 | Cell Culture | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 150 | 0.045454545454545456 | 공정 구역 | 회수실 | Harvest | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 60 | 0.01818181818181818 | 공정 구역 | 정제실1 | Purification 1 | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 300 | 0.09090909090909091 | 공정 구역 | 정제실2 | Purification 2 | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 100 | 0.030303030303030304 | 공정 구역 | 공정 준비실 | Preparation | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 80 | 0.024242424242424242 | 공정 구역 | 세척실 | Washing | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 60 | 0.01818181818181818 | 공정 구역 | 공급복도 | Supply corridor | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 150 | 0.045454545454545456 | 공정 구역 | 복도 | Return corridor | Grade D 또는 CNC | 250 | 0.07575757575757576 | 공정 구역 | 착의실 | Gowning | Grade B 또는 Grade C | 30 | 0.00909090909090909 | 공정 구역 | 전실 | CAL (Common Air Lock) | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 16 | 0.0048484848484848485 | 공정 구역 | 전실 | CAL-in (Common Air Lock in) | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 16 | 0.0048484848484848485 | 공정 구역 | 전실 | CAL-out (Common Air Lock out) | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 16 | 0.0048484848484848485 | 공정 구역 | 전실 | MAL (Material Air Lock) | Grade C 또는 Grade D | 16 | 0.0048484848484848485 | 공정 구역 | 전실 | MAL-in (Material

# GMP Layout Design Decision Tree **대상**: mAb 8,000L culture Line (전체 면적 3,300 m² 기준) **출처**: GMP_Layout_Logic_0510.xlsx — Layout 설계 원리 시트 (12개 항목) **버전**: v0.1 (개념설계 수준) --- ## 0. 트리 구조의 표기 규칙 - `Phase N` : 설계 진행 단계 (직렬 수행) - `D-x.y` : Decision Node (조건 분기) - `A-x.y` : Action Node (Drawing 행위) - `IF … → …` : 조건부 분기 - `[INPUT]` : 외부 입력값, `[OUTPUT]` : 산출물 --- ## Phase 1. 사전조건 분석 (Pre-condition Analysis) ``` D-1.1 건축물 인터페이스 확인 ├─ [INPUT] 원자재 반입구 좌표 (RAW_IN) ├─ [INPUT] 폐기물 반출구 좌표 (WASTE_OUT) └─ IF 층수 ≥ 2 → [INPUT] 엘리베이터 좌표 (ELEV) D-1.2 전체 면적/규모 확인 ├─ [INPUT] TOTAL_AREA (default 3,300 m²) ├─ [INPUT] PROCESS_SCALE = "mAb 8,000L culture" └─ [INPUT] OVERLAP_PRODUCTION (Y/N, 다품목·공정 overlap 여부) A-1.3 전역 좌표계 설정 └─ 단위: mm, 원점: 건축물 외곽 좌하단 ``` --- ## Phase 2. Zoning (구역 분할) ``` D-2.1 3대 구역 분할 ├─ 공정구역 (Process Area) │ ├─ 직접 제조 Room (배지/버퍼/배양/회수/정제/세척/준비/공급복도 등) │ └─ 면적 비율: │ IF OVERLAP_PRODUCTION == Y → 60~70% × TOTAL_AREA │ ELSE → 40~50% × TOTAL_AREA ├─ 보조구역 (Support Area) │ ├─ Cell bank / DS / 자재 / 장비 보관실, 갱의실, IPC, CIP 등 │ ├─ 청정등급: Grade D 또는 CNC │ └─ 위치: 입구(RAW_IN)에 가까운 쪽 └─ NC구역 ├─ 화장실, 사무실, 모니터링/제어실, 로비 └─ IF 별도 층/건물에 배치 계획 있음 → 본 Layout에서 생략 D-2.2 전체 동선 컨셉 ├─ 시작: RAW_IN 근접 (배지/버퍼 조제) ├─ 종료: WASTE_OUT 근접 (정제 후/DS 보관) └─ 단, Room 배열의 절대 우선순위 아님 (공정 특성 우선) A-2.3 복도 골격 배치 ├─ Supply Corridor → 중앙 └─ Return Corridor → 테두리(perimeter) ``` --- ## Phase 3. Room 청정등급 부여 (Clean Grade Classification) EU GMP Annex 1: **(1) Grade A, (2) B, (3) C, (4) D, (5) CNC, (6) NC** ``` FOR EACH Room r: D-3.1 무균공정 여부 판정 ├─ IF r에 층류장치(Isolator/RABS/Cleanbench/BSC) 존재 │ → r 내 barrier = Grade A │ → barrier 둘러싼 Room 본체 = Grade B │ └─ ELSE → D-3.2 D-3.2 주요 제조공정 여부 ├─ IF r ∈ {배지·버퍼 조제, 배양, 정제 …} (주요 공정) │ ├─ IF 모든 제조공정이 폐쇄형(closed) 장비 → Grade D │ └─ ELSE → Grade C │ └─ ELSE → D-3.3 D-3.3 보조 기능 Room 판정 ├─ IF r ∈ {자재 보관, 세척, 갱의, IPC, 통로(corridor)} │ → Grade D 또는 CNC │ ├─ IF r ∈ {화장실, 사무실, 모니터링실, 로비} │ → NC │ └─ ELSE (모호 시) → Grade D (보수적) A-3.4 Layout 색상 부여 (50% 투명도) ├─ Grade A : Green-diagonal Black ├─ Grade B : Green ├─ Grade C : Yellow ├─ Grade D : Blue ├─ CNC : Gray-dotted Black └─ NC : Gray ``` ### 참고 권장 청정등급 (Room 범례 시트 기반) | Room | 청정등급 | 권장면적(m²) | |---|---|---| | 배지 조제실 | C 또는 D | 100 | | 버퍼 조제실 | C 또는 D | 200 | | Seed 접종실 | B 또는 C | 40 | | 배양실1 (Seed train) | C 또는 D

(Room 범례 시트 기반) | Room | 청정등급 | 권장면적(m²) | |---|---|---| | 배지 조제실 | C 또는 D | 100 | | 버퍼 조제실 | C 또는 D | 200 | | Seed 접종실 | B 또는 C | 40 | | 배양실1 (Seed train) | C 또는 D | 150 | | 배양실2 (Cell Culture) | C 또는 D | 150 | | 회수실 (Harvest) | C 또는 D | 60 | | 정제실1 (Purification 1) | C 또는 D | 300 | | 정제실2 (Purification 2) | C 또는 D | 100 | | 공정 준비실 | C 또는 D | 80 | | 세척실 | C 또는 D | 60 | | 공급복도 | C 또는 D | 150 | | 리턴복도 | D 또는 CNC | 250 | | Cell bank 보관실 | D 또는 CNC | 20 | | DS 보관실 | D 또는 CNC | 50 | --- ## Phase 4. Room 크기 결정 (Room Sizing) ``` FOR EACH Room r: D-4.1 Room 형상 ├─ Default → 직사각형(정사각형 포함) └─ IF 공간 구조상 불가 → 직사각형 합으로 구성 D-4.2 Room 내부 면적 산정 ├─ 입력: 장비 목록 (제조장비 범례 시트 참조) │ e.g. 배양실 ← Wave Reactor 20L, BioReactor 100/500/2000/10000L, Storage 2000L ├─ 산식: Area_min = Σ(장비 footprint) + 장비간 간격(≥1000mm) + 벽-장비(600~1200mm) └─ A-4.2 r.area = max(Area_min, 권장면적) D-4.3 복도 폭(width) 결정 ├─ Default 2000~3000 mm ├─ IF 전체 면적 부족 → 1500~2000 mm 허용 └─ Hard min = 1500 mm D-4.4 전실(Air-Lock) 크기 ├─ IF 면적 여유 → 3000 × 3000 mm (이상) └─ ELSE → 사람·물품 통과 가능한 최소 크기 (e.g. PAL 12 m², MAL 16 m²) D-4.5 천정 높이(H) 결정 ├─ Default 2700 ~ 3000 mm ├─ IF 장비 높이 > 2700 mm → 우물형 천정(well type ceiling) └─ IF 장비 = 10,000L bioreactor → 하단 service area, head만 Room 노출 A-4.6 체적 산정 └─ Volume = Area × H (well type 천정 시 부분 보정) → 환기횟수(ACPH) 결정 인자 Grade A: 0.36~0.54 m/s (풍속) Grade B: 40~60 ACPH Grade C: 20~40 ACPH Grade D: 6~20 ACPH D-4.7 주요 공정 Room 합산 면적 검증 └─ IF Σ(주요 공정 Room 면적) ∉ [40%, 70%] × TOTAL_AREA → 면적 재조정 루프 (D-4.2 회귀) ``` --- ## Phase 5. Room 배열 (Room Arrangement — Process Flow 기반) ``` A-5.1 공정 순서대로 1차 정렬 mAb 공정 순서: Cell bank storage → Media prep → Buffer prep → Inoculation(Seed) → Cell Culture(배양1·2) → Harvest(회수) → Purification 1 → [Virus Filtration] ← 경계 → Purification 2 → DS storage D-5.2 One-way (편도) Flow 적용 구간 판정 ├─ Virus Filtration **前** 공정 Room (Seed 접종실, 배양실, 회수실, 정제실1) │ → One-way Flow (입구 ≠ 출구) │ → 입구는 Supply Corridor 측, 출구는 Return Corridor 측 │ └─ Virus Filtration **後** 공정 Room (정제실2, DS 등) → 양방향 가능 A-5.3 Room 도어 위치 1차 결정 └─ 자재/사람 출입 동선이 최단이 되는 변(edge)에 도어 부착 D-5.4 부속(Adjacent) Room 필요 여부 └─ IF 공정 Room이 부속실 필요(e.g. 정제실1 내 부분 격리) → 단일 도어로 연결, 사람·물품 자유 왕래 ``` --- ##

Filtration **後** 공정 Room (정제실2, DS 등) → 양방향 가능 A-5.3 Room 도어 위치 1차 결정 └─ 자재/사람 출입 동선이 최단이 되는 변(edge)에 도어 부착 D-5.4 부속(Adjacent) Room 필요 여부 └─ IF 공정 Room이 부속실 필요(e.g. 정제실1 내 부분 격리) → 단일 도어로 연결, 사람·물품 자유 왕래 ``` --- ## Phase 6. 전실(Air Lock) 배치 ``` FOR EACH 인접 Room 쌍 (r1, r2): D-6.1 AL 필요 여부 ├─ IF Grade(r1) ≠ Grade(r2) → AL 필요 ├─ IF 공정 중요도 구분 필요 (e.g. Virus 前후) → AL 필요 └─ ELSE → AL 생략 D-6.2 AL 개수 결정 (해당 공정 Room 기준) ├─ IF Grade(r) == B AND one-way │ → 4개 (PAL-in, MAL-in, PAL-out, MAL-out) │ ├─ ELIF Grade(r) == C AND one-way │ ├─ IF 공간 여유 → 4개 (PAL-in, MAL-in, PAL-out, MAL-out) │ └─ ELSE → 2개 (AL-in, AL-out ※ PAL+MAL 통합) │ └─ ELIF 양방향 Flow → 1개 (AL) D-6.3 AL 타입 (압력 패턴) 결정 ├─ Default → Cascade │ 공기 흐름: 高차압 Room → AL → 低차압 Room │ ├─ IF Biological safety로 r1↔r2 공기 격리 필요 │ ├─ AL이 양쪽보다 낮은 압력 → Sink (양쪽 오염원 차단) │ └─ AL이 양쪽보다 높은 압력 → Bubble (외부 침입 차단) A-6.4 AL과 복도 연결 규칙 ├─ One-way Room의 입구 측 AL → Supply Corridor 연결 └─ One-way Room의 출구 측 AL → Return Corridor 연결 D-6.5 AL Grade 부여 ├─ MAL-in/out, PAL-in/out (Grade B 인접) → B 또는 C 또는 D ├─ CAL (Common AL) → C 또는 D └─ 보조구역 진입 AL → CNC 또는 NC ``` --- ## Phase 7. 복도(Corridor) 설계 ``` D-7.1 복도 유형 분리 ├─ Supply Corridor : 인원 입실 + 자재 반입 (청정도 ↑) └─ Return Corridor : 인원 퇴실 + 폐기물 반출 (청정도 ↓) A-7.2 복도 연결 규칙 (HARD CONSTRAINT) ├─ Supply와 Return은 **직접 연결 금지** │ → 교차오염(cross contamination) 원천 차단 │ └─ Return Corridor 끝단 → 보조구역(Grade D 또는 CNC)으로 출구 D-7.3 복도 폭 └─ 2000~3000 mm (최소 1500 mm) ``` --- ## Phase 8. 도어(Door) 설계 ``` FOR EACH Door d on Room r: D-8.1 도어 폭 ├─ Default 1000 mm (swing door) └─ IF d ∈ MAL (자재용) → 1500 ~ 2000 mm A-8.2 도어 열림 방향 └─ Room 간 차압이 흐르는 방향으로 열림 (자연적으로 견고히 닫히도록 — passive fail-safe) A-8.3 도어 위치 └─ Room 내·외 동선 최단 위치에 부착 D-8.4 Room별 도어 개수 └─ 용도에 따라 1 ~ 4개 이상 ``` --- ## Phase 9. 제조 장비 배치 (Equipment Placement) ``` FOR EACH Room r: A-9.1 공정 순서대로 장비 배치 └─ 입력: 제조장비 범례 시트의 "단위 공정" 컬럼 순 D-9.2 장비 간 간격 └─ ≥ 1000 mm (작업 및 청소 공간 확보) D-9.3 장비-벽 간격 ├─ 600 ~ 1200 mm └─ 청소 및 유틸리티 접근성 고려 D-9.4 벽면 부착 여부 ├─ IF 전기/유틸리티 공급 = 벽면형 → 장비를 벽면 인접 배치 └─ IF 전기/유틸리티 공급 = 천정형 → 장비를 Room 중앙 배치 허용 D-9.5 대형 장비 특수 처리 └─ IF 장비 = BioReactor 10,000L (또는 H > Room 천정고) → 하단(스커트) = service area로 노출, head space만 Room

부착 여부 ├─ IF 전기/유틸리티 공급 = 벽면형 → 장비를 벽면 인접 배치 └─ IF 전기/유틸리티 공급 = 천정형 → 장비를 Room 중앙 배치 허용 D-9.5 대형 장비 특수 처리 └─ IF 장비 = BioReactor 10,000L (또는 H > Room 천정고) → 하단(스커트) = service area로 노출, head space만 Room 내 노출 → 또는 well type ceiling 적용 ``` --- ## Phase 10. 세척실 / 준비실 (Washing / Preparation) ``` A-10.1 연결 경로 ├─ 세척실 ↔ Return Corridor (출구 측 AL 경유) └─ 준비실 ↔ Supply Corridor A-10.2 Pass-through COP 공유 └─ 세척실과 준비실은 **인접 배치**, Pass-through COP를 공유 D-10.3 세척실 ↔ 준비실 사람 왕래 (HARD CONSTRAINT) └─ 물리적으로 차단 (직접 통행 금지) → 세척실은 Return, 준비실은 Supply 경로 분리 보장 ``` --- ## Phase 11. NC 구역 배치 ``` D-11.1 NC Room 필요성 판정 ├─ IF 별도 층/건물에 배치 계획 있음 │ → 본 Layout에서 생략 가능 │ └─ ELSE → A-11.2 A-11.2 NC Room 배치 원칙 ├─ 별도 영역으로 구분 (공정·보조구역 형태에 방해 ×) └─ 화장실, 사무실, 모니터링/제어실, 로비 ``` --- ## Phase 12. 차압(Differential Pressure) 부여 ``` FOR EACH Room r: A-12.1 차압 표기 단위 └─ Pa, 외부 대기압 = 0 Pa D-12.2 청정등급 간 차압 └─ 인접 Room 등급 다를 시 ≥ 10 ~ 15 Pa 압력차 유지 서열: Grade B > Grade C > Grade D > CNC > NC D-12.3 동일 등급 Room 간 차압 ├─ Default → 0 Pa (차압 미부여 허용) │ └─ 예외: Virus Removal 전후 정제실 → DP(정제실2) ≥ DP(정제실1) + 0.5 Pa A-12.4 급기/배기 균형 설계 └─ Room별 supply/exhaust 풍량 조합으로 목표 차압 형성 ``` --- ## 부록 A. 출입복장 분기 (Phase 3 의 종속 규칙) ``` FOR EACH Room r (사람 진입 시): ├─ IF Grade(r) == B → 무균복 (over gowning) ├─ IF Grade(r) == C → 무진복 (over gowning) └─ IF Grade(r) ∈ {D, CNC} → 스크럽 (degowning and gowning) ``` → 갱의실(Gowning Room)의 위치와 개수 결정 시 위 규칙으로 분기. --- ## 부록 B. Design Agent 구현 시 권장 데이터 흐름 ``` [INPUT] ─→ Phase 1 ─→ Phase 2 ─→ Phase 3 ─→ Phase 4 ──┐ │ (Room 정의 완료) ▼ Phase 5 (배열) ←─────── Phase 6 (AL) ←──── Phase 7 (복도) │ ▼ Phase 8 (도어) → Phase 9 (장비) → Phase 10 (세척/준비) │ ▼ Phase 11 (NC) → Phase 12 (DP) │ ▼ [OUTPUT] 개념설계 평면도 (DXF/SVG) ``` 각 Phase는 **(검증 → 부분 수정 루프)** 가 권장됨. - 예: Phase 4 종료 후 Phase 2의 "주요 공정 Room 40~70%" 제약 재검증 - 예: Phase 6 종료 후 Phase 7의 "Supply↔Return 직접 연결 금지" 재검증 - 예: Phase 12 종료 후 인접 등급 차압 일관성 재검증 --- ## 부록 C. Hard Constraint 요약 (Agent의 종료 검증 체크리스트) | # | 제약 (Hard Constraint) | 근거 | |---|---|---| | C1 | 공급복도와 리턴복도는 직접 연결 금지 | 교차오염 방지 | | C2 | 세척실과 준비실 사람 왕래 물리적 차단 | 교차오염 방지 | | C3 | One-way Room의 입구·출구 분리 (입구 AL ≠ 출구 AL) | Virus 前 공정 Bio-safety | | C4 | Grade B

| |---|---|---| | C1 | 공급복도와 리턴복도는 직접 연결 금지 | 교차오염 방지 | | C2 | 세척실과 준비실 사람 왕래 물리적 차단 | 교차오염 방지 | | C3 | One-way Room의 입구·출구 분리 (입구 AL ≠ 출구 AL) | Virus 前 공정 Bio-safety | | C4 | Grade B Room (one-way) → 전실 4개 필수 | EU GMP Annex 1 | | C5 | 인접 등급 간 차압 ≥ 10~15 Pa | EU GMP Annex 1 | | C6 | 등급 서열 : B > C > D > CNC > NC | EU GMP Annex 1 | | C7 | 복도 폭 ≥ 1500 mm | 인원·자재 동선 | | C8 | 장비 간 간격 ≥ 1000 mm, 장비-벽 ≥ 600 mm | 작업·청소 공간 | | C9 | 주요 공정 Room 합 ∈ [40%, 70%] × TOTAL_AREA | 면적 효율 | | C10 | 도어 열림 방향 = 차압 흐름 방향 | 자연 폐쇄 |
